content.text: Shàun Pàlajé
content.html: Shàun Pàlajé

content.text: Born in the capital, Hāké, on April 19, 1899. He fulfilled the conditions of the old tradition that had endured since the eighteenth century: the “Child of Destiny” who would found an Instructional Nation was to be a child of the Nine Clans born in that year and left-handed from birth.
content.html: Born in the capital, Hāké, on April 19, 1899. He fulfilled the conditions of the old tradition that had endured since the eighteenth century: the “Child of Destiny” who would found an Instructional Nation was to be a child of the Nine Clans born in that year and left-handed from birth.

content.text: Public opinion was violently divided over what should be done with the Child of Destiny. The ruling majority, the Republican Alliance (successor to the Nationalist Alliance), viewed him with particular alarm, fearing that he would become a seedbed of Instructionalism like Nine Earths. Pàlajé was torn away from his blood family and raised inside Kïnàiny—the order of knights directly under the Kàlavatan Republic—where he received an education as a republican that came close to indoctrination.
content.html: Public opinion was violently divided over what should be done with the Child of Destiny. The ruling majority, the Republican Alliance (successor to the Nationalist Alliance), viewed him with particular alarm, fearing that he would become a seedbed of Instructionalism like Nine Earths. Pàlajé was torn away from his blood family and raised inside Kïnàiny—the order of knights directly under the Kàlavatan Republic—where he received an education as a republican that came close to indoctrination.

content.text: The man who took on the role of father was Káin Kàna, a master of Duïlé who would later become the third president of the Kàlavatan Republic. Known for his unconventional nature, he taught Pàlajé the martial arts and learning of western Tinebris, and tried to keep him from becoming wholly bound by the Republic’s restraints. Kàna also raised him to value kindness, and when Pàlajé once let a creature he kept die, Kàna rebuked him severely.
content.html: The man who took on the role of father was Káin Kàna, a master of Duïlé who would later become the third president of the Kàlavatan Republic. Known for his unconventional nature, he taught Pàlajé the martial arts and learning of western Tinebris, and tried to keep him from becoming wholly bound by the Republic’s restraints. Kàna also raised him to value kindness, and when Pàlajé once let a creature he kept die, Kàna rebuked him severely.

content.text: For all Kàna’s nobility of character, Pàlajé secretly envied Ládén Kïnàbé, Kàna’s nephew and Pàlajé’s foster brother. Kïnàbé, unlike Pàlajé, was free—and loved both by his own family and by Kàna.
content.html: For all Kàna’s nobility of character, Pàlajé secretly envied Ládén Kïnàbé, Kàna’s nephew and Pàlajé’s foster brother. Kïnàbé, unlike Pàlajé, was free—and loved both by his own family and by Kàna.

content.text: On April 17, 1915, two days before Pàlajé’s coming-of-age at fifteen, Kàna was assassinated by a member of Nine Earths. Pàlajé mourned bitterly: not only had he lost the father who had raised him, he had also lost the chance to be acknowledged by Kàna as a full-grown man.

Pàlajé soon distinguished himself within Kïnàiny and carried out his duties as Kïnàbé’s capable partner. Even so, he was consumed by frustration at being denied proper recognition because of his birth as the “Child of Destiny.”

In 1918, the Kàlavé Council learned that Nine Earths had appeared in the sacred place Xāváléqï, and decided to send Kïnàbé to deal with it. By then Kïnàbé was already a Council member serving as an acting Instructor. Pàlajé, as usual refused permission to accompany him, left Hāké without the Council’s authorization.
content.html: In 1918, the Kàlavé Council learned that Nine Earths had appeared in the sacred place Xāváléqï, and decided to send Kïnàbé to deal with it. By then Kïnàbé was already a Council member serving as an acting Instructor. Pàlajé, as usual refused permission to accompany him, left Hāké without the Council’s authorization.

content.text: On the road to Xāváléqï, while evading the Council’s pursuer—Kïnàbé himself—Pàlajé was hidden by a girl his own age who called herself Èshe. She came from Làitéqï Village in Zoruā City and had lost both parents at birth. Their shared circumstances drew them quickly together. Kïnàbé caught up with them on the road, but on the condition of “results first,” he left Pàlajé to his own devices.
content.html: On the road to Xāváléqï, while evading the Council’s pursuer—Kïnàbé himself—Pàlajé was hidden by a girl his own age who called herself Èshe. She came from Làitéqï Village in Zoruā City and had lost both parents at birth. Their shared circumstances drew them quickly together. Kïnàbé caught up with them on the road, but on the condition of “results first,” he left Pàlajé to his own devices.

content.text: As they traveled, the three of them formed a bond; and between Pàlajé and Èshe, that bond deepened into love. They also saw, with their own eyes, people groaning under heavy taxation—very different from the prosperous folk along the coast of the Duram Sea.
content.html: As they traveled, the three of them formed a bond; and between Pàlajé and Èshe, that bond deepened into love. They also saw, with their own eyes, people groaning under heavy taxation—very different from the prosperous folk along the coast of the Duram Sea.

content.text: When they reached Làitéqï Village, Èshe’s home, they spent the night at a relative’s house.

Then, in the dead of night, a scream rang out from Èshe’s room, and Pàlajé and Kïnàbé were appalled. Làitéqï Village still preserved the old custom of yobai. Èshe’s relatives were poor, and they had come to resent the adopted child in their care. Meanwhile, a marriage arrangement between Èshe and a local landowner had been moving forward entirely without her knowledge.

Pàlajé struck the man who had crept upon Èshe in her sleep. He was about to hit him again when Kïnàbé stopped him. This was the village’s custom, Kïnàbé said—proof of the present limits of Kàlavé itself. Her relatives blessed it. The landowner blessed it. The whole village of Làitéqï blessed it. Unable to endure such madness, Pàlajé seized Èshe’s hand and said, “Let’s get out of here together.”
But Èshe shook him off. “You are the Child of Destiny. I’m only a poor village girl. And I want to repay the relatives and the village that raised me.”
Pàlajé ran from the village, and Kïnàbé went after him. After that, he never visited Èshe again.

Once they reached Xāváléqï, Pàlajé threw himself into maintaining order like a self-appointed guardian of public morals. Kïnàbé, traveling with him, warned him, “You’re not a policeman.” But there was nothing in Pàlajé’s mind except his own sense of justice—and the thought of saving “her.”

One day, members of Nine Earths launched a sudden attack. Pàlajé and Kïnàbé fought back with Duïlé, but then a man calling himself the “Shadow Owl,” Xàsané, appeared. Kïnàbé was helpless before him. Pàlajé, whose swordsmanship had grown erratic because of “her,” was also defeated with ease. Xàsané left Kïnàbé behind in Xāváléqï and abducted Pàlajé alone.

Taken to Zopāitān City, the stronghold of Nine Earths, Pàlajé was asked by Xàsané what had thrown his blade into disorder. For the sake of “her” honor, Pàlajé kept silent. Only after securing Xàsané’s word that Kïnàbé was alive did he speak, and even then he disclosed only the trauma in vague terms. After hearing him out, Xàsané praised him. “What Kàlavé needs,” he said, “is a ruler with a heart.”
content.html: Taken to Zopāitān City, the stronghold of Nine Earths, Pàlajé was asked by Xàsané what had thrown his blade into disorder. For the sake of “her” honor, Pàlajé kept silent. Only after securing Xàsané’s word that Kïnàbé was alive did he speak, and even then he disclosed only the trauma in vague terms. After hearing him out, Xàsané praised him. “What Kàlavé needs,” he said, “is a ruler with a heart.”

content.text: Xàsané himself had come from the poor of the Xāválavé region, and he deeply sympathized with the world Pàlajé had seen. At the same time, he insisted that the thing oppressing the common people was the very structure of the “Republic,” where merchants and clans fought to preserve their own interests and decision-making ground to a halt. Raised as a republican, Pàlajé was profoundly shaken and rejected Xàsané’s argument.

Even so, Xàsané told him, “I still affirm you.” He then issued a public declaration to the Republic government, demanding that Pàlajé be “freed from the bondage of the Republic” and made to “found an Instructional Nation as the First Instructor.”
content.html: Even so, Xàsané told him, “I still affirm you.” He then issued a public declaration to the Republic government, demanding that Pàlajé be “freed from the bondage of the Republic” and made to “found an Instructional Nation as the First Instructor.”

content.text: The Republic government could not possibly accept such demands. Kïnàiny was dispatched to Zopāitān City, and battle broke out with Nine Earths. Kïnàbé arrived on the scene and fought Xàsané. Xàsané reviled him: “You will never be able to save her. It is the future First Instructor who will bring forth a kinder Kàlavé.” But Kïnàbé answered Pàlajé instead: “The Republic still has a future. As rulers, let’s save the poor.”

Those words stirred Pàlajé. The two of them joined forces in Duïlé and defeated Xàsané. They tried to take him in for questioning about Nine Earths and the “White Owl,” but Xàsané took his own life to seal his lips forever.

Pàlajé and Kïnàbé expected punishment from the Council for disobeying orders. Instead, Hāké was swept up in a mood of celebration for Pàlajé’s return. Newspapers tied to Nine Earths reported not an “escape,” but an “abduction” by ethnic minorities, and hailed his return as “the triumphant homecoming of a national hero.” They also savaged the Republic for allowing the Child of Destiny to be kidnapped, and public opinion tilted toward Pàlajé and against the government.

In 1920, amid the “Pàlajé whirlwind” and the seizure of part of the Duram Sea by the Kingdom of Armona, right-wing forces such as the National Self-Determination Party and the Ismizlark Alliance, both of which called for breaking away from Armona colonialism, formed a coalition government after the elections. Yet fears of Armona’s backlash remained strong, and the true key to that coalition was the Ismizlark Alliance, which enjoyed powerful support from the conservative Dírál bloc.
content.html: In 1920, amid the “Pàlajé whirlwind” and the seizure of part of the Duram Sea by the Kingdom of Armona, right-wing forces such as the National Self-Determination Party and the Ismizlark Alliance">Ismizlark Alliance, both of which called for breaking away from Armona colonialism, formed a coalition government after the elections. Yet fears of Armona’s backlash remained strong, and the true key to that coalition was the Ismizlark Alliance, which enjoyed powerful support from the conservative Dírál bloc.

content.text: Pàlajé, for his part, met the formal qualifications to sit on the Council, being both twenty years old and a member of the Nine Clans. In principle, he should have been appointed. But because he was the “Child of Destiny,” the Republic government refused to let him become a Council member, allowing him to serve only as an acting Instructor of Kïnàiny.

Confronted by such absurdity, Pàlajé began around this time to cultivate a personal friendship with Shàun Pàlé, leader of the Ismizlark Alliance. The reason lay in what had happened to “her.” Still unable to forget that incident, Pàlajé and Kïnàbé used Kïnàbé’s authority as a Council member to urge legal reform so that public morality in Jïrīupēr Province—including Zoruā City—would be maintained in accordance with Dírál precepts. Pàlé, elected from Ládéqï, the largest city in Jïrīupēr Province, was deeply impressed by this. He invited Kïnàbé to dinner, but Kïnàbé, wary of the new administration, declined. Pàlajé attended in his place.
content.html: Confronted by such absurdity, Pàlajé began around this time to cultivate a personal friendship with Shàun Pàlé, leader of the Ismizlark Alliance. The reason lay in what had happened to “her.” Still unable to forget that incident, Pàlajé and Kïnàbé used Kïnàbé’s authority as a Council member to urge legal reform so that public morality in Jïrīupēr Province—including Zoruā City—would be maintained in accordance with Dírál precepts. Pàlé, elected from Ládéqï, the largest city in Jïrīupēr Province, was deeply impressed by this. He invited Kïnàbé to dinner, but Kïnàbé, wary of the new administration, declined. Pàlajé attended in his place.

content.text: Pàlé said, “To choose is a marvelous thing. When the Council acts and chooses as it did this time, Parliament has no choice but to respond. The problem is the present Parliament, which chooses only to preserve the balance among competing powers. The Council—no, the president—ought to possess still greater authority.” Pàlajé felt that argument sink deep into him. At parting, Pàlé embraced him and said, “This is what we do in my homeland. May fortune bless your choice.” Pàlajé would later recall that no one had ever embraced him before. He took Pàlé’s words to heart.
content.html: Pàlé said, “To choose is a marvelous thing. When the Council acts and chooses as it did this time, Parliament has no choice but to respond. The problem is the present Parliament, which chooses only to preserve the balance among competing powers. The Council—no, the president—ought to possess still greater authority.” Pàlajé felt that argument sink deep into him. At parting, Pàlé embraced him and said, “This is what we do in my homeland. May fortune bless your choice.” Pàlajé would later recall that no one had ever embraced him before. He took Pàlé’s words to heart.

content.text: In 1923, the clash between the two great powers, Armona and Niqeltoliĕnē, erupted into the world war known as the Long Night War. The Republic bordered Jaxadarn, a protectorate of Niqeltoliĕnē, and many “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities lived along that frontier. Backed by Niqeltoliĕnē, those forces rose in revolt and launched an invasion aimed at retaking the sacred place Xāváléqï.
content.html: In 1923, the clash between the two great powers, Armona and Niqeltoliĕnē, erupted into the world war known as the Long Night War. The Republic bordered Jaxadarn, a protectorate of Niqeltoliĕnē, and many “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities lived along that frontier. Backed by Niqeltoliĕnē, those forces rose in revolt and launched an invasion aimed at retaking the sacred place Xāváléqï.

content.text: The Council, however, believed that the “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities had risen in the first place because Nine Earths had oppressed them unjustly.

The Republic army was poorly trained and badly suited to the rebels’ guerrilla tactics. Armona sold it weapons, but offered no direct support; indeed, it feared rebellion inside the Republic itself. As a result, the Republic struggled under mounting deficits and a worsening military disadvantage.

Pàlajé argued before the Council that Kïnàiny should be mobilized to defend the sacred place from the approaching rebel forces, which posed an immediate threat. But because he was not a Council member, his view was dismissed out of hand. “The Council are loyal servants of the Republic,” they said, “not expendable pawns.”

Pàlajé vented his frustrations to his secretary, Lei. Lei adored Pàlajé as the Child of Destiny. Pàlajé, by contrast, felt no kinship with her. Unlike “her,” Lei possessed both family and wealth, and he treated her, at best, as a convenient woman.

In 1924, the Ismizlark Alliance, campaigning above all on the defense of the sacred place, won an outright majority in Parliament and came to wield great influence in the Conference as well. In the same year, Pàlé was elected president.

Alarmed by how conveniently public opinion was moving in favor of both Nine Earths and the Ismizlark Alliance, the Council asked Pàlajé to use his closeness to Pàlé and serve as a spy regarding secret ties between the ruling party and Nine Earths. Pàlajé, growing distrustful of a Council that only meant to use him, resolved to ask Pàlé directly and prove his innocence.
Invited to the presidential residence, Pàlajé asked him bluntly, “Surely you have nothing to do with Nine Earths—do you?” Pàlé answered, “I am the White Owl.”

For a moment Pàlajé was struck speechless. Then, in a surge of betrayed anger, he lashed out with a Duïlé knife—only for Lei, who should have been waiting outside the room with Kïnàiny in case of an emergency, to stop him and disarm him. Lei too was a member of Nine Earths, a spy placed there by Pàlé. More than that, she was a seasoned swordswoman capable of dealing with the Kïnàiny men outside by herself.

Pàlé said to him, “You know as well as I do that the Dírál faction brought order to Kàlavé. Republicanism cannot complete Instruct through Dírál. The founding of an Instructional Nation is our common dream.” Then he added, “If you wish to kill me, do so. May fortune bless your choice,” and had Lei place the knife back in Pàlajé’s hand.
content.html: Pàlé said to him, “You know as well as I do that the Dírál faction brought order to Kàlavé. Republicanism cannot complete Instruct through Dírál. The founding of an Instructional Nation is our common dream.” Then he added, “If you wish to kill me, do so. May fortune bless your choice,” and had Lei place the knife back in Pàlajé’s hand.

content.text: Pàlajé sheathed the knife and knelt before Pàlé. Pàlé said that the fall of Bïlàitān City, the capital of Káshatìléqï Province, was only a matter of time. When that happened, emergency powers would be granted to the president; and if, in that state, Pàlajé were to assume both the presidency and the office of First Instructor at once, the Republic could be reorganized into an Instructional Nation. Only Pàlajé—popular with the people and born of the Nine Clans—could carry out such a plan.
content.html: Pàlajé sheathed the knife and knelt before Pàlé. Pàlé said that the fall of Bïlàitān City, the capital of Káshatìléqï Province, was only a matter of time. When that happened, emergency powers would be granted to the president; and if, in that state, Pàlajé were to assume both the presidency and the office of First Instructor at once, the Republic could be reorganized into an Instructional Nation. Only Pàlajé—popular with the people and born of the Nine Clans—could carry out such a plan.

content.text: In 1925, with the fall of Bïlàitān City, Parliament passed a measure granting the president emergency powers. Pàlé promised that “I will relinquish those powers promptly once the war is over.” He also recognized the members of Nine Earths, who were voluntarily assisting the war effort, as volunteers, thereby checking the movements of the Council.
Through Nine Earths, he also had newspapers trumpet the rebirth of a “strong Kàlavé” and harness popular support for the founding of an Instructional Nation.
In 1927, the Council at last identified Pàlé as the White Owl. In truth, it was Pàlajé himself who had deliberately leaked the information: the time had finally come to set the plan in motion.

Kïnàiny entered the presidential residence to question Pàlé, only to find itself drawn into battle with him, a master of Duïlé. Pàlé annihilated the Kïnàiny men single-handedly, severing the Council’s limbs. Then, exactly as planned, he took his own life and left the Council to bear the stain of having assassinated the president.

Arrangements had already been made for the news of Pàlé’s death to be conveyed to Parliament through Nine Earths. Parliament appointed Pàlajé interim president and passed a resolution dissolving the Council.

To receive the Conference’s formal ratification—and to assume the office of First Instructor, for which he had long been destined—Pàlajé crossed the bridge linking the Parliament tower and the Conference tower of the Misenitïu Great Hall.

Waiting for him on that bridge was his closest friend, Ládén Kïnàbé, together with his companions. Intelligence agents inside the presidential residence had already informed Kïnàbé of the truth behind Pàlajé’s death and of the fact that, with Pàlé’s death, Pàlajé was to become the new White Owl. Kïnàbé intended to expose, in Parliament, Pàlajé’s covert role as White Owl in steering both Parliament and public opinion toward Instructionalism. The Ismizlark Alliance may have held the majority, and it may have desired the birth of an Instructional Nation—but even they would not have welcomed learning that they themselves had been manipulated by Nine Earths.

Pàlajé fought for Instructionalism; Kïnàbé for republicanism. They collided on the bridge. Each commanded roughly a company’s worth of men, and the struggle was evenly matched. Above all, both Pàlajé and Kïnàbé had by then reached the level of true masters with the sword, and the duel between them was fierce beyond words.

Kïnàbé asked him, “What would ‘she’ think if she saw this?” Pàlajé shouted back, “She’s gone!” Kïnàbé replied, “I went to see her—Èshe. She was living happily in Làitéqï Village. Look to the future, Pàlajé!” The news of Èshe’s safety shook Pàlajé so violently that he took a devastating blow to his right arm.
Writhing in pain, Pàlajé seemed at first to lose the will to fight. Then Lei appeared behind him, embraced him gently, and whispered, “May fortune bless your choice.”

Pàlajé rose again and committed Nine Earths to the fight, forcing Kïnàbé’s men onto the defensive. Having regained his composure, he told Kïnàbé, “It was the Republic—the Council—that killed Pàlé.” Kïnàbé demanded, “Kàna was our father. We are brothers.” Pàlajé rejected him. Leaving only the words “In the future, too,” Kïnàbé fell from the bridge.
Deprived of its commander, Kïnàbé’s force surrendered. Dragging his useless right arm behind him, Pàlajé entered the Conference tower.
There, too, he was confirmed as interim president and selected as First Instructor.
“I will not waver again. I choose a strong Kàlavé. Here and now, I declare the dissolution of the Kàlavatan Republic and the founding of the Kàlavé Instructional Nation. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”
content.html: “I will not waver again. I choose a strong Kàlavé. Here and now, I declare the dissolution of the Kàlavatan Republic and the founding of the Kàlavé Instructional Nation. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”

content.text: In 1927, Pàlajé set himself, step by step, to dismantling the surviving structures of the Republic: Parliament was dissolved, political parties were abolished, and warrants were issued for the remnants of the Council.
content.html: In 1927, Pàlajé set himself, step by step, to dismantling the surviving structures of the Republic: Parliament was dissolved, political parties were abolished, and warrants were issued for the remnants of the Council.

content.text: The remnants of the Council turned for aid to the royal army of the Kingdom of Armona, the suzerain power. But Pàlajé had already moved first. He had concluded a secret accord with Armona: so long as Kàlavé continued to fight in the Long Night War on the side of the Blood-Sea Alliance, Armona would not interfere in the upheaval within Kàlavé.
content.html: The remnants of the Council turned for aid to the royal army of the Kingdom of Armona, the suzerain power. But Pàlajé had already moved first. He had concluded a secret accord with Armona: so long as Kàlavé continued to fight in the Long Night War on the side of the Blood-Sea Alliance, Armona would not interfere in the upheaval within Kàlavé.

content.text: Pàlajé withdrew every man he had dispatched to the friendly nation of Skniune and concentrated them in the Xāváléqï Corridor, where he bent all his strength to the defense of the sacred place. Those who returned from Skniune had been schooled there in anti-guerrilla warfare, and that made it possible at last to take the offensive against the Alignment forces—the “orthodox” believers and ethnic-minority troops supported by Niqeltoliĕnē.
content.html: Pàlajé withdrew every man he had dispatched to the friendly nation of Skniune and concentrated them in the Xāváléqï Corridor">Xāváléqï Corridor, where he bent all his strength to the defense of the sacred place. Those who returned from Skniune had been schooled there in anti-guerrilla warfare, and that made it possible at last to take the offensive against the Alignment forces—the “orthodox” believers and ethnic-minority troops supported by Niqeltoliĕnē.

content.text: In 1928, Armona and Niqeltoliĕnē began preparing for peace talks. Pàlajé assumed that the talks would go smoothly enough. After all, the war in the Xāváléqï Corridor had begun as a backlash against Nine Earths’ oppression of the “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities; if he could offer up a scapegoat in place of the true culprits and save Niqeltoliĕnē’s pride, a settlement ought not to be difficult.
content.html: In 1928, Armona and Niqeltoliĕnē began preparing for peace talks. Pàlajé assumed that the talks would go smoothly enough. After all, the war in the Xāváléqï Corridor had begun as a backlash against Nine Earths’ oppression of the “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities; if he could offer up a scapegoat in place of the true culprits and save Niqeltoliĕnē’s pride, a settlement ought not to be difficult.

content.text: But the remnants of the Council laid bare the covert deeds of Nine Earths before the Alignment forces, and riots broke out among the “orthodox” believers and the ethnic minorities. Then, in 1930, the peace talks between Armona and Niqeltoliĕnē collapsed altogether. Pàlajé therefore threw the full weight of the state into annihilating both the remnants of the Council and the Alignment forces that sheltered them.

That same year, word reached him that a boy calling himself “Kïnàbé” had been stirring up revolt among the Alignment forces and winning a name for himself as a commander. Fearing that the name of Kïnàbé, as a symbol of the old Republic, would serve to inflame their morale, Pàlajé ordered Nine Earths to assassinate him. Yet he already suspected the truth: the real Kïnàbé—the one he himself had cast down from the Bridge of Conversion—ought long since to have been dead.
content.html: That same year, word reached him that a boy calling himself “Kïnàbé” had been stirring up revolt among the Alignment forces and winning a name for himself as a commander. Fearing that the name of Kïnàbé, as a symbol of the old Republic, would serve to inflame their morale, Pàlajé ordered Nine Earths to assassinate him. Yet he already suspected the truth: the real Kïnàbé—the one he himself had cast down from the Bridge of Conversion—ought long since to have been dead.

content.text: In 1932, an elite detachment of Nine Earths appeared at the Gàtïna Grand Auditorium in Hāké, the official residence of the First Instructor, with this “Kïnàbé” in tow. The boy’s true name was Lakán Lài, an ordinary commoner from Bïlàitān City. Pàlajé let out a breath that might have been disappointment or relief, then asked the members of Nine Earths why they had not killed the boy at once, but had bothered to bring him all the way here.
content.html: In 1932, an elite detachment of Nine Earths appeared at the Gàtïna Grand Auditorium in Hāké, the official residence of the First Instructor, with this “Kïnàbé” in tow. The boy’s true name was Lakán Lài, an ordinary commoner from Bïlàitān City. Pàlajé let out a breath that might have been disappointment or relief, then asked the members of Nine Earths why they had not killed the boy at once, but had bothered to bring him all the way here.

content.text: At that very instant, the cords binding Lài slipped free, and his blade flashed toward Pàlajé. Pàlajé and his guards drew at once, confronting Lài and the assassins who had come in the guise of Nine Earths men. Astonishingly, the form of Lài’s Duïlé was that of that Kïnàbé. “Where did you steal that?” Pàlajé demanded. “From our father,” Lài answered. At the sight of this “Kïnàbé,” who even knew of Kàna, Pàlajé struck back in anger. “I have no father—still less any brother.” Yet for all his imitation of that Kïnàbé, the boy’s sword was full of openings, and Pàlajé stripped him of his weapon in moments.
Then another man stepped before them, dressed in the uniform and mask of Nine Earths.
“I am here.”

It was Ládén Kïnàbé himself—the very man who was supposed to have plunged to his death from the Bridge of Conversion. The sword he drew from his breast was certainly diminished by the aftereffects of that fall, yet it still held enough strength to spirit Lài away from this place. Pàlajé launched a pitiless blow, saying, “It was settled long ago.” Kïnàbé turned it aside and answered, “You still do not know that,” never once pressing the attack himself. Calling Kïnàbé’s name through tears, Lài fled. “I’ll be waiting in the future,” Kïnàbé told him, and then he perished. Pàlajé was crushed by the bitter thought that, in a certain sense, he had allowed Kïnàbé to go on living.
content.html: It was Ládén Kïnàbé himself—the very man who was supposed to have plunged to his death from the Bridge of Conversion. The sword he drew from his breast was certainly diminished by the aftereffects of that fall, yet it still held enough strength to spirit Lài away from this place. Pàlajé launched a pitiless blow, saying, “It was settled long ago.” Kïnàbé turned it aside and answered, “You still do not know that,” never once pressing the attack himself. Calling Kïnàbé’s name through tears, Lài fled. “I’ll be waiting in the future,” Kïnàbé told him, and then he perished. Pàlajé was crushed by the bitter thought that, in a certain sense, he had allowed Kïnàbé to go on living.

content.text: In 1936, as the mood of peace spread across the world, Kàlavé concluded a settlement with Jaxadarn, territory of Niqeltoliĕnē. It recognized the protection of autonomy for the “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities in the Xāváléqï Corridor, and in return Jaxadarn acknowledged the Instructional Nation as the only state in Kàlavé. The remnants of the old Republic were driven into utter isolation.
content.html: In 1936, as the mood of peace spread across the world, Kàlavé concluded a settlement with Jaxadarn, territory of Niqeltoliĕnē. It recognized the protection of autonomy for the “orthodox” believers and ethnic minorities in the Xāváléqï Corridor, and in return Jaxadarn acknowledged the Instructional Nation as the only state in Kàlavé. The remnants of the old Republic were driven into utter isolation.

content.text: On August 9, in the Hlïyā Great Temple at Xāváléqï, now visited again by peace, the ceremony of tail succession for the Second Instructor was held. The Hlïyā Great Temple lay at the deepest level of the Mavwa Grand Auditorium, and was counted “the place nearest to God,” a place into which not even an Instructor might enter save at the moment of tail succession.
content.html: On August 9, in the Hlïyā Great Temple at Xāváléqï, now visited again by peace, the ceremony of tail succession for the Second Instructor was held. The Hlïyā Great Temple lay at the deepest level of the Mavwa Grand Auditorium, and was counted “the place nearest to God,” a place into which not even an Instructor might enter save at the moment of tail succession.

content.text: Into that forbidden ground Lài slipped, for he meant to cross blades with Pàlajé once more.
“You understand the impiety of this?” Pàlajé asked.
“Being unconventional runs in the family,” Lài answered.

That answer, which called Kàna to mind too vividly, stung Pàlajé to irritation, and he drew without delay. Lài’s sword was complete now: the perfect Stance of the Moderate, wherein Pàlajé’s Stance of Rigid Integrity and Kïnàbé’s Stance of Gentle Harmony stood together as one. Little by little, Lài’s skill drove the one-armed Pàlajé back.
At last Lài succeeded in disarming him.
“If you want to kill me, then do it. May fortune bless your choice.”
Pàlajé uttered the same cursed words that Pàlé had once spoken to him. But Lài replied, “I only wanted to talk to you.”
“I think nothing is ever decided by talk,” Pàlajé confessed.
“It is,” Lài said. “The future is waiting.”

Then Lei appeared with members of Nine Earths, and with their son Àkané. Lei declared, “Here dies His Highness the First Instructor who knew no hesitation. From this moment on, ‘our child’ shall inherit the office of First Instructor.” Their son, who was not yet fifteen but soon would be, was to be given the seat of the First Instructor: a coup, plainly and without disguise. Yet this was the forbidden ground. What was decided here would stand as nothing less than “the will of Hlï-Yíl-Tàu.”
Already battered nearly beyond endurance, Pàlajé lifted his sword one last time, if only to let his “brother” escape. Lài, grieving that once again all he could do was flee, nevertheless ran.
“I’ll be waiting in the future.”
Bïláin Àkané
content.html: Bïláin Àkané

content.text: He was born on August 9, 1921, the son of Shàun Pàlajé—who would later become the First Instructor—and his secretary Lei. There was an old belief that twins born on the Day of the Three-Tailed Fox were ill-omened. One of the pair, Shàun Àya, was therefore raised by the Shàun house, while Àkané was adopted into the Bïláin house. All of it would later prove to be one more piece in the design by which Lei meant to bring Pàlajé’s destiny to completion; but Àkané himself was reared without ever being told the truth of his birth.
content.html: He was born on August 9, 1921, the son of Shàun Pàlajé—who would later become the First Instructor—and his secretary Lei. There was an old belief that twins born on the Day of the Three-Tailed Fox were ill-omened. One of the pair, Shàun Àya, was therefore raised by the Shàun house, while Àkané was adopted into the Bïláin house. All of it would later prove to be one more piece in the design by which Lei meant to bring Pàlajé’s destiny to completion; but Àkané himself was reared without ever being told the truth of his birth.

content.text: He inherited his real father Pàlajé’s immense strength, and as a child he was known by the nickname Wāman—Monster. He excelled at Duïlé and went on winning match after match with a style that trusted power more than finesse. Though somewhat impulsive, he had a kind heart. When his younger stepsister Nèi was bullied by children of the Nine Clans, he leapt to her defense without hesitation. For that reason, a rumor spread among the Nine Clans: if he were ever to become First Instructor, he would surely be a tyrant. Àkané himself, however, never imagined such a future.
content.html: He inherited his real father Pàlajé’s immense strength, and as a child he was known by the nickname Wāman—Monster. He excelled at Duïlé and went on winning match after match with a style that trusted power more than finesse. Though somewhat impulsive, he had a kind heart. When his younger stepsister Nèi was bullied by children of the Nine Clans, he leapt to her defense without hesitation. For that reason, a rumor spread among the Nine Clans: if he were ever to become First Instructor, he would surely be a tyrant. Àkané himself, however, never imagined such a future.

content.text: On August 9, 1936, his fifteenth birthday, tragedy struck.

Àkané had accompanied the Instructors on their progress to Xāváléqï. At three in the afternoon, as he waited in the lodging house of Tïnagiá Parish for the banquet prepared to celebrate his coming of age, he received a dreadful warning from Nèi, who staggered to him grievously wounded.
content.html: Àkané had accompanied the Instructors on their progress to Xāváléqï. At three in the afternoon, as he waited in the lodging house of Tïnagiá Parish for the banquet prepared to celebrate his coming of age, he received a dreadful warning from Nèi, who staggered to him grievously wounded.

content.text: “Brother—run! Nine Earths is after you!”
content.html: “Brother—run! Nine Earths is after you!”

content.text: From the balcony, it took only an instant to grasp what had happened. The tail investiture of the Second Instructor had been carried out as scheduled; and, in sudden addition to it, the tail investiture of Bïláin Àkané by the First Instructor Shàun Pàlajé had been proclaimed. Impossible. He himself was not in the Hlïyā Great Temple.
Coming back to himself, Àkané reached for his sword to avenge Nèi. But his maid seized him at once and dragged him out of the lodging house—past the bodies of his stepsister and his parents.
“‘Destiny’ has been fulfilled.”

Shàun Àya, the other twin, had been born frail because nourishment in the womb had been drawn toward Àkané. In compensation, he possessed a brilliant mind and a gentle nature, and loved the keeping of living things. Lei ignored all of that. She raised him under a severe discipline meant to shape him into the future First Instructor. Medicine obtained by Nine Earths restored Lei’s body to something like ordinary strength, but the cost of it was severe.

Àya’s only salvation was his father, Pàlajé. Having himself been raised under the Republic’s indoctrination, Pàlajé passed on to Àya the swordsmanship and knowledge of every land and age. Yet there was one point on which his teaching differed from Kàna’s: Pàlajé believed that kindness without power was meaningless. Pursuing his father’s back, Àya built a career as the “Shadow Owl” of Nine Earths, determined one day to surpass even his father, the Child of Destiny.
content.html: Àya’s only salvation was his father, Pàlajé. Having himself been raised under the Republic’s indoctrination, Pàlajé passed on to Àya the swordsmanship and knowledge of every land and age. Yet there was one point on which his teaching differed from Kàna’s: Pàlajé believed that kindness without power was meaningless. Pursuing his father’s back, Àya built a career as the “Shadow Owl” of Nine Earths, determined one day to surpass even his father, the Child of Destiny.

content.text: On August 9, 1936, his fifteenth birthday, Lei brought him to the Hlïyā Great Temple. He had been told nothing. Yet when he saw his father defeated by a republican man and shrinking from “choice,” he understood what he himself had to do.
“Here dies His Highness the First Instructor who knew no hesitation. From this day onward, I will choose everything.”
Pàlajé summoned the last of his strength and swung his sword, but Àya parried lightly and dealt him a merciless blow.
“Can you kill your own father?” Pàlajé asked.
In that instant Pàlajé saw through the disguise: the man before him, calling himself “Bïláin Àkané,” was in truth his own son Àya. Àya faltered for the briefest moment, then mastered himself again.
“Of course,” he said. “If it is for this country.”
He raised the finishing stroke—only for his whole body to lock up, a side effect of the drug. Lei at once drove an injection into him and forced the sword back into his trembling hand.
“That is why I despise sheltered women,” Pàlajé spat at Lei.
But Lei answered, “I shall love you forever, Child of Destiny.”
Writhing in pain, Àya brought the last blow down.
“Splendid.”
With that, Pàlajé breathed his last.

The Xāválézï Grand Auditorium was the place where an Instructor, having inherited the tail at the Hlïyā Great Temple, gave his first address. On such an occasion a great audience gathered, eager to judge the new Instructor’s character and measure.
content.html: The Xāválézï Grand Auditorium was the place where an Instructor, having inherited the tail at the Hlïyā Great Temple, gave his first address. On such an occasion a great audience gathered, eager to judge the new Instructor’s character and measure.

content.text: “A country in which the sincere and the devout do not lose out: inheriting the ideal of the former First Instructor, who as of this day has resolved to walk the Heavenly Way, we must bring to Kàlavé the strength without which the good cannot be protected. The kinder we would be, the stronger we must become.”
The commemorative address delivered at the tail investiture of the false Àkané was met with thunderous applause. Then a report reached him and Lei from a member of Nine Earths.
“The sacrifice has escaped.”

Having fled Sàyā Parish, Lài entered Tïnagiá Parish, thick with pilgrims. At first the town seemed watched over on all sides by members of Nine Earths, yet in truth they were hunting someone else. By chance, in a narrow alley between lodging houses, Lài came face to face with that very person: Àkané.

The moment he saw a man who looked exactly like the mysterious figure—Àya—whom he had encountered in the Hlïyā Great Temple, Lài drew his sword. Flustered, Àkané tried to clear away the misunderstanding.
“The man you have in mind and I are probably different people. Call me… Kau.”

He had chosen the sort of false name any man in Kàlavé might use, and only made Lài more suspicious. Then members of Nine Earths appeared, and Àkané met them in combat. Watching him fight, Lài realized that this Àkané was not the same man as the weak-bladed Àya of the temple. His style was wholly different—raw force instead of that frail, uncertain sword.
“I know a little about you,” Àkané said. “You’re a wanted man among the remnants of the Republic, aren’t you? I’m hunted too. Our interests align.”
Lài accepted the offer, though with reluctance.

Lài’s skill and Àkané’s brute strength came together, and together they cut down the members of Nine Earths. But this was the enemy’s stronghold. Little by little they were overwhelmed by sheer numbers and driven onto the defensive.
Then a group appeared calling itself the Nine Earths Annex, Zopāitān Classroom, First Unit. Àkané’s maid had been taken prisoner by them.

The masked man who called on them to surrender swept up both Lài and Àkané, though they still resisted. The two swordsmen were exhausted, certainly, but even so, the skill of the man who subdued them both at once was extraordinary.
“Don’t worry. Do what you do best—pretend you’ve been caught. I’ll see you taken somewhere safe.”
The First Unit packed the pair into a car and drove toward Zopāitān Parish—the Zopāitān City of the republican age—where, they said, their base lay.
content.html: The First Unit packed the pair into a car and drove toward Zopāitān Parish—the Zopāitān City of the republican age—where, they said, their base lay.

content.text: On the road, the man removed his mask and began to speak.

“We are the Xàsané faction of Nine Earths. We studied under the late Shadow Owl, Aharen Xàsané, and now we mean to bring the hammer of justice down upon the Lei faction, which seized control of Nine Earths.”
“I’ll be blunt. We of the Xàsané faction and the remnants of the old Republic intend to suspend hostilities for the moment and join hands against our common enemy, the Lei faction.”
When they reached the Xàsané faction’s mountain base in Zopāitān Parish, they found even members of the former Republican Council gathered there.
content.html: When they reached the Xàsané faction’s mountain base in Zopāitān Parish, they found even members of the former Republican Council gathered there.

content.text: In 1936, with the end of the Long Night War, movements for colonial independence were erupting all over the world. The Instructional Nation was no exception, and tension was mounting steadily between it and the garrison troops of the Kingdom of Armona, its suzerain power. Armona controlled the oil interests of the Duram Sea and had no intention of letting Kàlavé go.
content.html: In 1936, with the end of the Long Night War, movements for colonial independence were erupting all over the world. The Instructional Nation was no exception, and tension was mounting steadily between it and the garrison troops of the Kingdom of Armona, its suzerain power. Armona controlled the oil interests of the Duram Sea and had no intention of letting Kàlavé go.

content.text: The countries surrounding Kàlavé made the matter still more complex. To the south lay Dool, itself an Armona protectorate, with a population well above two hundred million. During the war it had served as one of the sources of Armona’s manpower. Now anti-Armona agitation was spreading there as well. If Dool were lost, the Armona colonial empire would begin to break apart. The independence of Kàlavé might easily hasten the independence of Dool. More than that, Dool held a great many believers of the Dírál faction, and that in turn might inflame expansionist ambitions in the Instructional Nation toward an invasion of Dool.
content.html: The countries surrounding Kàlavé made the matter still more complex. To the south lay Dool, itself an Armona protectorate, with a population well above two hundred million. During the war it had served as one of the sources of Armona’s manpower. Now anti-Armona agitation was spreading there as well. If Dool were lost, the Armona colonial empire would begin to break apart. The independence of Kàlavé might easily hasten the independence of Dool. More than that, Dool held a great many believers of the Dírál faction, and that in turn might inflame expansionist ambitions in the Instructional Nation toward an invasion of Dool.

content.text: For that reason, the “Rebel Alliance,” formed by the remnants of the old Republic and the Xàsané faction of Nine Earths, proposed to move first: win Armona to their side and drive a wedge into the tyranny of the Lei faction. The Xàsané faction opposed that plan, insisting that there was no need to serve the western great powers of Tinebris, who had tormented Kàlavé for so long. It was Lài who brought the argument to a close.
“Even if it takes time, we can still talk.”

Once both sides had confirmed that they agreed on the essential point—that political initiative must be wrested back from the Lei faction—they laid plans. In one week they would travel to the Direct Diocese of Sèlàn and seek an audience with the bishop there. The Rebel Alliance itself could not speak directly with the Armona ambassador; there was nothing for it but to rely on the bishop of Sèlàn, who was known to be on poor terms with the central government of Kàlavé.
Thereafter Lài and Àkané received instruction in swordsmanship from the masked man of the Xàsané faction, who named himself Yïmádén Rià.
content.html: Thereafter Lài and Àkané received instruction in swordsmanship from the masked man of the Xàsané faction, who named himself Yïmádén Rià.

content.text: Àkané mastered the Stance of Savage Fury, a form that made use of his impulsive nature to bewilder an opponent with movements impossible to predict. Lài, for his part, learned the secret art Kïnàbé had used in his final years: the Stance of the Void Mind, which emptied the heart and let the enemy’s blows pass by.

On August 16, 1936, the Rebel Alliance departed Zopāitān Parish and crossed the Xāváléqï Corridor. They followed the road once taken by Xàsané in his youth, and by Pàlajé, Kïnàbé, and Èshe when they had traveled it at eighteen. Looking upon the same poor villages that those three had seen, Àkané felt his heart ache; Lài steeled his resolve, thinking, This is why we will change it; and Rià offered prayers for the now-dead Xàsané and Pàlajé. Bearing the thoughts of all three, the convoy entered the Kïnónó Desert.
content.html: On August 16, 1936, the Rebel Alliance departed Zopāitān Parish and crossed the Xāváléqï Corridor. They followed the road once taken by Xàsané in his youth, and by Pàlajé, Kïnàbé, and Èshe when they had traveled it at eighteen. Looking upon the same poor villages that those three had seen, Àkané felt his heart ache; Lài steeled his resolve, thinking, This is why we will change it; and Rià offered prayers for the now-dead Xàsané and Pàlajé. Bearing the thoughts of all three, the convoy entered the Kïnónó Desert.

content.text: Without warning, the Rebel Alliance came under gunfire from members of the Lei faction. Their own fighters counterattacked at once, and in the dust of the desert the struggle collapsed into close combat.

Àkané, burning with rage against the faction that had stolen Nèi and his parents from him, swung his heavy sword in great arcs. Lài, by contrast, listened for the voice of the wind and cut efficiently through the enemy ranks. Their blades were not graceful in combination; they were rough, asymmetrical, almost ungainly. Yet since the battle at Xāváléqï, both had grown beyond doubt.

When most of the enemy had been dealt with, Rià ordered the Rebel Alliance fighters back into the convoy and, with only a handful of comrades, moved to block the Lei faction reinforcements rising on the horizon. He easily hoisted up Àkané and Lài, both of whom meant to remain with him, and flung them into the bed of a truck.
“I’ll give you a passing mark. Leave the rear guard to me.”

The Rebel Alliance reached the Èmerán Grand Auditorium in Setain Parish of the Direct Diocese of Sèlàn, and used Àkané’s membership in the Bïláin family to secure an audience with the bishop. Naturally, however, Àkané, who was the very image of the First Instructor, and Lài, a wanted man, could not show their faces openly. Yet Sāna, the bishop directly over Sèlàn, already knew they would come. Sèlàn, it seemed, was Rià’s birthplace, and Sāna had known him since childhood.
content.html: The Rebel Alliance reached the Èmerán Grand Auditorium in Setain Parish of the Direct Diocese of Sèlàn, and used Àkané’s membership in the Bïláin family to secure an audience with the bishop. Naturally, however, Àkané, who was the very image of the First Instructor, and Lài, a wanted man, could not show their faces openly. Yet Sāna, the bishop directly over Sèlàn, already knew they would come. Sèlàn, it seemed, was Rià’s birthplace, and Sāna had known him since childhood.

content.text: Àkané came straight to the point. “Armona must not be allowed to recognize the independence of Kàlavé.”

At those words the men of the Xàsané faction frowned, but Sāna calmed them. The conservatives of Hāké and even the liberals of Sèlàn alike had long hoped that Kàlavé would one day come of age. Now that day had finally arrived. When Àkané demanded to know what, then, should be done, Sāna laid out an alternative.
content.html: At those words the men of the Xàsané faction frowned, but Sāna calmed them. The conservatives of Hāké and even the liberals of Sèlàn alike had long hoped that Kàlavé would one day come of age. Now that day had finally arrived. When Àkané demanded to know what, then, should be done, Sāna laid out an alternative.

content.text: In Líunïshèven Parish, which belonged to the Southern Coastal Archdiocese that included Sèlàn, there stood an Armona military base. It was of strategic importance to both Armona and Kàlavé alike, for to the north of Kàlavé lay Durguatan, a socialist state. For Armona, stationing troops in Kàlavé, the front line of anti-communism, was highly desirable; for Kàlavé, too, it mattered from the standpoint of collective security. Above all, there was no knowing when Varalia, the leader of the socialist camp, might deploy nuclear weapons to Durguatan. However much Kàlavé might expand its own forces, there was no escaping Armona’s “nuclear umbrella.”
content.html: In Líunïshèven Parish, which belonged to the Southern Coastal Archdiocese that included Sèlàn, there stood an Armona military base. It was of strategic importance to both Armona and Kàlavé alike, for to the north of Kàlavé lay Durguatan, a socialist state. For Armona, stationing troops in Kàlavé, the front line of anti-communism, was highly desirable; for Kàlavé, too, it mattered from the standpoint of collective security. Above all, there was no knowing when Varalia, the leader of the socialist camp, might deploy nuclear weapons to Durguatan. However much Kàlavé might expand its own forces, there was no escaping Armona’s “nuclear umbrella.”

content.text: Thus the bishops of the Southern Coastal Archdiocese were, in the main, voices for alliance with Armona. To the Lei faction, which wished to push forward with expansionism, the Armona army served as an irksome check. It therefore had every reason to silence the bishops by whatever means were necessary.

For the moment, then, Sāna and the Rebel Alliance set out for the Armona embassy in Yìlà Direct Diocese of the Northern Coastal Archdiocese. Yìlà was a port city under the gaze of the wider world. Even the Lei faction could not act there too openly.
content.html: For the moment, then, Sāna and the Rebel Alliance set out for the Armona embassy in Yìlà Direct Diocese of the Northern Coastal Archdiocese. Yìlà was a port city under the gaze of the wider world. Even the Lei faction could not act there too openly.

content.text: When they reached Narátā Parish in Yìlà, they found an unexpected complication: inspections were being carried out with unusual severity. Fortunately, the convoy of the bishop of Sèlàn was allowed through with ease, but the travelers heard the worst possible words.
“Please cooperate with inspection procedures in preparation for the progress of His Highness the First Instructor to Yìlà.”

At the Armona embassy, the party was admitted under the bishop’s name and led to the Second Guest Chamber. They were told that a prior guest was already in conference with the ambassador. There was, in the whole of Kàlavé, only one man who could keep a bishop waiting under such circumstances. Convinced that he knew who it must be, Àkané slipped out before the others could stop him. Lài went after him and, catching him, warned, “We only confirm. We do not make a scene.” The two quietly knocked out a pair of guards and took their disguises. At the door to the First Guest Chamber stood yet another guard. He asked them in Alardian what business they had there. Lài hesitated; Àkané answered at once.
“The bishop of Sèlàn requests that we confirm whether he may offer his greetings to the ambassador and to His Highness the First Instructor.”
With a certain pride, Àkané remarked that this was the difference between a man of the Nine Clans and an ordinary commoner. Lài’s expression darkened a little.
“Let us put an end to this farce.”
A woman in the uniform of Nine Earths had called out to them.
Both men spun at once with swords drawn. It was Lei. For Lài, she was the very figure he had seen one week earlier in the Hlïyā Great Temple.
“You’re the one who took Nèi, Mother, and Father from me!”
Now Àkané understood, and he sprang forward with a furious swing. Lei turned it aside with ease; her knife skimmed across Àkané’s face.
“Mother, you say? I am alive.”
Àkané took it for mockery and frowned hard.
“No one like you could ever be a mother.”
“You saw the ‘Àkané’ who is your very double, didn’t you? He is my son. You are the insurance.”
Lei gave a signal, and one of her subordinates brought forth Àkané’s maid.
“What she says is true,” the maid said. “On that day, your parents meant to reveal everything at the banquet.”
She went on speaking while Àkané stood stunned and motionless.
“Even so, we always thought of you as one of the Bïláin family.”
There were tears in the maid’s eyes. Seeing that there was no falsehood there, Àkané let his sword fall from his hand.
“No one ever told me any of this.”
Lei moved to silence the maid now that she had outlived her use. But Lài’s sword caught the knife before it could strike.
“Get up, Àkané.”
And then Lài began to speak of his own past.
Lakán Lài
content.html: Lakán Lài

content.text: He was born on September 25, 1910, to a farming family in Bïlàitān City. He had a younger brother named Kau. As the elder son, Lài was expected to inherit the family trade; Kau, by contrast, was free to study, and people spoke of him as a future scholar. The only thing Lài had over his brother was strength. Even so, he had thought that enough, so long as his family was happy.
content.html: He was born on September 25, 1910, to a farming family in Bïlàitān City. He had a younger brother named Kau. As the elder son, Lài was expected to inherit the family trade; Kau, by contrast, was free to study, and people spoke of him as a future scholar. The only thing Lài had over his brother was strength. Even so, he had thought that enough, so long as his family was happy.

content.text: Then, in 1925, the curse of fifteen descended upon him. Bïlàitān City was said to have fallen to the Alignment forces; in truth, the city had been the one set ablaze by Nine Earths itself. In the broad course of history, it was merely a pretext by which Shàun Pàlé obtained emergency powers. But history meant nothing to Lài.
content.html: Then, in 1925, the curse of fifteen descended upon him. Bïlàitān City was said to have fallen to the Alignment forces; in truth, the city had been the one set ablaze by Nine Earths itself. In the broad course of history, it was merely a pretext by which Shàun Pàlé obtained emergency powers. But history meant nothing to Lài.

content.text: His family vanished into the fire.
The Alignment forces who came later took him up and made him a soldier. He did not even know which side was enemy and which side ally. He only knew how to wield strength.
The one who stayed his hand was a scarred wanderer: Kïnàbé. Lài gave him food. When Kïnàbé asked what he wanted in return, Lài answered, “I want to be strong.”
And so his sword training began.
Kïnàbé would only shake his head at Lài’s wild, forceful cuts.
“If you never think about what is best, then you cannot protect even the thing you most wish to protect.”
“I have nothing left to protect.”
“You will. The person waiting in the future.”

Five years later, Lài at last became strong enough to defeat Kïnàbé. With that came the byname “Kïnàbé,” and a sword painted in a pure white lacquer that blood would not stain. Kïnàbé said it had once been handed down to him by his own master.
“You’re Bïláin Àkané, aren’t you!”

Àkané lowered his eyes to his feet. There lay the sword painted in the blood-red color of the Bïláin house, bearing its crest—a gift from the parents who had praised him, as a boy, for defending Nèi against the children of the Nine Clans. Upon the hilt were engraved the words: May it be stained with blood no more.
Àkané stooped and took up the sword again. Lài stood before the maid to shield her. So the brothers stood side by side, one with a white sword, the other with a red.
“Match your breathing to mine.”
Àkané’s Stance of Savage Fury shattered the rhythm of Lei’s blade; through the opening thus made, Lài’s Stance of the Void Mind slipped in.

Their master Rià excelled in the Stance of the Moderate. Yet his was not the fundamental form that reconciled the Stances of Rigid Integrity and Gentle Harmony. It was an applied form, one that reconciled Savage Fury and the Void Mind. Ordinarily no single man could perform it. That was why the two of them together surpassed even Rià.
Lài’s sword struck Lei’s knife from her grasp. Àkané’s blade rose to her throat.
“That is enough.”
The door of the First Guest Chamber opened. Standing there were the Armona ambassador—and the false Àkané, Àya.
“What a splendid sight you have allowed me to witness,” Àya said. “Ambassador, that woman is the terrorist.”
He pointed at Lei. She was shaken, but he continued calmly.
“Today, three matters have been decided.
“First: the Kingdom of Armona will work to recognize the full independence of the Kàlavé Instructional Nation by the year 1940.
content.html: “First: the Kingdom of Armona will work to recognize the full independence of the Kàlavé Instructional Nation by the year 1940.

content.text: “Second: even after that independence, the Kingdom of Armona’s army will remain stationed in Líunïshèven Parish.

“Third: in recognition of its considerable service to the independence and stability of the Instructional Nation, the Nine Earths Annex, Zopāitān Classroom, shall be recognized as a vigilante corps officially approved by the National Police Agency. Furthermore, the charge of high treason hitherto laid against its member Lakán Lài shall be withdrawn.”
content.html: “Third: in recognition of its considerable service to the independence and stability of the Instructional Nation, the Nine Earths Annex, Zopāitān Classroom, shall be recognized as a vigilante corps officially approved by the National Police Agency. Furthermore, the charge of high treason hitherto laid against its member Lakán Lài shall be withdrawn.”

content.text: Àya had cast aside the Lei faction and turned the entire design of the Rebel Alliance into an achievement of his own.
“Dispose of that woman outside. This is foreign soil.”
With that, Àya gave orders to his attendants and left the corridor.
“Wait! Do you think you’ll leave here alive?”
Àkané shouted after him, but Lài held him back.
“They’re the ones who let us live,” he said. “That aside—well done, all of you.”

By all rights Àkané and Lài had succeeded in checking the Lei faction’s tyranny. And yet what remained in them was an immeasurable sense of powerlessness, and the foreboding of an even greater threat still to come.
In 1940, in Rifrēz, capital of the Kingdom of Armona, the false Àkané—Àya—signed the peace treaty, and the independence of the Instructional Nation was guaranteed.

“There is a saying: yesterday’s enemy is today’s friend. But a relationship does not change merely because the date on the calendar has turned. To reach this place, we have made many choices. Only after that can we finally speak with one another. That is what must never be forgotten. ‘Choice’ always stands before ‘discussion.’”
When Àya finished his speech, the audience in Rifrēz answered him with thunderous applause.

At that same hour, Lài and Àkané were serving as secretaries to Bishop Sāna of Sèlàn. Àkané, however, did so under the name Kau, wearing a mask whenever he appeared before others. When he chafed at the indignity of such a life, Sāna soothed him: for now, he said, the time was one for deliberation with the bishop, in preparation for Àya’s next move.

Outside the auditorium, swordsmen of the next generation were at practice with bamboo swords. Teaching them was the man who would later be remembered as a legendary master: Yïmádén Rià. His skill remained overwhelming; even Lài and Àkané together could barely defeat him.
Then, all at once, a bamboo sword came to rest at his throat—wholly outside the bounds of his awareness.
“Well done,” Rià said. “Your name?”
“Nekinei.”
The girl who bore the name of the god of seeds gazed far beyond the horizon.

In the 1940s, Kàlavé, now independent of Armona, came into conflict with the Worl Federation, the Kingdom of Armona—both of the capitalist camp—and Varalia of the socialist camp over the oil rights of the Duram Sea. Shutolska on the capitalist side, and Durguatan and Skniune on the socialist side, all of them sharing that inland sea, insisted on treating the Duram as a “lake” and claimed territorial rights over it. Kàlavé, on the other hand, argued that it must be treated as a “sea,” so that its territorial waters could be extended and its right to develop oil secured. Though Kàlavé belonged to the capitalist camp, it won to its side former Alignment states such as Niqeltoliĕnē and Ishtelvia, both hostile to Armona. In December 1945 a compromise was reached: fifteen nautical miles would be an exclusive resource-development zone, twenty-five nautical miles an exclusive fishing zone, and all waters beyond that a “Joint Zone.” Thus Kàlavé, whose own territorial sea had been narrow, succeeded in gaining development rights over a vast shared district. The diplomatic triumph swelled the country’s pride at home, and its conflict with Worl—now stripped of its oil interests—grew markedly sharper.
content.html: In the 1940s, Kàlavé, now independent of Armona, came into conflict with the Worl Federation">Worl Federation, the Kingdom of Armona—both of the capitalist camp—and Varalia of the socialist camp over the oil rights of the Duram Sea. Shutolska on the capitalist side, and Durguatan and Skniune on the socialist side, all of them sharing that inland sea, insisted on treating the Duram as a “lake” and claimed territorial rights over it. Kàlavé, on the other hand, argued that it must be treated as a “sea,” so that its territorial waters could be extended and its right to develop oil secured. Though Kàlavé belonged to the capitalist camp, it won to its side former Alignment states such as Niqeltoliĕnē and Ishtelvia, both hostile to Armona. In December 1945 a compromise was reached: fifteen nautical miles would be an exclusive resource-development zone, twenty-five nautical miles an exclusive fishing zone, and all waters beyond that a “Joint Zone.” Thus Kàlavé, whose own territorial sea had been narrow, succeeded in gaining development rights over a vast shared district. The diplomatic triumph swelled the country’s pride at home, and its conflict with Worl—now stripped of its oil interests—grew markedly sharper.

content.text: In February 1948, Tïrïu Air Flight 102, bound from Yìlà International Airport in Kàlavé to Fiĕ Shenciĕné International Airport in Worl, was hijacked by the “Nine Earths Kau faction.” The ringleader, a man calling himself Yanan Kau, vanished into the outskirts of Fiĕ Shenciĕné City together with his followers. Worl suspected the Kàlavé government of involvement and detained not only members of the Kau faction but other citizens of Kàlavé as well. Kàlavé, in turn, denounced Worl before the international community.
content.html: In February 1948, Tïrïu Air Flight 102, bound from Yìlà International Airport in Kàlavé to Fiĕ Shenciĕné International Airport in Worl, was hijacked by the “Nine Earths Kau faction.” The ringleader, a man calling himself Yanan Kau, vanished into the outskirts of Fiĕ Shenciĕné City together with his followers. Worl suspected the Kàlavé government of involvement and detained not only members of the Kau faction but other citizens of Kàlavé as well. Kàlavé, in turn, denounced Worl before the international community.

content.text: In March 1949, the Kau faction infiltrated Erc City, the company town of the Erc corporation, incited workers already embittered by low wages and brutal labor conditions, and, together with them, brought the city down. Worl, meanwhile, was consumed by the Efarlia War of Independence that had broken out at the same time, and abandoned any plan to use force against the Kau faction. Kàlavé seized the opportunity. Under cover of “pacifying” the faction, it moved to take over Erc City itself, bought out Erc corporation, and thereby brought management rights into Kàlavé hands.

In November 1950, the Nine Earths Kau faction occupied the Worl embassy in Hāké, Kàlavé, on the pretext of espionage. En Nèkïné of the Hāké police—granted permission by Worl to storm the embassy—came face to face with a Kau faction member named Tïnàbé, who was holding the Worl ambassador hostage. Catching sight of the Duïlé knife at her breast, Tïnàbé made a proposal.
content.html: In November 1950, the Nine Earths Kau faction occupied the Worl embassy in Hāké, Kàlavé, on the pretext of espionage. En Nèkïné of the Hāké police—granted permission by Worl to storm the embassy—came face to face with a Kau faction member named Tïnàbé, who was holding the Worl ambassador hostage. Catching sight of the Duïlé knife at her breast, Tïnàbé made a proposal.

content.text: “If you are one of the faithful too, let us settle it with that.”
“Release the hostages first. That is my condition.”
The two took up their knives. Nèkïné, constrained by the presence of hostages, could not swing freely; Tïnàbé, by contrast, came on with blow after blow in a furious rush.
“As Hlï-Yíl-Tàu wills!”
At last Tïnàbé disarmed her and moved in for the finishing stroke.
At that very instant, one of his subordinates gave the signal. While Tïnàbé had been absorbed in the duel, his men had released the hostages.
Nèkïné instantly drew her pistol and shot him once through the leg. Tïnàbé shouted in outrage.
“This is sacred ground for a duel!”
“I don’t care,” she said. “I’m a police officer.”

She snatched away Tïnàbé’s knife, knocked him unconscious with the flat of her blade, clapped handcuffs on him, and freed the ambassador. Thus the embassy hostage crisis was brought to a safe conclusion.

Yet in January 1951 Worl severed diplomatic relations with Kàlavé, and in October 1954 it designated Kàlavé a state sponsor of terrorism. The antagonism between the two countries was now beyond repair.
One day in 1955, Yanan Kau was granted an audience with Àya—the false Àkané—at Gàtïna Auditorium in Hāké, official residence of the First Instructor.
content.html: One day in 1955, Yanan Kau was granted an audience with Àya—the false Àkané—at Gàtïna Auditorium in Hāké, official residence of the First Instructor.

content.text: “Preparations are proceeding without a hitch, Your Highness the First Instructor.”
“This is something you are doing of your own accord,” Àya replied. “Still, I do believe in ‘results first.’”

After the Long Night War, Dool had become independent from Kàlavé at the same time Kàlavé itself broke away. But Dool’s ruling party, the Dool Anti-War Council, turned hard toward extreme pacifism and decided, as proof of that turn, to abolish the military altogether. The Armona Royal Army, whose passage into Kàlavé had been blocked by Dool’s pacifist policy, chose to withdraw from the base at Líunïshèven. Those decisions encouraged an invasion of Kàlavé from Dool, which contained a great many Dírál believers. Àya intended to use the Kau faction—made up of remnants of the Lei faction—to push forward Kàlavé’s effective control over Dool.
content.html: After the Long Night War, Dool had become independent from Kàlavé at the same time Kàlavé itself broke away. But Dool’s ruling party, the Dool Anti-War Council, turned hard toward extreme pacifism and decided, as proof of that turn, to abolish the military altogether. The Armona Royal Army, whose passage into Kàlavé had been blocked by Dool’s pacifist policy, chose to withdraw from the base at Líunïshèven. Those decisions encouraged an invasion of Kàlavé from Dool, which contained a great many Dírál believers. Àya intended to use the Kau faction—made up of remnants of the Lei faction—to push forward Kàlavé’s effective control over Dool.

content.text: In November, the former members of the Rebel Alliance assembled in Dùrabay Autonomous Parish, near the Kàlavé-Dool border.
“We’ve talked and talked,” said Lài. “It’s a pity, but it looks as though the time has come.”
“You only put up with my selfishness, that’s all.”
Àkané tried to encourage Lài, who was pulling a vexed face.
“If you’ve got time to waste on chatter, get ready. The age of playing at swordfights is over.”

So spoke Yïmádén Rià, leader of the Xàsané faction and a master swordsman—though even as he said it, he went on sharpening the beloved blade he was so proud of. The Xàsané faction had once been recruited by Àya as a vigilante corps officially recognized by the National Police Agency. But the rise of the Kau faction, and still more the revelation of the real Àkané, had stirred them deeply, and Nine Earths split in two: the “Main Nine Earths,” puppets of Àya, and the anti-Àya Xàsané faction.
content.html: So spoke Yïmádén Rià, leader of the Xàsané faction and a master swordsman—though even as he said it, he went on sharpening the beloved blade he was so proud of. The Xàsané faction had once been recruited by Àya as a vigilante corps officially recognized by the National Police Agency. But the rise of the Kau faction, and still more the revelation of the real Àkané, had stirred them deeply, and Nine Earths split in two: the “Main Nine Earths,” puppets of Àya, and the anti-Àya Xàsané faction.

content.text: A brazen-looking youth stepped up before the Rebel Alliance barricade.
“Rest easy, Rià. In the name of the Tïràun house, your deeds shall be exempt from punishment.”

It was Tïràun Paté. Though one of the Nine Clans, he had spent his life thus far in tobacco and violence, a wastrel beyond redemption, and was counted among the lowest contenders in the race to become the next First Instructor.
content.html: It was Tïràun Paté. Though one of the Nine Clans, he had spent his life thus far in tobacco and violence, a wastrel beyond redemption, and was counted among the lowest contenders in the race to become the next First Instructor.

content.text: “There,” Lài said with a grin to Àkané. “One of your fellow Nine Clans.”
Annoyed, Àkané snatched the cigarette from Paté and warned him.
“I’m grateful you pushed for mobilizing the regular army. But do not, under any circumstances, do anything extra.”

The bishops of the Southern Littoral Archdiocese, Sāna of Sèlàn among them, had deep historic ties to the Tïràun house. So, under the official fiction that they were suppressing the “terrorist” Kau faction, the Tïràun house had indeed helped push through the deployment of the regular army. Even the First Instructor could not simply ignore a cause publicly championed by the Tïràun house.
content.html: The bishops of the Southern Littoral Archdiocese, Sāna of Sèlàn among them, had deep historic ties to the Tïràun house. So, under the official fiction that they were suppressing the “terrorist” Kau faction, the Tïràun house had indeed helped push through the deployment of the regular army. Even the First Instructor could not simply ignore a cause publicly championed by the Tïràun house.

content.text: Then the sound of gunfire rang out through the darkness. It was the signal to begin. Yet the “regular army” here meant no great force: the Tïràun house, a minority among the Nine Clans, had managed to provide only the Fifth Corps, a relatively green unit usually assigned to the quietest region in Kàlavé. Worse still, another matter had arisen suddenly that very day, and only a small number could be spared for this battle. Most likely Àya had seen to that himself.
“Then again,” Àkané said, letting his guard slacken a little, “we really may not have anything to do.”
At that very moment, a flash burst through the command room.
“'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï!”
A suicide assailant blew himself up, and members of the Kau faction stormed into the room.
But when they kicked open the door, the muzzles of their guns found no trace of Àkané or the others.
In the next heartbeat, Lài was behind them. He stepped into sword range and cut them down soundlessly.
“So in the end,” he muttered, “it comes to this.”
Àkané answered by swinging the familiar Blood-Red Knife—the heavy crimson blade he knew so well. The room filled with a wrath that seemed ready to devour the enemy’s dying cries.
“Help me, Rià!” Paté wailed.
“Shut up!”
Paté screamed almost as loudly as the men being cut down, while Rià, faintly irritated, fought on and shielded him all the same.
Within moments the rank-and-file were gone, and relief spread over Paté’s face.
“Right, then—I’m getting out of here.”
“Wait!”
A swordsman approached Paté in utter silence just as he turned to leave. The strike aimed at him pierced instead through Rià’s abdomen as he leapt in the way.
“Master!”
The swordsman looked down upon Rià where he had fallen, and at once drove in to finish him; but Lài’s blade stopped the blow.
“My name is Yanan Kau. In the name of the laws of Ismizlark, Dírál branch, I will bring down the hammer of justice upon you.”
content.html: “My name is Yanan Kau. In the name of the laws of Ismizlark, Dírál branch, I will bring down the hammer of justice upon you.”

content.text: “A terrorist has no right to speak of justice!”
Àkané’s killing intent flared toward the slayer of his master, and through that murderous heat Lài sent in a strike so soundless it seemed not to exist.
“‘Kau’ is my brother’s name,” he said. “I won’t let you stain it.”
Kau brushed aside Lài’s attack with ease and, in one fluid motion, sent the next blow at Àkané.
“This swordsmanship was bestowed on me by His Highness the First Instructor—upon you too, ‘sacrifice’ Àkané.”
Àkané tossed back, almost lightly, “That’s really the one thing you shouldn’t have said, isn’t it? Being in league with Àya, I mean.”
The taunt did not shake him as once it might have. The fact that he was merely his brother’s ‘insurance’ no longer held power over him.
“Be silent, sacrifice!”
Kau forgot himself in rage. At once Lài struck from behind.
“The moment you get emotional, the pace becomes ours. Come on, then—shall we ‘talk’?”

As soon as Kau was disarmed, Àkané moved in and pinned him down. The distant gunfire, too, was beginning to die away. According to this man, “Yanan Kau” was no more than the code name borne by the leader of the Kau faction; one head would be replaced, and another would appear.

The next day, Sāna of Sèlàn and Paté handed the captured man over to the First Instructor and announced publicly that the Kau faction had attempted an advance into Dool. Of course, that alone could not serve as proof of collusion between Àya and the Kau faction.
Paté, however, was rewarded by the First Instructor for his military “service.”
TÏRÀUN PATÉ SUPPRESSES TERRORISTS, NEWLY EMERGES AS LEADING FIRST INSTRUCTOR CANDIDATE.
“Give me a break! He didn’t do anything!”
Àkané tore the special-edition newspaper to pieces.
“At least Master pulled through.”
Lài tried to soothe him.
“In any case, let us pray that peace will return to Kàlavé now.”
Rià, stretched out in a hospital bed, was thinking instead of Kàlavé’s future.
“Prepare the operation.”
An intelligence officer of the Worl Federal Preparatory Committee—Worl’s intelligence organ—gave orders to the chiefs of the ethnic minorities in Kàshatïréqï State.

In 1961, Worl did not send in its regular army. Instead, through Jaxadarn, it dispatched the Efarlian army, along with mercenary companies—Alan International Service, Galival Mercenaries, and Gial D'Stēl PMC—into Kàlavé.
content.html: In 1961, Worl did not send in its regular army. Instead, through Jaxadarn, it dispatched the Efarlian army, along with mercenary companies—Alan International Service, Galival Mercenaries, and Gial D'Stēl PMC—into Kàlavé.

content.text: In May of that year, the Worl expeditionary force entered Vïshān Autonomous Parish inside Kàlavé territory. There they found Paté, who had been disowned by the Tïràun house for repeated misconduct. Worl intended to seat Paté in place of the First Instructor and establish a puppet regime in Kàlavé.

News of Worl’s advance reached Lài and the others in Sèlàn at once. Worl’s purpose was plain enough. The remnants of the Republic, living underground, had joined hands with Worl’s army in hopes of restoring democracy to Kàlavé. Yet Lài felt a deep unease. He had seen too much himself—Pàlajé, Lei, Àya, Rià. Their desire to protect Kàlavé had been real.
“Right,” Àkané burst out. “I’m not going to let us become some foreign power’s puppet!”
But his anger did not run in the same direction as Lài’s. Lài’s uncertainty was of another sort: he did not yet know whether he ought to choose “discussion” or “choice.”
“Then why not try both?”

The suggestion came from Nèkïné, who had returned home on leave for the holiday. To protect Kàlavé and at the same time overthrow Àya’s tyranny—could such a thing truly be done? Lài put a hand to his head. Yet the “discussion” continued through December. Fortunately, during that time the Kàlavé army managed to buy them time. But the Worl forces were already pressing as far as the sacred land of Xāváléqï. Try both, then. Lài decided to trust Nèkïné’s words.
