content.html: The suggestion came from Nèkïné, who had returned home on leave for the holiday. To protect Kàlavé and at the same time overthrow Àya’s tyranny—could such a thing truly be done? Lài put a hand to his head. Yet the “discussion” continued through December. Fortunately, during that time the Kàlavé army managed to buy them time. But the Worl forces were already pressing as far as the sacred land of Xāváléqï. Try both, then. Lài decided to trust Nèkïné’s words.

content.text: That same month, Worl’s field command, having finally reached Xāváléqï, was established in Tïnagiá Parish. It was the place where Lài and Àkané had begun, and the two of them knew the ground better than anyone. Lài, Àkané, and Nèkïné slipped into the headquarters.
“We have been expecting you. Bïláin Àkané, Lakán Lài—and the young lady from the Hāké police.”
content.html: “We have been expecting you. Bïláin Àkané, Lakán Lài—and the young lady from the Hāké police.”

content.text: A man who seemed to be the commander noticed them. Members of the Federal Preparatory Committee leveled their guns, just beyond the outer edge of sword range. Such was the thoroughness of a great power’s intelligence service: even the fact that the First Instructor Àkané was a counterfeit had already reached them.
“The fighting starts now,” Àkané said, provoking the commander.
Lài checked him with a glance. The commander—Ganali—began to speak.
“To state our conclusion first: we have no intention of founding a ‘Republic.’ You may set your minds at ease. All we require is a convenient head.”

From the next room came the sounds of Paté amusing himself with women. Worl wanted only to recover the oil rights and plant its own capital in the soil of Kàlavé. But the price of that “only” was enormous. Lài could already see the future: Kàlavé’s culture overturned from its roots, the country made dependent on Worl capital.
“Then where is justice?”
“Wherever money can be made,” Ganali said flatly.
Then he continued:
“That aside, your little infiltration was impressive. Not equal to us, of course—but impressive. And that young lady among you is exceptional.”
Ganali drew his sword. Àkané and Lài moved to do the same at once, but Ganali’s subordinates restrained them.
“‘Duel’ is a custom that survives in my homeland as well. What do you say, young lady?”
“You sure you don’t want me instead?” Àkané cut in.
Ganali shook his head. “Regrettably, you do not interest me. It’s called generational change.”
Lài caught hold of the furious Àkané and held him back.
“You don’t have to force yourself.”
“It’s fine.”
Ignoring Lài’s concern, Nèkïné drew her sword.
Lài and Àkané could do nothing but watch.

Steel rang out. Nèkïné—the undefeated prodigy who had managed even to take a point from Rià himself—met Ganali’s foreign blade on equal terms. Duïlé had originally been a sword art meant to mark the enemy with poison painted on the tip, and so it lacked the single deadly blow meant for a vital spot. Ganali’s sword was another matter. It was the dueling blade of the Galival people, made to strike with certainty at the opponent’s weak points.
“So the rumors were true, after all.”
Disappointed, Ganali drove in a kick. The etiquette of Kàlavé dueling did not apply here. Nèkïné remembered her master Rià’s words.
“Only the one who has mastered the form can break the form.”
She changed stances. Now she stood not in a duelist’s guard, but in the riot-control baton stance taught to the police.
“I should have done this from the beginning. My goal isn’t to beat you. It’s to shut that mouth of yours.”

She gave up on defeating him outright. She had likely already judged that in pure “dueling” he was the better of the two. So instead she met each blow as it came and drew him deeper and deeper into fatigue.
“You’re not attacking?” Ganali asked. “Then the match will never be decided.”
“It will,” said Nèkïné. “The future is waiting.”

She caught Ganali’s blade, slipped his kicks, caught his arm in the gap between sword and foot, and snapped a cuff around one wrist. Then she struck his knee with the back of her blade, seized the opening, locked the other wrist as well, and bore him down.
“Call it,” she said, “the execution of justice.”
“Magnificent. I surrender.”
Nèkïné kept her sword at his throat and threatened him where he lay.
“Then do as I say if you want to live.”
Ganali’s subordinates at once trained their guns on her.
“Wait,” Ganali said. “I had intended to give you a reward from the start.”

He stayed his men and accepted her terms: Àya, Àkané, and Lài were to be brought face to face, and the three of them allowed to decide matters for themselves. If Àya did not repent, then Paté would be placed in power on Worl’s terms.
“We keep our promises,” Ganali said after the handcuffs were removed. “We are merchants, after all.”
Àkané doubted every word of it. Lài, for his part, was simply relieved that Nèkïné had survived.

Together with the Worl expeditionary force, the three of them made for Hāké through the Xāváléqï Corridor. It was the first time in twenty-seven years that Lài had walked that road again. Whether by Àya’s doing or not, the poverty that had marked those days was beginning to recede. Farmers watched the Worl column with anxious eyes. Could their talk of “freedom” truly be trusted? Lài’s unease showed plainly enough, and Àkané and Nèkïné tried to reassure him.
content.html: Together with the Worl expeditionary force, the three of them made for Hāké through the Xāváléqï Corridor. It was the first time in twenty-seven years that Lài had walked that road again. Whether by Àya’s doing or not, the poverty that had marked those days was beginning to recede. Farmers watched the Worl column with anxious eyes. Could their talk of “freedom” truly be trusted? Lài’s unease showed plainly enough, and Àkané and Nèkïné tried to reassure him.

content.text: Then a transmission crackled from the head of the column.
“We are entering Hāké. All personnel, prepare for battle.”

In January 1963, fighting broke out in Hāké between the Kàlavé army and the Worl expeditionary force. Faced with Worl’s overwhelming strength, the Kàlavé army could do nothing but retreat toward the country’s largest city, Yìlà.
“Your Highness the First Instructor, we should fall back to Yìlà for the moment.”
“No. Take Akau and go to Yìlà yourselves.”

Àya refused the proposal of the Second and Third Instructors and urged them to depart carrying only his son Akau. The gunfire had already come within reach of Gàtïna Auditorium itself. Once the other Instructors had gone, “Yanan Kau” entered the room and made his report.
“Your Highness the First Instructor, the ‘sacrifice’ has arrived at the gate.”
“I know. And stop calling him ‘sacrifice.’ His name is Bïláin Àkané.”
Àya was in a panic of the soul. There was hesitation inside him now—just as there had been inside his father.
Back at Gàtïna Auditorium, Lài returned there for the first time in thirty-one years. It was the place where he had lost Kïnàbé and had been able to do nothing but run.
“I won’t lose anything else.”
So he resolved.
“That sort of line is what people call a flag, you know,” Àkané said, trying to lighten the air.
Yet he too was not free of dread at the thought of facing his brother.
“Have you eaten?”
Nèkïné bit into a piece of cheese. Her natural, offhand way of being loosened the tension better than anything else.

The three of them ran for the First Instructor’s office, scattering the guards as they went. Àkané’s heavy blows set the rhythm; Lài filled the gaps between them. Nèkïné, meanwhile, deliberately broke their otherwise perfect coordination, throwing the enemy’s pace into confusion.
When they flung open the office door, the man standing inside was not Àya.
“His Highness the First Instructor is waiting in the audience chamber,” he said. “But the woman there was not invited.”
“In that case,” said Nèkïné, “I’ll make the arrest here, Yanan Kau.”
Nèkïné faced Kau. At her signal, Àkané and Lài left the room behind her.
She parried Kau’s sword and searched for an opening. To someone who had already fought Ganali, Kau’s swordsmanship felt old-fashioned—classical.
“You were taught a remarkably antique way of fighting. What, was your master from the fifteenth century?”
“Do not insult His Highness the First Instructor!”

Nèkïné knew how this worked: when a taunt lands, the opponent loses command of his pace. A calm answer to each tense, forceful stroke was enough to “subdue” him. She struck his right hand, knocked away the knife, and for a moment the fight seemed over.
Then Kau pulled out a syringe and plunged it into himself. It was a doping drug—apparently a new one developed in Varalia, with immediate effect and life itself as the price.
“His Highness the First Instructor uses this as well. Those men will never make it home alive.”

Nèkïné’s breathing faltered with anxiety. Kau snatched up his knife at once. The injection gave him bottomless stamina and senses honed to unnatural sharpness. Nèkïné’s defensive tactics were not merely neutralized; now it was her strength that was steadily being ground away. She would have to attack. And in that instant she remembered the conversation she had had with Ganali in the car in the Xāváléqï Corridor.
“Do you gamble?” he had asked.
She had answered that, as one who kept the Dírál precepts, she avoided such things. And Ganali had gone on:
“Between calculation and speculation, speculation makes more money. People who truly calculate don’t gamble to begin with. It’s not very mathematical, of course.”
At the time Nèkïné had laughed at the gulf between their values. Now, perhaps, she understood him.

She deliberately created an opening. Kau took the gamble. And when he showed her the same opening in turn, she drove a flying knee into him. He crashed through the window and fell from it. The victory was Nèkïné’s.
“Àkané, Lài, Master Rià—I did it.”
Then consciousness left her and she crumpled to the floor. The opening she had made had also opened her own side.
“What a splendid range, the Hlïkau Mountains. A sacred mountain bearing the name of Kau, the god of honesty. That is why I gave my son the name Kau within Akau as well.”
Àya spoke as he looked out the audience chamber window.
“Àya, there’s no need for brothers to fight anymore. We can do this by ‘talking.’”
Àkané pleaded desperately.

“You were able to talk,” Àya replied, “only because I kept on making ‘choices.’ At the Armona embassy, in Dùrabay—by whose grace did you come back alive? You took my debt into yourselves, and now you have joined hands with Worl to bring in a new ‘choice.’ Where is justice in that?”
Every word of it was true. Àkané found himself unable to answer.
“So this time,” Lài said in his stead, voicing the thought Àkané could not, “we choose for ourselves.”
“No,” said Àya. “I will decide alone. A ruler is always solitary.”
He drew out a syringe and injected himself.
“It was by my own will that I asked my mother for injections, and I have kept taking them since childhood. This is their final form.”

Àya pulled a sword from inside his robes. Muscles trained for this one day stood out all over him. The true force that dwelt in Àya—greater even than that of his father Pàlajé or his brother Àkané—was finally released.

Àkané deliberately caught Àya’s sword and did not counterattack. He felt he had no right to. Lài swung his own blade as though trying to stitch the two swordsmen together. He understood that Àya wanted only to cross blades. Two men unable to press the attack—against Àya, who did nothing but pour out bottomless violence.

Gradually Àkané and Lài lost strength. Àya’s attacks began to land directly, and at last the two of them fell, their consciousness reeling away. Through the half-open door, Ganali could be seen watching.
“The matter is settled. There has been no repentance. Time to fulfill the contract.”
Ganali held a pistol in his hand.
“Wait!”
“We want to decide this ourselves!”
Àkané and Lài shouted together. And before them they saw, as if from another world, the shades of the dead.
Nèi and Àkané’s parents. Kau, Lài’s younger brother, and Lài’s parents. Pàlajé. Kïnàbé. Those lost figures gave them the strength to rise.
“Let’s win.”
Resolve settled into both their hearts.
In Àya’s eyes, the two of them had become Pàlajé and Kïnàbé.
“You are the past!”
He struck in fury. Àkané received the blow head-on and forced it back. There was the weight of two men in that stand: Àkané, and Pàlajé through him.

Lài ran, almost dancing, across the footing created by Àya and Àkané’s clash, and steadily drove Àya into a corner—like Kïnàbé in his later years. Àkané forced Àya to lose his sword. Lài moved into the posture for the final stroke. The matter ought to have ended there.
“So—what do we do?” Àkané asked.
“After all,” Àya said, “I still want to decide.”
Lài smiled. “All right. That’s not so bad either.”
And the three of them fell at once.

A week later, Paté, having completed the tail-inheritance rite at Hlïyā Great Temple, delivered his first address at the Hāváréjï Great Auditorium. In his hand he held the manuscript Ganali had written for him.

“There is a saying: yesterday’s enemy is today’s friend. Yet crossing from one day into the next does not necessarily change a relationship. People’s ways of thinking may change suddenly between yesterday and today. The discussions we have built up, and the choices we have built up, may one day be wiped clean all at once. Even so, tomorrow we must again make choices, and again hold discussions. Which of the two is right is as Hlï-Yíl-Tàu wills. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”
There were three special editions of the newspapers that day.
THE FIRST ADDRESS OF HIS HIGHNESS TÏRÀUN PATÉ, FIRST INSTRUCTOR, CAUSES A STIR FOR ITS OBSCURITY.
THE MAN WHO HAD IMPERSONATED BÏLÁIN ÀKANÉ, FIRST INSTRUCTOR, FOR LONG YEARS—SHÀUN ÀYA—TO BE TRIED BY THE NINE CLANS IN THE ISMIZLARK LEGAL ASSEMBLY.
PARLIAMENT CONVENED FOR THE FIRST TIME IN THIRTY-SIX YEARS, SINCE THE DAYS OF THE REPUBLIC; LAKÁN LÀI ELECTED SPEAKER.

Nèkïné was directing traffic in Marazei Parish, Hāké. Her abandonment of duty in joining Worl’s coup had come to light, and she had been demoted to the traffic division—but by the narrowest of margins she had managed to remain on the force.
“Please don’t throw the newspapers away! I understand how you feel, but please don’t stomp on His Highness the First Instructor’s face!”

At the Shàun family residence in Flinner Parish, Hāké, Àya was under house arrest. He had barely survived, thanks to treatment arranged by Ganali, but remained bedridden. From outside the window he could hear his son Akau speaking with his sword master Rià. Lài had given Akau his pure-white sword. When offered the choice between Àkané’s crimson blade and Lài’s pure-white one, Akau had chosen the white.
“Akau,” Rià said, “you may choose. Or you may consult others. Only make sure you do not live to regret it.”

At Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium in the I Ma Archdiocese of Hāké, Lài crossed the Bridge of Conversion from the Conference side toward Parliament. Today’s agenda was the annihilation of the remaining Kau faction. The Worl army, installed at a base in Líunïshèven, was occupied with guarding the offshore oil fields and had left the unprofitable extermination of the Kau faction entirely to the Kàlavé army.
content.html: At Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium in the I Ma Archdiocese of Hāké, Lài crossed the Bridge of Conversion from the Conference side toward Parliament. Today’s agenda was the annihilation of the remaining Kau faction. The Worl army, installed at a base in Líunïshèven, was occupied with guarding the offshore oil fields and had left the unprofitable extermination of the Kau faction entirely to the Kàlavé army.

content.text: “Order! As believers of the Dírál branch, we must exchange no words that would shame us even before God!”

At Èmerán Great Auditorium in Setain Parish, Sèlàn, Àkané had succeeded the bishop directly under Sèlàn under the name Kau. Worl, fearing that the legitimate Àkané might by some chance become First Instructor, had forced him into life there. Yet Àkané himself, having lived twenty-seven years as an ordinary commoner, said that this life suited him better. Together with the maid, he laid the Blood-Red Knife before his family’s grave.
“May Kàlavé be stained with blood no more. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”

The Worl-backed puppet regime sent in Worl troops to monopolize the oil concessions, but left the unprofitable work of annihilating the Kau faction to the Kàlavé army. Worse still, it was revealed that Worl had also been supplying arms to the Kau faction and profiting from the conflict between the Kàlavé army and the Kau faction. Anti-Worl sentiment in Kàlavé rose to its peak.
content.html: The Worl-backed puppet regime sent in Worl troops to monopolize the oil concessions, but left the unprofitable work of annihilating the Kau faction to the Kàlavé army. Worse still, it was revealed that Worl had also been supplying arms to the Kau faction and profiting from the conflict between the Kàlavé army and the Kau faction. Anti-Worl sentiment in Kàlavé rose to its peak.

content.text: In 1966, a coup d’état broke out in Jaxadarn, and Worl lost its military right of passage. With that, its supply network was cut, and Worl’s hold over Kàlavé began rapidly to totter.
content.html: In 1966, a coup d’état broke out in Jaxadarn, and Worl lost its military right of passage. With that, its supply network was cut, and Worl’s hold over Kàlavé began rapidly to totter.

content.text: In August 1968, the Second and Third Instructors—who until then had concerned themselves only with ritual observances—demanded Paté’s resignation. He died in an accident while fleeing into exile in Worl.

With the support of a majority in the Conference, Hánén Kïlàné of the Shàun-family line became First Instructor. Kïlàné appointed Ládén Tané of the Ládén family as Public Relations Officer and had him fiercely denounce Worl’s interference in domestic affairs. His brilliant oratory won him the support of a public already seething with anger at Worl, and people began to speak of him as the next First Instructor.
content.html: With the support of a majority in the Conference, Hánén Kïlàné of the Shàun-family line became First Instructor. Kïlàné appointed Ládén Tané of the Ládén family as Public Relations Officer and had him fiercely denounce Worl’s interference in domestic affairs. His brilliant oratory won him the support of a public already seething with anger at Worl, and people began to speak of him as the next First Instructor.

content.text: Shàun Akau
content.html: Shàun Akau

content.text: Akau was born in Hāké in 1961. She was female. Her father was Shàun Àya, the man who had impersonated Bïláin Àkané, the second First Instructor. The Nine Clans—especially the Tïràun house, whose influence had grown under the Paté administration—chose not to punish Àya under the law, in order to preserve the dignity of the Nine Clans. Instead, the final authority over Akau’s upbringing was entrusted not to Àya but to Sèine, his first wife.
content.html: Akau was born in Hāké in 1961. She was female. Her father was Shàun Àya, the man who had impersonated Bïláin Àkané, the second First Instructor. The Nine Clans—especially the Tïràun house, whose influence had grown under the Paté administration—chose not to punish Àya under the law, in order to preserve the dignity of the Nine Clans. Instead, the final authority over Akau’s upbringing was entrusted not to Àya but to Sèine, his first wife.

content.text: Sèine, who had borne no son, resolved to raise Akau as a boy, so as to prevail in her political struggle with the second and third wives. Even Àya himself knew nothing of it. It became one of the deepest secrets in the whole Instructional Nation.

In January 1970, Akau was taken hostage in the Flinner Parish barricade incident, carried out by the mafia group Nia Kïnàiny on orders from politicians who had built their fortunes alongside Worl under the Paté regime. Though still a child, Akau placed herself before the hostage-takers in order to protect her bedridden father Àya. In her hand she held the Pure-White Knife. The police could not enter that “forbidden zone”; Kïnàiny no longer existed. Under such circumstances, the man who came into the Shàun residence to rescue them was a mysterious figure who called himself Apàla.
content.html: In January 1970, Akau was taken hostage in the Flinner Parish barricade incident, carried out by the mafia group Kïnàiny" data-ref-type="glossary" data-canonical="Nia Kïnàiny">Nia Kïnàiny on orders from politicians who had built their fortunes alongside Worl under the Paté regime. Though still a child, Akau placed herself before the hostage-takers in order to protect her bedridden father Àya. In her hand she held the Pure-White Knife. The police could not enter that “forbidden zone”; Kïnàiny no longer existed. Under such circumstances, the man who came into the Shàun residence to rescue them was a mysterious figure who called himself Apàla.

content.text: Using the Stance of the Void Mind, Apàla hid his presence and cut down the members of Nia Kïnàiny one after another. At last he faced their leader, a man calling himself Nia Kïnàbé, and spoke.
“Wretches who sully the name of the ‘legendary’ Kïnàbé—under the law of Ismizlark, I bring down the hammer of justice.”
content.html: “Wretches who sully the name of the ‘legendary’ Kïnàbé—under the law of Ismizlark, I bring down the hammer of justice.”

content.text: They were amateurs, nothing more. Against a seasoned warrior of Nine Earths, they had no hope of winning. Apàla slipped aside from the false Kïnàbé’s gunfire and struck swiftly at his vitals. When he untied Akau’s hands, she asked him,
content.html: They were amateurs, nothing more. Against a seasoned warrior of Nine Earths, they had no hope of winning. Apàla slipped aside from the false Kïnàbé’s gunfire and struck swiftly at his vitals. When he untied Akau’s hands, she asked him,

content.text: “Who are you?”
“I am one who will bring choice back to Kàlavé. You have already chosen to protect someone. One day, learn to make the ‘choice’ to erase an enemy with your own hands.”
With that, Apàla vanished from the mansion.

In March of the same year, when remnants of the Kau faction were discovered on islands off Duïlavé Bay, Kïlàné ordered the National Police Agency’s anti-terrorist unit to carry out the final extermination operation against them. The commander given those orders was Èn Nèkïné. She had risen through the Hāké police and now also served as swordsmanship instructor to her subordinate, Lakán Sài.
content.html: In March of the same year, when remnants of the Kau faction were discovered on islands off Duïlavé Bay, Kïlàné ordered the National Police Agency’s anti-terrorist unit to carry out the final extermination operation against them. The commander given those orders was Èn Nèkïné. She had risen through the Hāké police and now also served as swordsmanship instructor to her subordinate, Lakán Sài.

content.text: “Enemy leader: ‘Yánán Kau,’ real name Asásén Pàlau. Capture him alive without fail. Your safety, and the safety of your men, comes first.”

Nèkïné passed the order on to Sài. Sài and his team boarded the Kau faction’s vessel and subdued its members with practiced efficiency. Their long feud with the Kau faction had taught them its every habit. At last they confirmed the capture of the “last” Yánán Kau and all remaining members.
Then, all at once, an explosion roared through the ship. Fire raced through the interior. Sài’s subordinates began to withdraw.
“Sài, fall back.”
“There’s still an enemy aboard.”
Ignoring Nèkïné’s order, Sài advanced deeper into the burning vessel in search of the source of the blast. There, amid the flames, a lone man appeared.
“So you’re the last of the ‘Kau faction.’”
“Do not call me by a title from a dead age. I am Apàla, the emissary who will usher in a new era.”
Apàla came at him without heeding Sài’s demand that he surrender. He slipped through Sài’s gunfire and drew a knife.
“So it’s time to use this after all.”

Sài realized he had entered knife range and drew his own blade. He took up the Stance of the Moderate-Refined, a form developed by Nèkïné by fusing Duïlé swordsmanship with police riot-suppression techniques. It not only turned aside Apàla’s attacks, but never forgot to attack in return. In that sense, Sài’s style corrected Duïlé’s weaknesses.

Yet Apàla’s style too had evolved beyond the old “legend.” He left the afterimage of emotion inside his emptiness on purpose. Sài’s eye was caught by it, and so he realized too late that Apàla’s next move had already filled the whole ship.
The vessel exploded again.
Blown overboard by the blast and hauled up by a Maritime Police launch, Sài looked back and saw Apàla’s silhouette standing upon the burning ship.
“The true Instructional Nation shall rise again!”
Apàla’s cry remained in Duïlavé Bay as the ship sank.

In 1976, Kïlàné dismissed Tané from his post as Public Relations Officer. Tané had been a devout admirer of Shàun Pàlajé. By that time Pàlajé’s virtues and crimes alike were already known, and he was spoken of only as a legend—the “Founding Father.” But Tané was different. He adored Pàlajé and dreamed of restoring the “true Instructional Nation” that Pàlajé had made. Kïlàné had sensed that hidden thing lurking inside the words with which Tané stirred the masses.
content.html: In 1976, Kïlàné dismissed Tané from his post as Public Relations Officer. Tané had been a devout admirer of Shàun Pàlajé. By that time Pàlajé’s virtues and crimes alike were already known, and he was spoken of only as a legend—the “Founding Father.” But Tané was different. He adored Pàlajé and dreamed of restoring the “true Instructional Nation” that Pàlajé had made. Kïlàné had sensed that hidden thing lurking inside the words with which Tané stirred the masses.

content.text: In his despair, Tané went to pray in a chapel in Flinner Parish.
“'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï. Hlï-Yíl-Tàu, guide and instruct me, for I have lost my way.”
Into the chapel came Akau and Majén Qàlé, who would later become the Second Instructor.
content.html: Into the chapel came Akau and Majén Qàlé, who would later become the Second Instructor.

content.text: “Brother Tané! This is Akau—the Founding Father’s grandson.”
Qàlé introduced them.
“This is before God. Quiet yourselves.”
Tané chided them, and the three left the chapel together.
“How do you do, Your Excellency Public Relations Officer. I am Shàun Akau.”
“Former Public Relations Officer,” Tané said darkly. “You’ve seen the news.”
He could not understand what he had misjudged. He had only been loyal to the Instructional Nation.
“Please smile,” Akau told him. “If you’re alive, that’s enough. It may not be much comfort, but still.”
She gave him a radiant smile. Tané laughed.
“You’re cute.”
He had praised her. Cute. It was a word no one had ever used for her in all her life.
“Cute? Akau’s cool, not cute!”
Qàlé hurried to lighten the mood. But Akau’s face was already burning. Once again, at fifteen, her fate began to veer audibly off its course.

It was Akau who was officially chosen to succeed him as Public Relations Officer. She herself thought there was no way she could possibly fill Tané’s place. But Tané pushed her onward. He believed Akau could do the job. He taught her the art of speech, and as she deepened her ties with him, her thoughts turned more and more toward him.

That December, when the Instructors traveled on pilgrimage to Xāváléqï, Akau, Tané, Qàlé, and a girl from Hologant City in Deltai—Koto Tál Aitēl—accompanied them. Presumably Koto had been brought along at the urging of the Ládén family, which wished to bind Tané to Koto, daughter of the great Deltai conglomerate Aitēl. By Kïlàné’s arrangement, the “young people” were allowed to amuse themselves at a ski resort in neighboring Gīlàtān City.
content.html: That December, when the Instructors traveled on pilgrimage to Xāváléqï, Akau, Tané, Qàlé, and a girl from Hologant City in Deltai—Koto Tál Aitēl—accompanied them. Presumably Koto had been brought along at the urging of the Ládén family, which wished to bind Tané to Koto, daughter of the great Deltai conglomerate Aitēl. By Kïlàné’s arrangement, the “young people” were allowed to amuse themselves at a ski resort in neighboring Gīlàtān City.

content.text: “What are you making?”
“Gàtïna Grand Auditorium.”
Akau called over to Qàlé, who had started playing in the snow. Though only ten, he seemed already to prefer calm pursuits.
“If you get scared and lean your weight backward, you’ll just lose your balance.”
Tané, gently supporting Koto as he glided beside her, was teaching her to snowboard.
“Thank you. You’re kind.”
Koto pressed herself against him a little too deliberately. She probably understood, as Tané did, that she herself was no more than a piece in an arranged marriage.

But Akau was different. In earnest, she feared that Tané’s heart might be moved by Koto. More than anything, she carried her own “secret”: no matter how hard she tried, she could never end up united with him. She hated that.

Sulking, she took to the slope alone and in her recklessness shot off the course. She fell just short of the cliffside, and Tané came running and pulled her against him. Feeling the narrowness of Akau’s body, he released her at once.
“I’m sorry. Are you all right?”

In a confusion of joy and embarrassment, Akau turned her face away. Tané too felt ashamed, having allowed an improper thought to flicker across his mind. The two of them had just regained their footing and were about to leave the cliffside when—
Koto reached out, trying to push Tané over the edge.
“What do you think you’re doing?”
Akau seized Koto’s wrist and stopped her.
“I was so taken with Lord Tané’s back that I simply reached for it.”
Koto smiled it away. She was lying. There had been killing intent in it, and Akau, trained in Duïlé since childhood, understood that at once.
“What are you doing? Come on, let’s go.”
Tané noticed nothing and walked away. Koto too started to follow, but Akau stopped her.
“Wait. You did that on purpose.”
“Whatever do you mean?”
Koto shook off Akau’s hand.
“Don’t play dumb. If you’re plotting something against Tané, I’ll stop you.”
“Isn’t it you who’s plotting ‘something’?”

Koto’s taunt struck home. Akau threw the first punch. It became a duel in the snow—only this time, with fists. Rià had taught her more than swordsmanship alone. Akau’s blows, driven by raw strength, came at Koto in earnest. But Koto slipped around them lightly. Even on the snow, she moved like someone perfectly trained.
“I can tell by the way you move.”

Akau saw through her. Koto ignored the accusation and counterattacked. Hers was an art made for killing: minimum motion, strikes aimed at vital points, each movement narrowing the footing beneath her opponent. Little by little, Akau was driven back toward the cliff.
“It can’t be helped. I suppose you’ll have to do in his place.”
Again Koto tried to hurl Akau over the edge.
“Hey! We’re heading back!”
Qàlé appeared just then, and both girls lowered their hands.
“I saw how close you and he were getting, and I became a little jealous. Forgive me.”
Koto apologized and took Akau’s hand. It was a lie. Akau shook her off and hurried away. What had that woman wanted? The doubt remained in her heart.

On the train ride back from Xāváléqï to Hāké through the Xāváléqï Corridor, that road once trodden by so many heroes now lay crossed by high-speed rail, and the poverty that had once marked it was nowhere to be seen.
content.html: On the train ride back from Xāváléqï to Hāké through the Xāváléqï Corridor, that road once trodden by so many heroes now lay crossed by high-speed rail, and the poverty that had once marked it was nowhere to be seen.

content.text: “You were trying to shield me, weren’t you?”
Inside the carriage Tané spoke quietly to Akau.
“It’s what you’d call a ‘political marriage.’ The Shàun family and Aitēl have been pushing it through without regard for my wishes. You were opposing that, weren’t you? Thank you.”

It was not exactly the truth, and yet Akau was happy. At the same time, she saw in him a gentleness she had not fully understood before. He was the kind of man who could create a kinder Kàlavé. In that instant, she swore that she would stake her life on making Tané the First Instructor.

Akau undertook a sweeping reform of the state broadcaster and made a name for herself by launching a new media strategy through entertainment and current-affairs programming. Above all, she laced propaganda for Tané’s succession throughout it. Before long, the whole country was convinced: next would be Tané.

Then, in August 1978, a shocking report ran through Kàlavé. Kïlàné had died suddenly. Sài, by then assigned to the Metropolitan Police Department’s First Investigations Division after the destruction of the Kau faction, was conducting the scene examination. Nèkïné, now head of the same division, warned him.
“Sài, I understand how badly you want the truth. But don’t dig too deep. I have a bad feeling about this.”
“Which is exactly why we investigate.”

He could feel a great conspiracy in it. While his colleagues tried to write it off as “no criminality involved,” Sài kept pursuing the case. Before he knew it, he was isolated within the police itself.
At the same time, Akau, upon hearing the news at Gàtïna Grand Auditorium, felt the same foreboding. She too ordered her subordinates to investigate the case by their own means.

The next day, one of them—a masked man—reported his findings to her. Under pressure from the Main Nine Earths, which at that time were officially recognized by the National Police Agency as a vigilante corps, the police had halted the investigation. Yet one man alone, a detective from the Metropolitan Police Department’s First Investigations Division calling himself Sài, had sent a petition to the Prime Minister’s Residence asking that the investigation be reopened. When Akau asked whether they had any suspects, the masked man read out a list—and among the names was Tané’s.
Akau went cold. There was no possible way he could have murdered His Highness the First Instructor.
“Where is that detective now?”
“In the First Committee Room, in the Kyogakuin Tower of Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium.”
I have to go. At once. Driven by a nameless impatience, Akau made to leave her office. Then the man behind her spoke.
“The time for ‘choice’ is close at hand. If the need arises, call on me.”
Akau ignored him and left.
At Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium, she ran across the Bridge of Conversion from the Hōgiin side toward Kyogakuin. She had to protect the man she loved; that resolve blew her fatigue away.
content.html: At Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium, she ran across the Bridge of Conversion from the Hōgiin side toward Kyogakuin. She had to protect the man she loved; that resolve blew her fatigue away.

content.text: The Kyogakuin Tower, outside the session period, lay wrapped in silence. Akau entered the First Committee Room.
“You must be the detective in question. I am Shàun Akau, Public Relations Officer. Please tell me what you know.”
She addressed Sài, who was examining the scene.
“Your Excellency Akau? Thank goodness. I’m Lakán Sài, First Investigations Division, National Police Agency. I have a favor to ask.”

Sài began to explain. Many people had been present at Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium that day, but all had their belongings inspected at the entrance. Only members of the Nine Clans had been exempted by privilege. And among the Nine Clans present in the First Committee Room at the time, there had been only one: Ládén Tané.
Akau was struck speechless. Tané was under suspicion. Her voice trembled as she asked,
“But even that isn’t decisive proof that he’s the culprit, is it?”
“No. His Highness the First Instructor died of cardiac arrest. If poison was involved, then in this country there are only limited routes by which it could have been obtained.”
And when she heard that, she understood what she herself had to do.
“So—what do you want from me?”
“I want you to submit this investigative report to the Prime Minister’s Residence and ask for the case to be reopened.”
“I refuse.”
Akau’s knife struck true at Sài’s vitals. Before he could understand what had happened, he collapsed.
But in a near-fatal state Sài forced himself up again.
“If I die here, justice dies too. I will leave this room alive.”

He drew his baton. There was almost no time left now. He needed an attacking Duïlé, and despite his mortal wounds he launched a furious assault on Akau. She snatched up the knife again and turned aside his heavy blows.
“Tané is the man who will become First Instructor!”

She slashed in anger. The clash of their weapons rang through the silence. Sài batted her knife away and struck back, landing a blow in her solar plexus. She forced the pain down through sheer strength of will.
At last Akau made him lose his baton. In that instant Sài collapsed for good.
“Father... did I manage to surpass Lakán Lài the way I was supposed to?”
content.html: “Father... did I manage to surpass Lakán Lài the way I was supposed to?”

content.text: As Akau watched Sài’s strength leave him, the masked man who served under one of her own subordinates entered the room. It was Apàla.
“So you saw.”
“Put your mind at ease. I have come to offer you a ‘choice.’”
With that, he handed her the badge of the White Owl. Nine Earths would take care of everything here. Without hesitation, she took the badge into her hand.
“I won’t hesitate anymore. I will protect Ládén Tané.”
The paint on the Pure-White Knife she held had chipped slightly, and blood had seeped into it.

Once Kïlàné’s mourning period ended, public opinion filled with cries that Tané alone should become the next First Instructor. The Conference effectively ratified what the people already believed, and Tané ascended to the office of First Instructor.

By 1984, Tané had built Tané Triport—a unified complex of airport, seaport, and railway station—and founded a new flag carrier, Foll Air, turning it into the hub of Kàlavé’s international transport. At home he introduced free medical care for citizens only, established a minimum wage system, and expanded women’s suffrage by appointing female legislators. He also tightened the prohibition laws against alcohol until drinking itself was clearly an illegal act, and restructured the national mode of self-identification: not “Kàlavé people,” but “Dôlmart ethnic peoples,” stressing religious and ethnic identity above all.
content.html: By 1984, Tané had built Tané Triport—a unified complex of airport, seaport, and railway station—and founded a new flag carrier, Foll Air, turning it into the hub of Kàlavé’s international transport. At home he introduced free medical care for citizens only, established a minimum wage system, and expanded women’s suffrage by appointing female legislators. He also tightened the prohibition laws against alcohol until drinking itself was clearly an illegal act, and restructured the national mode of self-identification: not “Kàlavé people,” but “Dôlmart ethnic peoples,” stressing religious and ethnic identity above all.

content.text: Abroad, he concluded the Lurpe Agreement in 1986 and raised the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé into the position of leading state within the regional community. As part of an international public-relations strategy, he founded the news organization Kuo Hramé; and he also pushed Kàlàbal cuisine overseas by making luxury food culture—caviar above all—one of its pillars.

Behind Tané’s brilliance stood Akau’s dark labor. After the unrest in West Altos in 1979—the Skniune civil war and the economic slump in Deltai—the Main Nine Earths had been formally recruited into and absorbed by the National Police Agency’s Public Security Bureau. In particular, Five Heavens, the Deltai criminal syndicate that expanded rapidly amid that recession, united criminal organizations across Deltai and amassed vast wealth through the trade in illegal drugs.

In December 1984, Nine Earths launched an operation to destroy Five Heavens within Kàlavé, all in the name of sound Instruction. Having obtained intelligence that the Aitēl company was a front for Five Heavens, Akau personally led Nine Earths in an assault on its headquarters.
“It’s hot.”
Though it was December, Koto wore light clothing. She had become the second wife of Ládén Tané. Her other face was that of Blue Heaven, one of the executives of Five Heavens.
“I wanted to kill you myself. It was worth the trouble of coming here in person.”
So saying, Akau drew her knife. She could not leave alive the woman who had stolen love away from her.

Koto drew her own blade and met her. The reason she dressed so lightly was not only to move more freely, but also to sharpen her own oversensitive senses. She read Akau’s movements through her skin and answered them on reflex.

Akau’s swordsmanship had been honed. For eight years she had studied Koto’s movements in preparation for this very reckoning. Koto always moved as little as possible. Wide, committed attacks were dangerous—but then Koto’s style hardly consumed stamina, so dragging the fight out was no easy answer either.
“You love Tané too, don’t you? I understand.”
Koto taunted her.
“Too? You only ever used His Highness the First Instructor.”
“You understand nothing. I truly loved him. Enough to want to kill him.”
The room was covered wall to wall with photographs of Tané. Her love was genuine, whatever else it may have been.
Akau flung open the window. Cold air rushed in. The wind dulled Koto’s reflexive counters, and in that instant Akau forced the knife from her hand.
“I’ll kill you slowly, savoring eight years of hatred.”
“Your love is almost adorable.”
The moment Akau leveled her blade at her, the room exploded.
When Akau awoke in a hospital room, Tané was there.
“Are you all right, Akau? You were caught in a gas explosion. Koto was caught in it too—she died instantly. I’m just glad that at least you survived.”
He knew nothing. He grieved for the wife he had lost, yet from the bottom of his heart he was relieved that Akau had lived.
“I caused you concern, Your Highness the First Instructor.”
Akau was happy—not because Koto had been eliminated, but because the man she loved had worried for her so sincerely.

A week later, Akau received word that Blue Heaven was alive in Durguatan. In Kàlavé, however, Koto was treated as dead, and the fact that she had been Blue Heaven became knowledge confined to the Instructors alone. Akau did not much care. She had eliminated one who sought Tané’s life, and had protected him. That was enough.
content.html: A week later, Akau received word that Blue Heaven was alive in Durguatan. In Kàlavé, however, Koto was treated as dead, and the fact that she had been Blue Heaven became knowledge confined to the Instructors alone. Akau did not much care. She had eliminated one who sought Tané’s life, and had protected him. That was enough.

content.text: At Gàtïna Grand Auditorium, Akau appeared in the First Instructor’s audience chamber.
“There’s something I have to tell you. The truth is, I...”
She laid bare her secret before Tané. She was terrified of how he might answer.
“That has nothing to do with me.”
Tané drew her into an embrace. For the first time in her life, Akau let another person see her cry.
“I want you to smile. If you’re alive, that’s enough. Isn’t that so?”
He looked straight into her face. Smiling as brightly as she could, she handed him the bouquet of cyclamen she had brought in her hand.
Its flower language was: the realization of a dream.
The End.

In 1986, at Lurpe, the port city of Skniune, Kàlavé took the lead in signing a regional economic pact with Skniune—which had broken free from Varalia and democratized. This was the Lurpe Agreement, an integration project built on a unified currency, the abolition of border inspections, and the removal of tariffs. It strengthened Tané’s power base and served as the lever that raised Kàlavé into the position of hegemon within the regional community.
The Lurpe Agreement marched under slogans of anti-communism and anti–Five Heavens, and placed special importance on excluding Five Heavens’ influence from all member territories.

That December, the Main Nine Earths were assigned to escort a cargo vessel sailing from Shmekerpe in Skniune to the direct parish of Yìlà in Kàlavé. They had sensed that Five Heavens might attack the ship while it transported “confidential materials.” Searchlights raked the midnight sea from end to end.
content.html: That December, the Main Nine Earths were assigned to escort a cargo vessel sailing from Shmekerpe in Skniune to the direct parish of Yìlà in Kàlavé. They had sensed that Five Heavens might attack the ship while it transported “confidential materials.” Searchlights raked the midnight sea from end to end.

content.text: “Keep watch underwater as well.”

Apàla, the Shadow Owl, commanded the operation on site. The Main Unit had long since been folded into the National Police Agency’s Public Security Bureau. Apàla wore the latest equipment issued by the state, but “tradition” still rested, unchanged, inside his pocket.
content.html: Apàla, the Shadow Owl, commanded the operation on site. The Main Unit had long since been folded into the National Police Agency’s Public Security Bureau. Apàla wore the latest equipment issued by the state, but “tradition” still rested, unchanged, inside his pocket.

content.text: “Shadow Owl—there’s a noise coming from one of the containers.”

The speaker was a blonde female operative. Unlike the ordinary patterns by which Nine Earths personnel were transferred, this woman had been reassigned by “orders from above.” Her expression looked pasted on. For intelligence work, she was probably ideal.
Members of Nine Earths surrounded the container. Just as they moved to open it, the container burst apart in a great explosion and sprang open on its own.
“I’ve come to kill you, my beloved husband!”
Out from the container came several members of Five Heavens and, among them, a woman wrapped in bandages. The inside walls of the container were covered with photographs of Tané.
“So Blue Heaven herself has shown up.”
At Apàla’s order, his subordinates opened fire. Koto slipped through the powder smoke and closed to knife range. One after another, Nine Earths operatives fell.
“Oh dear. That didn’t feel at all like killing my husband.”
Koto could no longer see. But having lost one of her five senses, all the others had grown yet sharper.
“You insolent creature who takes the name of ‘Heaven’ in vain—under the law of Ismizlark, I bring down the hammer of judgment.”
content.html: “You insolent creature who takes the name of ‘Heaven’ in vain—under the law of Ismizlark, I bring down the hammer of judgment.”

content.text: Apàla drew his knife and faced her. His Stance of the Void Mind was a form that read the flow of presence itself; for that reason it paired poorly with Koto’s “automatic counterattack.”
“Unlike that woman’s, your blade has no charm at all.”

Koto kept her attacks to a minimum and saved her strength for counters. Her style, too, paired badly with Apàla’s, for he excelled at controlling emotion itself. Trading blades, the two moved gradually into the cabin.
“Futile, isn’t it?”
“You still don’t know that.”
Apàla drew his sidearm and fired into the ceiling. The sprinklers went off, soaking Koto.
“I took precautions.”
He sent his knife straight for her center line.
Blood dripped down with the water—from Apàla’s own chest. Koto had swung the knife by her own will.
“I took precautions too.”
She had learned how to switch between “automatic” and “manual.”
Pulling her blade from the collapsing Apàla, Koto turned to leave the cabin. At that moment the blonde operative appeared before her, standing at the very edge of knife range and leveling a pistol.
“I need to speak with you, Koto Tál Aitēl.”
content.html: “I need to speak with you, Koto Tál Aitēl.”

content.text: “I’ve nothing to say. I dislike people who want to talk.”
Koto moved for her gun, but the blonde woman shot cleanly through her hand before she could. Then she pulled out the “confidential materials” and tossed them to Koto.
“Let’s make a deal. There is something I want from your people.”
She walked toward Koto. The two women slipped out of Apàla’s field of vision.
“May the true Instructional Nation endure forever!”
His cry rang through the cabin.
In October 1986, Qàlé was a student of nuclear engineering at Nalegia Federal University in Sansonle, capital of the Republic of Nalegia.
“Now then, today I’ll be explaining the moderation process of neutrons in a light-water reactor. The material used most commonly as a moderator is...”

Qàlé sat in the very front row, listening to the lecture with rapt attention. On either side of him sat his favorite stuffed animals. Gone was the boy he had once been; at twenty, Qàlé had grown into a quiet young man. Then a blonde girl came in, removed one of the stuffed animals, and took the seat.
“Sorry—mind if I sit here?”
Qàlé was faintly displeased at having one of his “friends” displaced, but he was too kind to refuse.
“Being late is bad form, Miss Long. Since you interrupted the class, perhaps you’d explain why neutrons mustn’t remain fast.”

The professor was scolding the blonde girl—Satella Long. She was a Solan from the Republic of Har-Lopinong. Har-Lopinong accepted many Solan immigrants, and she belonged to the second generation. With the clear-cut features so often associated with East Asians, she was popular on campus. Satella had no answer ready, so Qàlé slipped her a discreet crib note.
content.html: The professor was scolding the blonde girl—Satella Long. She was a Solan from the Republic of Har-Lopinong. Har-Lopinong accepted many Solan immigrants, and she belonged to the second generation. With the clear-cut features so often associated with East Asians, she was popular on campus. Satella had no answer ready, so Qàlé slipped her a discreet crib note.

content.text: “Uh—uranium-235 reacts more efficiently with thermal neutrons. In other words, once they slow down, fission becomes easier.”
“Correct. I’ll let it pass this time, but from here on, lateness counts as absence.”
Satella gave the professor a small nod, though her expression had tightened slightly.
“Thanks, Qàlé. Honestly, that old guy gets on my nerves.”
“You’re the one at fault.”
Qàlé dealt coolly with her grumbling. She was the only person who spoke to him as an equal, though he belonged to the Nine Clans of Kàlavé and might as well have been royalty.
content.html: Qàlé dealt coolly with her grumbling. She was the only person who spoke to him as an equal, though he belonged to the Nine Clans of Kàlavé and might as well have been royalty.

content.text: When class ended, Qàlé headed alone for the cafeteria, with Satella trailing after him. Since she insisted on buying him lunch to thank him, he reluctantly allowed her to join the “luncheon party” of himself and his stuffed animals.
“Classes are so boring! But then again, I don’t exactly want a job either!”
She said she had entered the engineering department only because there happened to be a foreign-student quota. She had no real interest in study at all.
“I’m not like you. I study because I care about it. Kàlavé may have natural resources, but it’s still dangerous to go on depending on thermal power forever.”
Ignoring Satella’s attempt to fish for sympathy, Qàlé explained why nuclear energy mattered. Satella slurped her noodles with obvious boredom.
“Well, if you’re enjoying yourself, that’s enough for me.”
She gave him one of her clumsy smiles, and it made Qàlé’s heart skip a beat. By the time they had finished talking, the noodles had long since gone soft.

In December 1986, after the sudden death of his father Alei, Qàlé became the Second Instructor. He began living between Kàlavé and Nalegia, carrying out public duties while continuing his studies. People would later say that Nalegia joined the Lurpe Agreement partly because Qàlé had personally fostered ties between it and Kàlavé.

Qàlé entered the same seminar as Satella and began writing his graduation thesis. Its title was “Optimization of the Fuel Cycle and Analysis of Neutron Economy in Next-Generation Fast Breeder Reactors.” While helping Satella keep up with studies she was in danger of failing, he steadily produced results of his own.

Around that time, Qàlé’s aides began to suspect that there was something unnatural about Satella’s admission. But by the time they finally discovered the truth, Qàlé and Satella were already on the verge of graduating. It was too late for everything.
In June 1988, after receiving their degrees, Qàlé and Satella began packing up their dorm room.
“Qàlé, look—this is that periodic-table mug we got as a participation prize when we gave our seminar presentation, isn’t it? We never did use it.”
Together they packed away one memory after another. Satella moved briskly and efficiently; Qàlé’s hands kept stopping, reluctant to let the moment go.
“If we’d won, there would’ve been prize money, you know. But you lost your script the day before the presentation.”
“Don’t hold grudges. You’re rich, aren’t you? Besides, experience matters more than anything.”

Satella waved it away with some plausible-sounding nonsense. After finishing with her own belongings, she moved to help pack his as well. Qàlé could not bring himself to keep moving. Once this was done, she would leave.
“Say... won’t you come to Kàlavé with me? It’s a little inconvenient, maybe, but it’s safe and it’s a good country. More than that—I don’t want to part from you.”
Gathering all his courage, Qàlé confessed his feelings. He had never imagined in his life that he would say such a thing.
Satella looked flustered and stumbled over her words. When she had pulled herself together enough to answer, what she said was the one thing Qàlé had hoped not to hear.
“I’m sorry. I found work in my own country.”
Qàlé burst into tears. Satella handed him a tissue at once and began soothing him.
“And besides—we’ll meet again.”

She took the periodic-table mug from among her things and handed it to him. Qàlé, in turn, rummaged through his belongings and gave her the red spider lily badge he treasured. The red spider lily was Kàlavé’s national flower, and its floral meaning was: looking forward to the day we meet again.
After exchanging gifts, they went back to packing. Their time together vanished in what felt like an instant. As Satella turned to leave the room, she called out to him.
“See you.”
She still wore that same clumsy smile. Qàlé answered it, and the two of them shook hands. Left alone at last, he stood looking at the mug she had given him.
Then his subordinates came running in, white-faced, and Qàlé learned the truth. The mug slipped from his hands and shattered.
At that same moment, in the restricted-access room of Professor Ain’s laboratory at Nalegia Federal University, a blonde woman was stealing the professor’s materials.
“What are you doing, Satella?”
Qàlé had followed her. His expression was severe, his gaze fixed straight on Satella where she stood in the dark.
“No—nothing. I just forgot something.”
“That room is off limits.”
Once she realized the lie had failed, Satella let the pasted-on smile drop from her face. It was an expression Qàlé had never once seen in those four years.
“I wanted to stay your friend until the very end. But this is my mission. Approaching you was part of it too.”

Satella was a “Witch,” one of the operatives of the Har-Lopinong secret society Guĕnar. Guĕnar moved in the shadows with the aim of “making the world happy.” It regarded Kàlavé’s instructional monarchy as one source of the world’s unhappiness, and saw the elimination of Nine Earths—and above all of the White Owl who propped that unhappiness up—as Satella’s ultimate mission. Nine Earths had stolen “confidential materials,” that is, information on uranium enrichment, from Har-Lopinong. The confusion sown through Five Heavens, the corroboration offered by those “confidential materials” and by Professor Ain’s own work, the approach to the Second Instructor—the pieces of the puzzle had all fallen into place.
“So you betrayed me. And you think I’m going to help you?”
“I do.”

Satella drew her knife. Even Qàlé, who had learned only the bare minimum of self-defense, understood what she was doing. She was moving with deliberate exaggeration. It was a demonstration meant to plant fear. He turned aside her attack and slammed her down onto the floor.
“I see. So this much isn’t enough to frighten you.”

Her face went rigid in a frightening way. In the next instant she truly tried to kill him. Her knife-work was as light as a witch’s wand, and cuts opened one after another across Qàlé’s body. There was a resemblance to Duïlé in that it specialized in wounds rather than single decisive blows, but hers was incomparably faster.
Little by little Qàlé was driven back against the wall. At this rate, he really would die. He cast his eyes around him.
“See you.”
As Satella raised the knife, Qàlé snatched up a flask from close at hand and hurled it at her. It struck true. She lost her balance and dropped the knife, and he seized it at once.
“Let’s stop this. I have no obligation to stop Nine Earths.”
“You do. Once you know the truth.”
Satella began to tell him. The true identity of the White Owl. The truth behind Kïlàné’s death. And the crime committed by his friend Akau.
Qàlé fell silent and let the knife drop from his hand. In a single day, he had lost two friends.
In August 1990, the funeral of Lakán Lài, former Speaker of Parliament, was held at Èmerán Great Auditorium in Setain Parish. Invited as an honored guest, Tané read the eulogy.
content.html: In August 1990, the funeral of Lakán Lài, former Speaker of Parliament, was held at Èmerán Great Auditorium in Setain Parish. Invited as an honored guest, Tané read the eulogy.

content.text: “As Speaker of Parliament, His Excellency Lakán Lài lived for long years between ‘choice’ and ‘discussion.’ As First Instructor, I present to him the Foxinated Knife. It is, needless to say, a symbol of our country, and only one who has guided this nation in both name and truth is permitted to wield it.”
content.html: “As Speaker of Parliament, His Excellency Lakán Lài lived for long years between ‘choice’ and ‘discussion.’ As First Instructor, I present to him the Foxinated Knife. It is, needless to say, a symbol of our country, and only one who has guided this nation in both name and truth is permitted to wield it.”

content.text: Akané, now bishop directly under Sèlàn, accepted the Foxinated Knife from Tané, placed it into Lài’s coffin, and offered a prayer.
“Before Hlï-Yíl-Tàu, may our brother continue to guide us rightly. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”
The mourners followed with prayers of their own. Sobs and prayer together filled the hall.
Beside the graves of Akané’s family stood the graves of the Lakán house. Sài’s name too was carved there.
“My brother regretted Sài’s death all his life,” Akané said.
Tané listened. Neither of them knew that it had been the White Owl who had buried Sài.
“Akau seems well enough,” Akané added.

There was no citizen in Kàlavé who did not know how Akau was faring. She was the spokesman of the Tané administration, stirring the people with ever more incendiary rhetoric. Akané had intended the words as irony, but Tané pretended not to hear it. Then Nèkïné came running up to them.
“Akané—the knife is finished.”
She handed him the polished Blood-Red Knife. Today, Akané and Tané were to offer a sacred match before God in Lài’s memory.
“On it is engraved: ‘May there be no more bloodshed from this point on.’ But if you deceive me here before God, then this blade will be dyed with your blood.”
“I understand. I will not hold back.”
Tané gave his word. Upon the special stage, the two men drew their knives. The mourners watched the prayerful clash of blades.

Tané held an unadorned knife. The Instructors were permitted to bear the Foxinated Knife, yet he did not. There were two reasons. One was reverence for those who had gone before. The other was that a knife was not, in his eyes, an instrument of “choice.”

Akané showed again the Stance of Savage Fury he had used in years past. He let anger rise within him: the anger of all those dissatisfied with the Tané administration, and the bitterness his brother had carried unavenged. Tané received it in earnest. He faced the people’s anger, and Lài’s unfinished longing, head-on.
“There is one thing I would ask.”
Akané spoke as the knives crossed.
“Did His Highness the former First Instructor truly do no more than die out his natural span?”
The air among the mourners froze. Perhaps in all the country, only Akané had the right to ask such a question now.
“In the name of Hlï-Yíl-Tàu, I swear that I know nothing. I was only told that he had died in the natural course of things.”
Tané answered. Everyone present understood the weight carried by those opening words.
Tané was not lying. Once Akané knew that, he let his hand fall still and sheathed his knife, wearing an expression that might have been relief or disappointment.
“It is time.”
They bowed before God and stepped down from the stage. Neither of them spoke another word after that.
That same night, Tané and Akau lay together in a bedchamber of Gàtïna Grand Auditorium. Only Tané knew that Akau was a woman. Staring up at the ceiling, he spoke of what had happened that day.
“Do you think everyone will understand my ideals?”
“If they love Your Highness the First Instructor, they must understand them.”
Akau answered at once. But she realized that that was not what he was asking now, and amended herself.
“You are too kind. That is why I came to love you. To want to be loved by everyone—that itself is arrogance.”
“And if I were to say that, even so, I want everyone to love me?”
Tané aspired to become a First Instructor truly understood by the people—not a false image of understanding manufactured by Akau’s propaganda.
“If you say that, I’ll grow jealous.”
Akau puffed out her cheeks. She had always been very good at smiling.
“You’re as cute as ever, Akau.”
Tané drew her close. She found herself thinking that if only this happiness could go on forever, then there was nothing she would not do to preserve it.

In October 1992, Akau stood in the Nine Clans’ private lobby at Tané Triport in the direct parish of Yìlà. This great gateway of Kàlavé, bearing Tané’s own name, was the very culmination of her love for him. Today she meant to depart from Yìlà for Xāváléqï and, as the White Owl, carry out a special military operation in Dáhlé Autonomous Parish. Its true substance was an invasion of Deltai under the pretext of “annihilating Five Heavens.” Then Satella stepped in front of her.
content.html: In October 1992, Akau stood in the Nine Clans’ private lobby at Tané Triport in the direct parish of Yìlà. This great gateway of Kàlavé, bearing Tané’s own name, was the very culmination of her love for him. Today she meant to depart from Yìlà for Xāváléqï and, as the White Owl, carry out a special military operation in Dáhlé Autonomous Parish. Its true substance was an invasion of Deltai under the pretext of “annihilating Five Heavens.” Then Satella stepped in front of her.

content.text: “So you mean to go tell on me to His Highness the First Instructor?”
“No. Ládén Tané is a bringer of ‘unhappiness.’ It would be no loss to erase him.”
content.html: “No. Ládén Tané is a bringer of ‘unhappiness.’ It would be no loss to erase him.”

content.text: Akau’s face twisted in fury as she drew the Pure-White Knife, still stained with blood. Satella too drew her blade, and the two met in combat.
content.html: Akau’s face twisted in fury as she drew the Pure-White Knife, still stained with blood. Satella too drew her blade, and the two met in combat.

content.text: Akau’s attacks were straightforward. It was the violent, strength-driven Stance of Savage Fury handed down through the Shàun family line—through Pàlajé, Àya, and Akané. Against it, the opponent was forced on the defensive; once that happened, the rhythm belonged to her.

Yet Akau’s movements were too simple, and little by little Satella learned them. She slipped thrusts toward Akau’s vital points into the narrow gaps between one attack and the next. Gradually Satella’s knife began to dictate the rhythm of the battle between them.
“You, who cannot even love a single person, have no right to speak of happiness!”
What drove Akau onward was boundless love for Tané. She was nothing like this woman, who could trample Qàlé’s love underfoot.
“This is a cause greater than ourselves. I bear the duty of making all the people of the world happy!”
Satella’s “happiness,” by contrast, was abstract. It saw no individuals. To realize some grander happiness, she possessed the strength to crush the smaller happiness of one person named Qàlé.
As the fight dragged on, Akau, whose style burned stamina, began to lose ground. A blow to one leg, another to one arm—at last she sank to one knee.
“My duty is to protect His Highness the First Instructor, even at the cost of my life!”
“Is it? From the very beginning, you were never protecting Ládén Tané at all.”

Satella knew the truth of fourteen years earlier. At the time of Kïlàné’s murder, Tané had not been the only member of the Nine Clans exempted from baggage inspection at Mïsénïtíu Great Auditorium. It had been Guĕnar—none other—who supplied poison to the Tïràun house, rivals of the Shàun family. After passing inspection, a member of the Tïràun house handed the poison to a subordinate and sent that subordinate on to the First Committee Room. Guĕnar had witnessed the entire sequence.

For the first time, Akau became conscious of the blood staining her Pure-White Knife. On that day, she had been unable to believe in Tané. Satella seized her by the collar, preparing the finishing blow.
“That’s enough!”
A special unit of the National Police Agency burst into the lobby. At its head was Nèkïné. With her were Tané and Qàlé.
“Satella—we meet again. Unfortunately, Akau and the rest of us have decided to settle this by ‘talking.’”
Qàlé had already told Tané the truth.
“I will never forgive you for killing Sài. The justice you stole—you must win it back yourself.”
Nèkïné ran to Akau’s side and held out her hand to her.
“I have my answer to that night now. Even so, if you wish to be loved by everyone, then you must be sincere. Akau—live true to the name of Kau, the god of sincerity.”
“I failed to notice your suffering. So from this day onward, as First Instructor, I will bear your mistakes—indeed the mistakes of all my people—upon myself.”
Tané came to stand beside Akau. In his hand he held the Blood-Red Knife entrusted to him by Nèkïné.
“Let us settle this.”
Tané and Akau, red and white knives together, drove at the Witch.
“How truly unhappy the people of this country are.”
No one paid any heed to Satella’s lament. At least, no one standing here did. Each of them, in that moment, was happy.

Satella’s assault became a frenzy, blind with self-loss. The two of them parried it calmly and then each did what they alone had to do. Akau stirred her love for Tané higher and higher, showing again the Stance of Savage Fury that loads emotion into the knife. Tané slipped through the spaces between Akau’s emotions and closed in on Satella with the Stance of the Void Mind. It was the moment when the union of shadow and light had been handed down across three generations: Pàlajé and Kïnàbé, Akané and Lài, and now Akau and Tané.
Tané’s knife knocked Satella’s blade to the floor, and Akau’s knife found Satella’s throat.
The matter was settled.
A week later, Satella was deported back to Har-Lopinong. There she would no doubt receive severe punishment in her homeland.

The Nine Clans smothered the entire chain of incidents—from Kïlàné’s assassination to Sài’s murder—in order to preserve the authority of both the Instructional Nation and the Nine Clans. Even so, the members of the Tïràun house involved in Kïlàné’s murder were punished, and Akau was expelled from the Shàun family.
Akau resigned as Public Relations Officer, citing ill health. In truth, it was her own decision, made so that Tané would not have to bear responsibility for her fall.

Tané too spoke, in one of his addresses, of “the news of hibernation.” It was close to a declaration that the First Instructor intended to relinquish office within the coming decade—a form of atonement, after Tané’s own fashion, for being unable to bring the truth to light.
Akané and Nèkïné stood before the graves of the Lakán family.
“Akané—was this really for the best?”
Discontent showed plainly on Nèkïné’s face. Akané, by contrast, looked almost serene.
“My brother would have said it himself: ‘The future is waiting.’”

At the same moment, in the Ládén residence in Flinner Parish, Hāké, Qàlé was holding a “home party,” quite apart from Akau, who remained in seclusion there. The guests were Tané, Akau, and an appalling number of stuffed animals.
content.html: At the same moment, in the Ládén residence in Flinner Parish, Hāké, Qàlé was holding a “home party,” quite apart from Akau, who remained in seclusion there. The guests were Tané, Akau, and an appalling number of stuffed animals.

content.text: “So then! People are always saying, ‘What about radioactive waste?’ But the waste is under strict control, and the technology for vitrifying it, sealing it in stainless-steel containers, and disposing of it geologically is steadily being established! And more than that—with fast reactors, even that waste might be reusable! That’s what the nuclear fuel cycle is about, you know, reprocessing it into MOX fuel and—”
Qàlé went on and on about nuclear power. Whether this was his way of trying to cheer her up, or whether he simply wanted to talk about it, was probably obvious enough.
“I’m sorry, but we can’t leave all these stuffed animals in my house. And if you’re going to eat that cheese, open a window—it smells. Look, Akau doesn’t like it.”
Tané scolded Qàlé sharply. For days he had been crushed under one burden of state after another, and yet Qàlé still rampaged on without the slightest concern.
“That’s not true, is it? Akau, you’ll eat some cheese too, won’t you?”
“No... I’m fine.”
Akau’s face was still dark. She had been like this for a whole week.
“Akau, there’s a difference between reflecting on yourself and sulking.”
Tané laid a hand on her shoulder. But Akau still felt she had no right to look him in the face.
“That’s right, and—ah!”

Getting carried away, Qàlé knocked over the mountain of stuffed animals. Tané disappeared under the flood; when he emerged, a teddy bear was sitting on his head. Akau could not help it—she burst out laughing.
“That’s better, Akau.”
Encouraged by Tané, she finally lifted her face.
She truly was very good at smiling.

In 1890, Kàlavatan—Deltai territory in the land called Kàlavé—was exploited as a peripheral possession of the Deltai mainland. Real power in the Kàlava Council, the administrative organ of Kàlavé, lay in the hands of moderate members of the Nine Clans such as the Tïràun family and the Nálén family, men scrupulously obedient to Deltai’s wishes.
content.html: In 1890, Kàlavatan—Deltai territory in the land called Kàlavé—was exploited as a peripheral possession of the Deltai mainland. Real power in the Kàlava Council, the administrative organ of Kàlavé, lay in the hands of moderate members of the Nine Clans such as the Tïràun family and the Nálén family, men scrupulously obedient to Deltai’s wishes.

content.text: The members of the Council gathered in Tïnāléqï, capital of Kàlavatan.
“We are assembled today for one matter only: the reactivation of Nine Earths.”
content.html: “We are assembled today for one matter only: the reactivation of Nine Earths.”

content.text: Ládén Lā, the First Instructor, presided over the debate. The chamber had split cleanly in two—conservatives led by the Shàun family on one side, moderates led by the Tïràun family on the other—and the hall rang with shouted accusations.
content.html: Ládén Lā, the First Instructor, presided over the debate. The chamber had split cleanly in two—conservatives led by the Shàun family on one side, moderates led by the Tïràun family on the other—and the hall rang with shouted accusations.

content.text: “Nine Earths may be extreme, but they are not without reason. They oppose Deltai’s secularization policy, and they have been watching over border security and customs inspection for us.”

So spoke Shàun Àusé in Nine Earths’ defense. He did not, however, defend them without reserve. The “Instructional Nation” they proclaimed would mean dissolving the Council itself, and that would strip men like him of their privileges as well.
“What nonsense are you talking? Those ‘volunteers’ only inflamed Deltai’s resentment and pushed secularization further.”

The objection came from Tïràun Wàrai, one of the Council’s heavyweights. The logic of the Tïràun family was plainer still. With ties to Deltai’s bureaucracy, they merely wished to eliminate anything that threatened their vested interests. Their talk of doing things “peacefully” was no more than a pretext.
“You are trapped in the old question of which came first, the egg or the chicken. The real matter before us is the serial murder of members of the Nine Clans by Nine Earths.”

Lā checked both factions. Recently, members of the Nine Clans had been murdered one after another. According to testimony from the Tïràun family, who had witnessed one of the crimes, a member of Nine Earths had been seen leaving the scene.
At that moment, a Kïnàiny instructor came running into the chamber.
content.html: At that moment, a Kïnàiny instructor came running into the chamber.

content.text: “The train on the Xāváléqï Line has been hijacked by miners. A Deltai high official has been taken hostage. Their demands are that the ‘transport tax’ be withdrawn and that Kàlavatan become independent as an Instructional Nation!”
content.html: “The train on the Xāváléqï Line has been hijacked by miners. A Deltai high official has been taken hostage. Their demands are that the ‘transport tax’ be withdrawn and that Kàlavatan become independent as an Instructional Nation!”

content.text: The instructor’s name was Ahhárén Xàsané. The man who would later become the Shadow Owl of Nine Earths was, at that time, no more than an ordinary knight.
content.html: The instructor’s name was Ahhárén Xàsané. The man who would later become the Shadow Owl of Nine Earths was, at that time, no more than an ordinary knight.

content.text: Deltai had established a “transport tax” on iron ore from the Duïlé range. In concrete terms, ore shipped from Kàlavé to Deltai was exempt, while shipments to other countries—or even within Kàlavé itself—were burdened with steep tariffs. The transport routes, moreover, were restricted to Deltai carriers.

In truth, the whole arrangement was a self-serving scheme engineered jointly by corrupt Deltai officials and Nine Earths. The corrupt officials cooperated in order to skim kickbacks from transport and distribution interests, and the Deltai government itself knew nothing of it. Nine Earths had staged an “exploitative structure” for the purpose of inflaming the people of Kàlavé against Deltai.

Then, when the anger of miners and transport workers against Deltai had reached its peak, Nine Earths incited them to seize the Xāváléqï Line running from Tïnāléqï in Kàlavé to Tenkanand in Deltai and take a Deltai official hostage.
“It is obvious that Nine Earths is involved. And in any case, crime must be suppressed.”
Lā proposed exchanging the hostages for Kïnàiny at Hāké. But the only man then in Hāké was Káin Kàna, a disciple of Xàsané. Lā sent out his order by telegraph.
content.html: Lā proposed exchanging the hostages for Kïnàiny at Hāké. But the only man then in Hāké was Káin Kàna, a disciple of Xàsané. Lā sent out his order by telegraph.

content.text: Receiving the message in Hāké, Kàna packed at once and made for Hāké Central Station in Malazei Parish. A boy came running after him. His name was Ládén Kïnàbé. He too stood at the fateful age of fifteen.
content.html: Receiving the message in Hāké, Kàna packed at once and made for Hāké Central Station in Malazei Parish. A boy came running after him. His name was Ládén Kïnàbé. He too stood at the fateful age of fifteen.

content.text: “Is it an order from Father? I’m coming with you.”
“I told you to stay put, Kïnàbé. And not ‘Father’—‘His Grace the First Instructor.’”
Buried in his duties, Lā feared that he could not care properly for his son. So he had asked Kàna, his own blood-kin, to stand in as Kïnàbé’s father and teach him the sword.
“Let’s call it ‘results first.’ Do as much as you can.”
So Kàna told him, and the two boarded the waiting train.
“Don’t move! We’ll let the passengers go. But not you two—and not the official.”
The miners surrounded them. They were far beyond reason, wholly swept away by excitement.
“Kïnàbé, remember this. A strategy that tries to make the enemy angry won’t work on an enemy who is already angry. At times like this, you do the opposite. You wake them up.”
With that, Kàna drew a knife from inside his coat and drove it into a chest.
Kïnàbé’s chest.
Kïnàbé glanced down, then convulsed and collapsed to the floor in agony.
“Y-you! You’re with us, aren’t you? What are you doing?”
The miners came to their senses.
“Excellent reaction.”

Kàna drew his second knife—the Pure-White Knife—and in the blink of an eye carved through the miners. Neither the hijackers nor the hostages could understand what had happened. After cramming every miner into a third-class carriage and blocking the door, Kàna turned to Kïnàbé, who still lay sprawled on the floor.
content.html: Kàna drew his second knife—the Pure-White Knife—and in the blink of an eye carved through the miners. Neither the hijackers nor the hostages could understand what had happened. After cramming every miner into a third-class carriage and blocking the door, Kàna turned to Kïnàbé, who still lay sprawled on the floor.

content.text: “That’s enough. Splendid acting.”
Kïnàbé rose and dusted off his clothes. His chest was unmarked.
“When you suddenly asked to borrow my wooden sword, it took me a moment to guess what you were planning.”

The improvised performance of friendly fire spoke of Kàna’s unconventional mind, but Kïnàbé’s instant adaptation was just as extraordinary. Freed of his bonds, the rescued official told the two men to head for the engine room. The mastermind, he said, was there.
The cramped engine room held only a single engineer.
“We have the culprit. Stop the train.”
The engineer ignored Kàna’s order. The train did not slow. Instead it plunged onto a branch line. Ahead lay a dead end.
“I am the White Owl, Sàushé. Let us have the pleasure of crossing hands.”

Producing two knives, Sàushé fell on them. Kàna and Kïnàbé drew at once. In the cramped engine room, Sàushé moved in three dimensions, stripping Kïnàbé of his knife almost immediately. His second blow—a kick—drove Kàna back into the first-class carriage.

Sàushé hurled Kïnàbé’s stolen knife at Kàna, and Kàna barely evaded it. In pure swordsmanship the two were evenly matched, but in close quarters the advantage belonged to Sàushé. Kàna’s leg was slashed; he fell to the floor. Without a word, Sàushé moved to finish him.
Then the train stopped.
Just short of the buffer, at the last possible instant, Kïnàbé had pulled the brake. Sàushé turned and walked toward him.
“Use this!”
