Kàna threw his own Pure-White Knife to Kïnàbé. Catching it, Kïnàbé leapt down from the train.
“This is no longer your range.”
He stood in an open stretch of grass. Sàushé drew a naginata hidden in the engine room and took his stance.
“In respect for your ‘choice,’ I’ll fight you on your own ground.”
The blade of the naginata swept the grass and cut the wind. Riding the currents of air, Sàushé struck once, then again, then yet again. It was the Stance of the Void Mind.

Kïnàbé parried each blow with his knife. His own Stance of Gentle Harmony lacked the power to seize the offensive, and against the Stance of the Void Mind the two were like oil and water. The contest could have gone on forever.

Then Sàushé changed. If it would not be decided that way, he would force it. He shifted into the Stance of Savage Fury and swung the naginata with brutal strength. The momentum-laden strikes crashed down on Kïnàbé. The instant Kïnàbé lost his balance, Sàushé tried to capitalize.

Kïnàbé caught the naginata on instinct and, matching the direction of Sàushé’s force, flung it away. Driven by its own momentum, the weapon stabbed violently into the ground. Kïnàbé’s knife came up beneath Sàushé’s throat.
“That settles it.”
Kàna emerged from the train, dragging his wounded leg.
“A forceful solution.”
Praised by Kàna, Kïnàbé answered with quiet composure.
“I do rather well in physics.”
The warmth of the moment lasted only an instant. A gunshot cracked through the air.
Sàushé threw himself in front of the bullet meant for Kàna and took it into his body.
“I feel... as though I caught a glimpse of the future.”
Leaving those words to Kïnàbé, Sàushé fell.
“And thus the White Owl, Sàushé, died. Even so, just as he himself said, I do not believe he was truly the White Owl.”
That was Kàna’s testimony before the Council in Tïnāléqï. The wound to his leg convinced him to withdraw from the front lines and devote himself to raising disciples.
“No one asked for the opinion of a man who is not a Council member. A crime was suppressed. That is all that occurred.”
Wàrai ignored Kàna outright. Kàna belonged to the Shàun family, and that alone was enough.
“Exactly. How Nine Earths is to be used will be decided by the Council.”
Even the Shàun family, who ought to have been allies, were cold to Kàna. They could not afford to abandon their posture of defending Nine Earths.
“It should be obvious at a glance! Why would you assign a swordsman of that caliber to a mission where he was meant to be thrown away from the start? Because he was not the White Owl!”
Kïnàbé pleaded his case. But Kàna drew him back, and the boy recovered himself.
“The next time Nine Earths moves, uncover the truth for us at last, my son.”
Lā praised Kïnàbé. The boy answered with a smile that lit his whole face.
Note: In this age, Instructors did not involve themselves in politics. The First Instructor governs only in an Instructional Nation.

In 1899, Lā issued a declaration that in this fin-de-siècle year the Child of Destiny would be born from among the Nine Clans. And so there was born the child left-handed from birth: Shàun Pàlajé. To prevent him from becoming a seedbed for Instructionalism through Nine Earths and the like, he was taken from his father Àusé and raised under Kàna as a member of Kïnàiny. At the same time, in recognition of his role in resolving the Xāváléqï Line hijacking, Kàna was recommended for the presidency. Fearing that becoming a politician would keep him from watching over his sons as they grew, Kàna declined. But in 1904 Àusé himself became another victim of the serial murders, and Kàna, following the dead man’s final wish—“Please let my son be free”—resolved to enter politics after all, so that he might shield Pàlajé from the Republic’s indoctrination.
content.html: In 1899, Lā issued a declaration that in this fin-de-siècle year the Child of Destiny would be born from among the Nine Clans. And so there was born the child left-handed from birth: Shàun Pàlajé. To prevent him from becoming a seedbed for Instructionalism through Nine Earths and the like, he was taken from his father Àusé and raised under Kàna as a member of Kïnàiny. At the same time, in recognition of his role in resolving the Xāváléqï Line hijacking, Kàna was recommended for the presidency. Fearing that becoming a politician would keep him from watching over his sons as they grew, Kàna declined. But in 1904 Àusé himself became another victim of the serial murders, and Kàna, following the dead man’s final wish—“Please let my son be free”—resolved to enter politics after all, so that he might shield Pàlajé from the Republic’s indoctrination.

content.text: In 1912, Kàna stepped down from the presidency at the end of his term. Even so, he remained active in politics as a member of Parliament in order to watch over the Republic until Pàlajé reached fifteen. At the same time, he never neglected his affection: on his days off he taught Pàlajé the martial arts and learning of western Tinebris, and helped him tend the creatures he kept.
content.html: In 1912, Kàna stepped down from the presidency at the end of his term. Even so, he remained active in politics as a member of Parliament in order to watch over the Republic until Pàlajé reached fifteen. At the same time, he never neglected his affection: on his days off he taught Pàlajé the martial arts and learning of western Tinebris, and helped him tend the creatures he kept.

content.text: Then, in 1915, sudden news of death reached Xàsané in Ima Parish, Hāké. Kàna had become a victim of the serial murders. Xàsané immediately reported the fact to the Council, but the Council declared that Nine Earths had committed the crime and added, furthermore, that because Flinner Parish was a residential district of the Nine Clans, it lay beyond the police’s right of investigation.

Xàsané could not hide his fury. Throwing aside his subordinates’ attempts to stop him, he set out for Zopāitān City, where the stronghold of Nine Earths was said to be. He seized low-ranking members one after another and tried to work his way up the chain to the main base, but the process stretched on without end. Before he knew it, the year had turned.
content.html: Xàsané could not hide his fury. Throwing aside his subordinates’ attempts to stop him, he set out for Zopāitān City, where the stronghold of Nine Earths was said to be. He seized low-ranking members one after another and tried to work his way up the chain to the main base, but the process stretched on without end. Before he knew it, the year had turned.

content.text: In 1916, a girl calling herself Lei approached him.
“I’ll take you to our White Owl.”
Clinging to that hope, Xàsané followed her into the back alleys. It was a trap. He was abducted by Nine Earths and subjected to savage torture while they demanded to know his purpose.
“My disciple’s future was stolen! I hate you for it!”
Lei laughed at his rage.
“Oh, the serial murders of the Nine Clans? Then you’re looking in entirely the wrong direction.
“Because the ones who did that were your own people.”
Stunned into silence, Xàsané listened as Lei went on.

The Tïràun family had come to resent the Shàun family for wresting vested interests away from them under Deltai rule. Even after the Republic was founded, the Tïràun family tried to build new ties with Armona, while the Shàun family turned toward conservatism and pressed for decolonization. The final blow was Kàna’s policy of loosening the Republic’s grip on the Child of Destiny: among Dírál believers, it threatened to become a dangerously rich source of conservative votes. So he was removed.
content.html: The Tïràun family had come to resent the Shàun family for wresting vested interests away from them under Deltai rule. Even after the Republic was founded, the Tïràun family tried to build new ties with Armona, while the Shàun family turned toward conservatism and pressed for decolonization. The final blow was Kàna’s policy of loosening the Republic’s grip on the Child of Destiny: among Dírál believers, it threatened to become a dangerously rich source of conservative votes. So he was removed.

content.text: Even after laying bare the whole truth, Lei continued tormenting him. By the time Xàsané’s tears had run dry and no sound would leave his throat, Nine Earths abandoned him on the road through the Xāváléqï Corridor.
content.html: Even after laying bare the whole truth, Lei continued tormenting him. By the time Xàsané’s tears had run dry and no sound would leave his throat, Nine Earths abandoned him on the road through the Xāváléqï Corridor.

content.text: The Xāváléqï Corridor: the road so many heroes would walk in years to come. Xàsané walked it now. Hidden beneath everything else was the fact that this was his native land. Here Kïnàiny “employed” orphans, and the Republic paid local strongmen in return. He had always known that Kïnàiny were merely the hands and feet by which the Republic earned its living. Even so, until this very moment, he had continued to hope that one day some sort of future might yet arrive.
When he reached Flinner Parish in Hāké, he kicked in the door of the Tïràun house. He had come for one man only: Tïràun Wàrai.
“What in the world happened to you, turning up like this, Xàsané?”
Even now, was the man going to pretend ignorance? Wàrai came hurrying toward him.
“I came to kill you.”
Xàsané drew his sword. Wàrai did the same, and the two faced each other.
“You killed Àusé. You killed Kàna too, didn’t you?”
“Calm yourself, Xàsané!”

Xàsané threw himself forward in the Stance of Savage Fury—or rather, in something that no longer deserved even the name of stance, a sword swung wholly at the mercy of the moment. Wàrai was a man who served as acting Instructor of Kïnàiny. Fending off the blade of a Xàsané who had lost all reason was easy for him. Exhausted, Xàsané dropped his sword, and Wàrai’s attendants, drawn by the commotion, seized him.
“I truly know nothing, I swear it in the name of Hlï-Yíl-Tàu.”
In Kàlavé, those words carried terrible weight. Perhaps this man really did know nothing. If that was so, then what, exactly, was Xàsané supposed to hate?
Kïnàiny—the same Kïnàiny that had done nothing when Kàna was murdered—threw him into a holding cell.
“What deserves hatred is the system.”
Those were the words of Shàun Pàlé, leader of the Ismizlark Alliance, when he came to see Xàsané in prison and revealed who he was.
content.html: Those were the words of Shàun Pàlé, leader of the Ismizlark Alliance">Ismizlark Alliance, when he came to see Xàsané in prison and revealed who he was.

content.text: “I will restore the ‘choice’ that was taken from you. So keep choosing. For the sake of protecting your grand-disciple.
“May fortune bless your choice.”
A month later, bailed out with money put up by Pàlé, Xàsané disappeared into Zopāitān City. Never again did he call himself a knight.
In 1927, nine years after Xàsané’s death, an elite Kïnàiny unit led by Wàrai arrived at the presidential residence. The Council had identified Pàlé as the White Owl.
“Mr. President, you stand under suspicion of being the White Owl. For questioning, you will accompany us to the Council.”
To Wàrai’s warning, Pàlé replied,
“Do you even understand what you’re doing? I am the president.”
Kïnàiny men poured into the room one after another.
“Whether you remain president will be decided by the Council.”
“It is I who decide!”
Pàlé’s roar broke through the room like thunder.
Wàrai turned to order his men to charge. By the time he looked back, they had already been cut down.

There was no other word for it but monstrous strength. The form Pàlé used united the brute force of the Stance of Savage Fury with the speed of the Stance of the Void Mind: the true Stance of the Moderate, the completed form of Duïlé. Against that stance, victory through Duïlé was impossible.
“You devil!”
Wàrai shouted. Yet when he brought down his sword, Pàlé’s blade was already there, as though fate itself had placed it in the way.
“A devil? What a delightful thing to call me! I know the truth of twelve years ago, Wàrai!”

As had come to light in 1917, the true perpetrators of the serial murders of the Nine Clans had been Wàrai’s own subordinates. To curry favor with him—or to protect the vested interests they enjoyed beneath him—they had killed his political enemies of their own accord. As a result, the Tïràun family profited, though the matter had in fact proceeded beyond Wàrai’s knowledge.
“That is what deserves to be called the work of devils, Wàrai! The Shadow Owl’s anger was entirely justified!”
Pàlé’s sword flashed like a conductor’s baton, orchestrating a verbal assault in time with his blows. Killing Wàrai would have been simple. He was tormenting him instead.
“If that’s your argument, then why didn’t you tell Xàsané the truth?”
Wàrai demanded.
“It was the system that killed Kàna! The Republic, the Council, Kïnàiny—none of that is a lie, is it?”
“If you reduce anger to something petty, you can never smash the system. He protected Pàlajé to the very end. And today, Pàlajé begins to walk the path of Destiny!”
What Pàlé said was sophistry. Yet the madness that could make sophistry sound like righteousness now ruled this country.
“It’s time.”
Having finished his sport, Pàlé put away his toy. With practiced motions he struck Wàrai once in each limb to render him helpless, then drove the final blow into his throat.
The room fell still at once.
“I offer up my life as the sacrifice and pray for the birth of the Instructional Nation. 'Ï'E'A Géjéné Kuējebeyï!”
Thus their story came to its close.

In July 1965, in the Sèlàn Directly Governed Parish, the city to which Worl had brought “freedom” looked almost like a foreign country. Men walked in Western clothes, and women in garments that bared their skin.
content.html: In July 1965, in the Sèlàn Directly Governed Parish, the city to which Worl had brought “freedom” looked almost like a foreign country. Men walked in Western clothes, and women in garments that bared their skin.

content.text: In Setain Parish, the Èmerán Great Hall where Àkané—living under the name Kau—served as bishop stood nearly deserted. Over the past two years, the traditional Kàlavé holidays that had once gone hand in hand with worship had gradually been eroded by growing entertainments and the spread of work on days once kept sacred.
“Sometimes I find myself thinking it was better when Lord Àya was the First Instructor.”
content.html: “Sometimes I find myself thinking it was better when Lord Àya was the First Instructor.”

content.text: That was what Àkané’s maid said.
“If ‘freedom’ already exists before our ‘talking things through,’ then all we can do is change the way we live to match it.”
Àkané remained calm. Everyone in Kàlavé, in one way or another, was trying to come to terms with the “freedom” that had descended on them like a natural disaster.
Meanwhile, in Flinner Parish under the direct jurisdiction of Hāké, Àya spent the holiday with his “son,” Akau, at the Shàun family residence.
content.html: Meanwhile, in Flinner Parish under the direct jurisdiction of Hāké, Àya spent the holiday with his “son,” Akau, at the Shàun family residence.

content.text: “Naked!”
Akau pointed at a provocatively dressed woman on a television commercial. Beriwaus Channel—Worl television—could be received here in Kàlavé as well.
“You mustn’t watch things like that!”

A maid immediately changed the channel to Kàlavé state broadcasting. The wide show was covering the donation scandal of Parliament Speaker Lài. Politicians with vested interests tied to Worl had manufactured a scandal around the “relatively” conservative Lài, and the media, greedy for ratings, kept it on the air. Any sensible citizen of Kàlavé could understand Lài’s troubles. There were even voices nostalgic for the “fake Àkané.”
content.html: A maid immediately changed the channel to Kàlavé state broadcasting. The wide show was covering the donation scandal of Parliament Speaker Lài. Politicians with vested interests tied to Worl had manufactured a scandal around the “relatively” conservative Lài, and the media, greedy for ratings, kept it on the air. Any sensible citizen of Kàlavé could understand Lài’s troubles. There were even voices nostalgic for the “fake Àkané.”

content.text: “Even so, we chose ‘discussion.’ My turn to appear on the stage is over.”
Confined to his sickbed, Àya gazed at Akau—the child who was also “the future.”

“Suppose, for example, that the demand and supply functions of wheat are ∂D/∂P < 0 and ∂S/∂P > 0. If, however, contracts without substance—that is, reckless option strategies not subordinate to price—intervene in that function space, then the equilibrium point E(P*, Q*) of supply and demand may at times even cease to have a real-valued solution. This, in other words, is ‘the ghosting of the market.’”

That was Paté, delivering a lecture in Sazány Great Hall in Hāké’s Hlïkau Parish, a venerable place of worship said to have been founded in 770. It was an economics lecture hopelessly out of place in a house of faith, and not a soul in the audience could keep up. His retainers sighed in the wings while Ganali laughed so hard he had to clutch his stomach. The moment the address ended, Paté hurried over to him.
“Why didn’t you prepare cue cards for me? Thanks to you I had to think for myself!”
Without the slightest sign of remorse, Ganali handed him a perfunctory wad of bills. Paté accepted it like a dog being fed by hand.
“Well, no matter. They were all trembling before my eloquence.”

Paté had no idea how the world actually saw him. Seeing the day’s headline—“A First Instructor Unlike Any Since the Founding of Kàlavé”—he ordered a retainer to clip it out and preserve it without understanding in the least what sort of meaning it carried.
“More importantly, Your Grace the First Instructor, Lady Weli of the Liĕnen house of the Principality of Pestart in the Eltēna Union has arrived today.”

Ganali informed Paté of the day’s schedule. The Eltēna Union was an absolute monarchy close to Kàlavé and, as a rule, did not recognize republican states at all. It was also known as one of the granaries of the world. Worl’s aim was to open a new diplomatic channel by using Kàlavé, which was also—at least formally—an absolute monarchy.
“She’s a woman, right? The women of Eltēna—do they have big breasts?”
Paté shook Ganali by the shoulders. For him, a woman’s value was measured by money and flesh.
Ganali flung Paté into a warehouse and, looking down at him, spoke in a low, threatening voice.
“It has been some time since we clarified the nature of our contractual relationship. You are the ‘tire’ by which ‘we’ put profit into motion. And replacements for that tire are endless.”
Paté went white. He prostrated himself at once and tried to hand back the money he had received. Ganali ignored the gesture and went on.
“Make absolutely certain that you do not offend Lady Weli. She is an important business partner.”
Ganali left the warehouse. Paté, terrified beyond endurance, wet himself.

At the guest house of Gàtïna Great Hall in Hāké, a convoy lined up before the palace where the flag of the Eltēna Union fluttered. A woman in accordance with custom stepped from a car with her face veiled in black cloth. It was Weli Liĕnen fia Pestart. Paté went out to greet her and addressed her in Kalabar. Ganali immediately translated into Melkiĕ, the language of Eltēna, and Weli opened her mouth.

“Ardia will do nicely. How do you do, Your Grace Tïràun Paté, First Instructor. I am Weli Liĕnen fia Pestart. In our country the given name comes first and the family name after. The reverse of Kàlavé.”
content.html: “Ardia will do nicely. How do you do, Your Grace Tïràun Paté, First Instructor. I am Weli Liĕnen fia Pestart. In our country the given name comes first and the family name after. The reverse of Kàlavé.”

content.text: Weli knew not only the language but Kàlavé culture as well. Paté, who had already failed to remember her name, asked for it again. Ganali ignored him and answered Weli in Ardia instead.
“‘Welcome. We have much to discuss inside.’”

Paté and Weli entered the palace. At the dinner banquet, a full course of Kalabar cuisine was served. Ganali had warned Paté to eat with restraint, and he was fighting down an almost painful urge to gorge himself.
“I hear that horseback riding is among your hobbies. How often do you ride?”
Paté did no such thing. Beforehand he had told his retainers, “Horse racing—and the betting side of it.” Ganali had altered that and passed it along to the Eltēna delegation.
“‘During official business, at a ranch outside Hāké.’”
As if overwriting Paté’s answer, Ganali supplied a harmless reply himself. Bit by bit, Paté was earning more of Ganali’s wrath.
“Excuse us. It is time for the First Instructor’s ‘worship.’ He will leave the table for a moment.”
Once he had thrown Paté into the guardroom, Ganali punched him.
“I warned you not to be rude, didn’t I? What was that—‘I hear your country is poor; please do eat plenty today’?”

Eltēna was an agricultural power; even its small states produced more than one hundred thousand koku in yield. Ganali’s mistake had been to assume that even an ignoramus like Paté would understand at least that much. He himself was an intelligence operative of the Worl Federal Preparatory Committee, yet for the sake of the mission he was forced to carry on his shoulders the man who should never have become First Instructor. There was no way that came without strain.

Once Ganali had finished “disciplining” him, there came a knock at the door of the guardroom. Up to that point he had taken meticulous care not to draw a single eye. Sensing something wrong, Ganali pulled out a pistol.
The door cracked open and a flashbulb went off. In that instant of blindness, an assassin’s knife flashed toward Paté. Ganali caught it by a hair’s breadth.
“The Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy, is it?”
That ministry was Eltēna’s intelligence service. Its purpose was plain: to install the kind of monarch the world ought to have. Removing the incompetent ruler Paté was precisely what it had come for.

Ganali knocked the knife aside and counterattacked with his favored kicking techniques. The assassin evaded, opened the distance, and threw another knife. Ganali caught it and flung it back. He felt his blood rising. It had been far too long since he had fought.
“You aren’t fighting in your specialty, are you?”
Ganali said it at once. The assassin’s true trade was not killing from the shadows but dueling. Being the same kind of creature himself, Ganali had seen through him.
“This is an assignment.”
The assassin spat the words coldly and whipped another knife at Ganali from outside his line of sight. Ganali parried it without ever seeing it.
There were sounds outside. This had dragged on too long. The assassin fired off another flash and fled the guardroom.
“Come. We should leave as well.”
When Ganali turned back, Paté had fainted outright from terror.
In August 1968, when the Second and Third Instructors jointly demanded Paté’s resignation after finally losing patience with his disgraceful behavior, Paté resolved to flee to Worl.
“I’ll hide inside an instrument case!”

The members of the Federal Preparatory Committee grimaced at the suggestion. True enough, an instrument case would not fit through an X-ray machine, which meant it stood a good chance of avoiding inspection. And with a private jet, that judgment would be left largely to the pilot or the operating company.

Ganali and his men loaded the instrument case with Paté stuffed inside it onto the bed of a blue truck and drove from Hāké toward Líunïshèven. On the road, a black car took up the chase behind them. It belonged to the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy.

The two vehicles raced side by side along a narrow cliff road, trading gunfire. The Federal Preparatory Committee shot out a tire on the black car, but a shot from the Ministry’s side grazed the instrument case.
The chase seemed to be turning decisively in favor of the blue truck, the gap widening little by little.
Then, just before a sharp bend, one of Ganali’s subordinates discovered a fatal fact.
The brakes were gone. Gunfire had ruptured the brake line and the fluid had leaked away.

The blue truck slammed straight through the guardrail. The gunfire had also loosened the cargo restraints, and the instrument case toppled free, bounced once, and went tumbling down the cliff into the river below.

Having succeeded in eliminating Paté, the Ministry assassin, Pata Vēsha, stepped out of the black car, which had ground to a halt, and turned to leave. At that very moment, a single shot skimmed across his cheek.
“Let’s finish what we started the other day.”

Crawling out of the smoke-shrouded blue truck came Ganali. Though he ought to have been a man who had failed his mission, his face looked strangely clear, as though a great weight had dropped from his shoulders.
“I’ve got no more business here.”
Dodging Pata’s return fire, Ganali closed the distance in an instant. The knife in his hand clearly sought Pata’s chest, while his leg snapped out in a crushing kick that drove Pata to one knee.
“God gave us hands so that we might cast the dice! This is freedom!”

Mad with exhilaration, Ganali slashed downward. In a flash, Pata instinctively knocked the knife aside with a roundhouse kick. Servant of an organization though he had been, he remembered at last the aesthetics of the duel.
“I won’t lose to scum. This is my choice.”
“Magnificent. That is what a duel ought to be.”

Pata’s high kick lifted Ganali off his feet. Kicking techniques were Pata’s own specialty. As Ganali’s balance broke, Pata immediately followed with another kick. But Ganali answered with a flying knee aimed straight at Pata’s skull.
After that exchange of kicks, Ganali was the first to fall.
“So I’m unfit for the service after all.”
With those words, Pata too gave out. He died at last as a human being.

A week later, Paté’s body was found at the mouth of the Yìlà River. Once his mourning period had ended, Hánén Kïlàné, who had become the new First Instructor, immediately ordered the repeal of the policies imposed under the Worl puppet regime.
content.html: A week later, Paté’s body was found at the mouth of the Yìlà River. Once his mourning period had ended, Hánén Kïlàné, who had become the new First Instructor, immediately ordered the repeal of the policies imposed under the Worl puppet regime.

content.text: In the Sèlàn Directly Governed Parish, men were once more dressed in traditional clothing and women once more covered their skin with veils. The “freedom” that had certainly existed until yesterday was now restricted. Even so, the people were relieved. It was, after all, their own “choice.”
In the garden of Èmerán Great Hall, Sài practiced Duïlé. Under Nèkïné’s instruction, he aimed to become a policeman—one who would one day surpass his father, Lài.

The provocative advertisements of Worl capital disappeared from the streets, replaced by advertisements for Kàlavé companies. People said, “Worl is the problem,” even as bottles of Worl-made cola sat in their hands. Capital had already been planted there, and irreversibly.

Ganali took off from the Líunïshèven base for Worl. Inside the aircraft he opened the development notes for the Stance of the Moderate-Refined, written by Nèkïné. Every page was crammed with Ganali’s red-ink annotations.
“There’s no need to read this anymore.”
He put the notebook away and looked out over the nightscape of Kàlavé through the window. There below shone a light he himself had lit.
On the near side of “the end,” on the bank of the Hlïmélé River—the river of the farther shore.
“It’s all right. I’ll stay with you until your mother and father come.”

Ládén a Mono was minding the children as they stacked little towers of stones. Hlï-Yíl-Tàu wills that no child should go before their parents. The loneliness of those left behind is, by rights, a burden children ought to bear for their parents—not parents for their children.
content.html: Ládén a Mono was minding the children as they stacked little towers of stones. Hlï-Yíl-Tàu wills that no child should go before their parents. The loneliness of those left behind is, by rights, a burden children ought to bear for their parents—not parents for their children.

content.text: “Final warning. Return to the world of the living at once. If you do not, you will be judged under the law of Ismizlark.”
content.html: “Final warning. Return to the world of the living at once. If you do not, you will be judged under the law of Ismizlark.”

content.text: Bearing a message from the Three-Tailed Fox, Lakán Sài came across the bridge from the opposite bank. After death, he had been entrusted with the great office of heaven’s gatekeeper, a martyr for the future of the Instructional Nation.
content.html: Bearing a message from the Three-Tailed Fox, Lakán Sài came across the bridge from the opposite bank. After death, he had been entrusted with the great office of heaven’s gatekeeper, a martyr for the future of the Instructional Nation.

content.text: “So you were worried about me. Thank you.”
But Mono did not waver. Even if it meant defying the god himself, she would not leave the children alone.
“No. I am speaking of His Highness Tané. Have you no wish to answer his love?”
Sài stooped, picked up a flat stone, and sent it skimming across the water. The children tried to copy him, but their stones would not fly.
“Tané has Akau-chan. I don’t want to steal her happiness away.”
The tower of stones Mono had built collapsed. The children paid it no mind and began piling up a new one.
“I say this not out of personal feeling, but as a guardian of the law. What sort of happiness can a ‘murderer’ claim?”
At that, anger blazed in Mono’s face.
“What could you possibly know about Akau-chan? About the pain of being raised as a boy? About a love left unfulfilled all that time?”
Mono seized Sài by the front of his clothes. Silence fell over the riverbank.
Then a little girl came running up.
“I did it! I made it skip!”
At once Mono straightened, then patted the girl on the head.
“That,” Sài said, watching the children go on playing by themselves, “is exactly what I meant.
“No one can measure another person’s happiness. The happiness of twisting justice for the sake of love—that is something I do not understand.”
He picked up another stone and cast it over the river.
“But I know my own happiness. A life martyred to justice was a happy one. What, then, is your happiness?”
Mono copied him and threw a stone of her own.
“I want a home party. I want to eat cheese with Tané, and Akau-chan, and Karei-kun. That’s my happiness.”
The smile returned to Mono’s face. Relief softened Sài’s as well.
The children gathered to see Mono off.
They gave her a clover hair ornament they had braided for her—flowers that stood, opposite the red spider lily, for the happiness of this world.
“Big sister, don’t come back too soon, all right!”
Mono hugged them tight.
“Please tell Lady Akau this: I am proud that the heir to Pure White was you, and not the son of Lakán Lài.”
content.html: “Please tell Lady Akau this: I am proud that the heir to Pure White was you, and not the son of Lakán Lài.”

content.text: Mono took Sài’s hand.
In January 1993, in the special ward of Hāké Central Hospital.
content.html: In January 1993, in the special ward of Hāké Central Hospital.

content.text: The hand holding Mono’s was Tané’s.
“…Mono!?”
The instant he saw that she had awakened, Tané told Akau to hit the nurse call.
“…Tané? You’re still handsome as ever.”
At her very first words, tears spilled from Tané’s eyes. She was the first woman who had ever allowed him to let others see him cry.
“I’ve grown older, haven’t I? I did my best—for your sake.”
Beyond the hospital window lay the night view of Hāké, the city Tané had built.
The next day, after the examinations were over, Mono asked that a “Festival of Mono’s Return” be held in her room.
“So you really did ban alcohol? Even if I told you I wanted a drink, you wouldn’t change the law for me?”
She was not yet able to eat, and still she joked.
“We could always redefine it,” Tané said. “In one country, I hear that if the proof is low enough, it no longer counts as alcohol.”
It was rare for so rigid a man to say something so loose.
“Tané, if you keep saying things injurious to the national interest, I shall put you to sleep here in Lady Mono’s place.”
Akau rebuked him at once. But she too was celebrating Mono’s return from the bottom of her heart.

“Putting that aside, Mono-kun, do you know oganesson—the superheavy element at the far edge of the periodic table? Atomic number 118. Its chemical properties are already almost beyond prediction, and it exists for mere milliseconds! But the fact that it was there matters! It proves humanity forced the atomic nucleus to yield!”
Karei’s love of nuclear science, as ever, had run away with him.
“That’s right,” Mono said after turning his words over in her mind. “What matters is that we met, even if it wasn’t forever.”
That she had given him such an unexpectedly sensible answer delighted Karei beyond measure.
The party drew toward its close almost at once. Everyone left early out of concern for Mono’s strength. As the room emptied, Mono called to Akau.
“Akau-chan. Let me ask you bluntly—aren’t you afraid I might take Tané from you?”
Akau lowered her eyes a little and laid a hand over her chest.
“Tané’s happiness and Lady Mono’s happiness matter more to me than anything. But if you ask whether I feel no fear at all, that would be a lie.”
Relief crossed Mono’s face.
“I won’t give Tané up. But I can’t love a Tané whom Akau-chan does not love.”
Akau drove her pure-white knife into the earth.
“Before Hlï-Yíl-Tàu, I swear it. I too will make no compromise at all with my love.”
Mono urged the kneeling Akau to raise her head.
“In that case, let us sometimes talk things out, and sometimes choose. For the next hundred years, and the hundred after that. ’Ï’E’A Géjéné Kuējebeyï.”
In April 1994, at the Tïràun family residence in Flinner Parish, the gentle light of spring fell across the forbidden precincts of the Nine Clans as one man strode through them in broad daylight.
content.html: In April 1994, at the Tïràun family residence in Flinner Parish, the gentle light of spring fell across the forbidden precincts of the Nine Clans as one man strode through them in broad daylight.

content.text: The news of hibernation in 1992 had shaken the Conference to its core. Deprived of the unity they had once maintained under the name of Ládén Tané, the Nine Clans had split into three great factions.
content.html: The news of hibernation in 1992 had shaken the Conference to its core. Deprived of the unity they had once maintained under the name of Ládén Tané, the Nine Clans had split into three great factions.

content.text: One was the Ládén house, which backed Shàun Akau as Tané’s rightful successor.
content.html: One was the Ládén house, which backed Shàun Akau as Tané’s rightful successor.

content.text: Another was the Shàun house, the very house that had cast Akau out.
And the third was the Tïràun house, hereditary enemy of the Shàun.
“Heir of the Nálén house, Sālé-kun. All you need do is stand there. It is our task to secure what lies behind the scenes.”
An elder of the Tïràun family handed a briefcase of “reward money” to the permed young man, Nálén Sālé.
content.html: An elder of the Tïràun family handed a briefcase of “reward money” to the permed young man, Nálén Sālé.

content.text: Sālé opened the case, and banknotes fluttered to the ground in a soft clatter.
“This earth is my wallet,” he said. “And it belongs to the whole nation.”
Leaving the speechless elder behind, he walked out of the mansion.
That afternoon, in a conference room at the Gàtïna Grand Auditorium in Ima Parish.

As the crowning work of his reign, Ládén Tané had begun expanding the Lurpe Agreement. The two wings of that effort were Shàun Akau, newly elevated to Prime Minister, and Nálén Sālé, Vice Minister of Commerce.
“Now then,” said Tané, “let me hear your policy proposal again.”
At that instruction, Sālé distributed his papers without the slightest delay.
“I shall begin with the conclusion. My aim is to create the wallet that will remain after the oil runs out, so that Lady Mono’s happiness—and the people’s happiness now—may be protected forever.”
Tané let out a low sound. Akau watched Sālé with a slightly cool gaze.

“First: the promotion of data centers and the export of computing resources. Rather than selling the energy of the Yìlà coastal gas fields as raw fuel, it is better to sell it as processing power. A ‘quiet industry,’ one that produces no factory noise, suits the beautiful Kàlavé Your Highness envisions.”
content.html: “First: the promotion of data centers and the export of computing resources. Rather than selling the energy of the Yìlà coastal gas fields as raw fuel, it is better to sell it as processing power. A ‘quiet industry,’ one that produces no factory noise, suits the beautiful Kàlavé Your Highness envisions.”

content.text: It was at once a highly rational and a highly aesthetic proposal: a departure from the slash-and-burn economy of selling off raw resources piece by piece, and at the same time a way of banishing the smoke of heavy industry that marred the country’s stillness. Sālé continued without so much as a catch in his voice.
“The same is true of hydrogen, ammonia, and the chemical industries.”
“We should also make full use of the gas fields’ by-products and increase the revenue base for welfare spending. All of it is for the people whom Lady Mono loved.”
“...A quiet industry, hm. Not bad.”

Tané leaned deeply back in his chair and let out a short breath. In his mind he saw a people offering up their prayers in silence, their faces unstained by soot and smoke. If that vision could be preserved, there was no reason to reject the means, whether digital or chemical.
“Next, in order to maximize the value of Your Highness’s great achievement, Tané Triport, let us concentrate logistics and processing bases around it.”
content.html: “Next, in order to maximize the value of Your Highness’s great achievement, Tané Triport, let us concentrate logistics and processing bases around it.”

content.text: Akau did not move, her eyes still lowered to the papers in her hand. Concentration of logistics—the phrase was a lovely one, but it was also a drastic medicine: it would stop the blood from reaching the provinces and let only the heart grow bloated. Yet for all that, efficiency gave her no opening for an immediate rebuttal.

“Next comes the institutionalization of rent—that is, oil income—and the reorganization of the sovereign wealth fund. Oil revenue is as fickle as water. If Your Highness’s benevolence is to reach children a hundred years from now, then we must establish an automatic savings rule so that politicians cannot spend it on a whim.”

Sālé went on to recommend strengthening the authority of the National Statistics Bureau. To deliver mercy, one had first to know who was hungry. Rescue without an accurate map, he argued with a logic both fervent and cold, was no more than self-satisfaction.
“If we do not know exactly where the poor are, Your Highness’s salvation will never reach them. We need correct numbers.”
“...Quite right.”

Tané gave a small but forceful nod. To hear voices that had no sound—that was the duty of a ruler. To make invisible poverty visible: the proposal struck directly at Tané’s earnest desire to save every one of his people.
“We cannot tolerate bloated public corporations that lounge on vested interests and devour the budget meant for Lady Mono. Unprofitable projects should be liquidated.”
(He’s good.)

Akau clicked her tongue inwardly. Speak of breaking vested interests, and a man as upright as Tané had no choice but to agree. But would that blade cut away only fat? Or would it also cut through traditions, and through the ordinary lives of people whose worth never appeared in numbers? Akau’s instincts had begun to sound the alarm.

“And finally, the industrial development of water resources—mega-dams and canals. Draw water into the desert, and it becomes paradise. Let us offer the miracle of a green earth to Your Highness’s reign.”

The future Sālé described was a dazzling paradise indeed. “Facility horticulture.” “Chemical crop rotation.” It was a gospel that would turn backward peasants, caked in mud and sweat and forever at the mercy of the weather, into modern workers in clean clothing, guaranteed stable harvests.
“Let us transform the brutal labor of farming in the mud into something as clean and easy as factory work, through vinyl greenhouses and chemical fertilizer.”
“...But that...”

A deep furrow appeared between Tané’s brows. Ease was a good thing. And yet, was “agriculture” severed from the soil and stripped of the seasons truly the happiness these people desired? The smiles Tané knew among his people had smelled more of earth and sun than that.
“We will stabilize the lives of farmers, now at the mercy of the weather, by turning them into salaried employees whose wages are guaranteed by the state.”

Akau bit her lip before she knew she was doing it. In that promise of “stability,” she thought she could hear the sound of “home” being sold away. Once turned into employees, the farmers would no longer be keepers of the land. They would become no more than labor. The warm tables and places of return Tané had wanted to protect would be replaced by an inorganic system, and the chill of that vision darkened Akau’s eyes like a winter sky.
When the meeting ended, Tané hurried out of the room to deliver a different address.
“When all is said and done,” Akau asked, “what is it you actually want?”
Sālé met her gaze without flinching.
“To dismantle a state that does nothing but distribute largesse while leaning on resources, and prepare its conversion into a state that earns—one that compels discipline and productivity.”
“And I’m to take that as a repudiation of His Highness the First Instructor’s reign?”
content.html: “And I’m to take that as a repudiation of His Highness the First Instructor’s reign?”

content.text: Akau’s hand went to her breast.
“If His Highness Tané is a winter fire, then I am the sun of spring.”
Sālé too drew his knife.
“That stance... I’ve never seen it before.”
“‘The Stance of Revolving Flame.’ My grandmother used to say, ‘Cut away anger, desire, and deceit; endure hardship, and in time you will attain this stance.’”
content.html: “‘The Stance of Revolving Flame.’ My grandmother used to say, ‘Cut away anger, desire, and deceit; endure hardship, and in time you will attain this stance.’”

content.text: Akau showed the Stance of Savage Fury. In the next instant, her pure-white knife was already at Sālé’s throat.
But Sālé handled the onslaught with astonishing skill.

The Stance of Revolving Flame—Shade: Sālé’s learning state. It endured every attack, doing nothing but study the opponent’s tendencies. Unlike the Stance of Gentle Harmony, it could not answer with a counter.
(So it’s a defense-specialized counterpart to the Stance of Gentle Harmony. But those openings—there’s something eerie about them.)
Akau struck his shins, then both arms, with the back of her blade—textbook modern Duïlé disarming technique.
“It’s over!”
Her pure-white knife lunged for Sālé’s throat.
...And Sālé’s blade slipped between them. He had read the attack ahead of time.
“One minute, fifty-eight point twenty-five seconds. That’s the time you have left to shine.”
Then Sālé counterattacked.

The Stance of Revolving Flame—Sun: Sālé’s released state. It bewildered the opponent with overwhelming speed. It came with a time limit, one that lengthened in proportion to the duration of the learning state. Once the limit expired, it required a cooldown—complete inability to fight.
(Too fast... He’s the equal of Satella Long. Perhaps even more than that.)
content.html: (Too fast... He’s the equal of Satella Long. Perhaps even more than that.)

content.text: As she fended off his furious assault, Akau gradually lost hold of her own rhythm.
But Akau too was a veteran of countless battles. She shifted into the true Stance of the Moderate.
“There!”
Within the storm she found a single instant of calm and set her blade there. For the first time, Sālé was caught off guard.
“‘The true Stance of the Moderate,’ is it? But I am the Child of Destiny for the twenty-first century. I go beyond even that.”
content.html: “‘The true Stance of the Moderate,’ is it? But I am the Child of Destiny for the twenty-first century. I go beyond even that.”

content.text: Sālé readied himself for the decisive exchange.
“How arrogant can one man be?”
Akau gripped her knife more tightly.
“Life is more fun when you think that way.”
Sālé smiled, but Akau’s blade went on honestly for his throat.

Heaven Overcomes, Earth Surges—the secret art of the Stance of Revolving Flame, releasing a slash at maximum output at the exact instant the time limit expires. Even a fraction too early, and the backlash wounds the user as well. Even a fraction too late, and it fails entirely.
At one minute, fifty-eight point twenty-four seconds, it was Sālé who collapsed first. But Akau too was left battered from head to toe.
“Finished posturing?”
Akau held out her hand.
“Everyone wants Kàlavé to become an adult someday. That someday has already arrived. It’s just...”
Sālé took her hand.
“If I am the sun, then you are the moon. Together, let us shine on those people who plant their feet on the earth and labor with all they have.”
“What’s that even supposed to mean?”

Ládén Tané had eight years and four months left in his term as First Instructor. Many in Kàlavé still supported Tané’s “rich winter,” or else Akau as his rightful successor. But Kàlavé had no universal suffrage. The key lay in the Conference’s recommendation—and above all, in a single word from the Nine Clans. Who would become the next First Instructor? As always, all of it rested in the will of Hlï-Yíl-Tàu.

In June 1999, in the Sósos district of Hellén Directly Governed City in the Har-Lopinong Republic, the sounds rang out once again of Mancal—left-wing student self-governing groups—wielding clubs and fighting Five Heavens over turf. Satella sat alone in a café, wrapped in the long twilight of her thoughts. Once she had been called a witch. Now, expelled from Guĕnar, she spent her days wasting away in her hometown. When she was a child, her parents had been murdered in a robbery. The culprit had been their neighbor, who envied the family for prospering and rising in the world. Satella had concluded from that that happiness required leveling. Recruited at university, she entered Guĕnar. But Kàlavé’s “uneven happiness” had functioned all too well. Her great cause had collapsed.
content.html: In June 1999, in the Sósos district of Hellén Directly Governed City in the Har-Lopinong Republic, the sounds rang out once again of Mancal—left-wing student self-governing groups—wielding clubs and fighting Five Heavens over turf. Satella sat alone in a café, wrapped in the long twilight of her thoughts. Once she had been called a witch. Now, expelled from Guĕnar, she spent her days wasting away in her hometown. When she was a child, her parents had been murdered in a robbery. The culprit had been their neighbor, who envied the family for prospering and rising in the world. Satella had concluded from that that happiness required leveling. Recruited at university, she entered Guĕnar. But Kàlavé’s “uneven happiness” had functioned all too well. Her great cause had collapsed.

content.text: “Mind if I sit here?”
A woman with scarlet eyes spoke to her. It seemed every other seat was full. The woman unfolded a newspaper.
“Latest front in the Deltai Civil War: Bombing of the capital Tenkanand imminent.”
content.html: “Latest front in the Deltai Civil War: Bombing of the capital Tenkanand imminent.”

content.text: The Deltai Civil War. After falling into economic crisis in 1970, Deltai had now become a battleground for the proxy wars of surrounding states. The country’s lawlessness had also helped underground organizations such as Five Heavens and Nine Earths expand. Here too was an unhappy country—at least, that was how Satella of old would have described it.
content.html: The Deltai Civil War. After falling into economic crisis in 1970, Deltai had now become a battleground for the proxy wars of surrounding states. The country’s lawlessness had also helped underground organizations such as Five Heavens and Nine Earths expand. Here too was an unhappy country—at least, that was how Satella of old would have described it.

content.text: “What is happiness, I wonder?”
The words slipped from Satella before she knew it. She had meant them as a private murmur, but the scarlet-eyed woman answered.
“It’s that instant when you brush against the unknown and the emptiness falls silent for just a heartbeat.”
The woman poured sugar into her coffee. How many packets had she already used? Empty sugar sticks lay scattered all over the table.
Satella finished her black coffee, bowed lightly to the woman, and left her seat. A red spider lily badge hung from her bag.

Half a day later, the headquarters of Skiĕl, a front company of Five Heavens, was under tight guard by the organization’s members. Today was the day of the Branzi Dool meeting—the gathering of Five Heavens’ senior officers.
“So in the end only two of us made it? Maybe the location was bad. I heard the railways were suspended because of the snowfall.”

The man glancing back and forth between meeting papers and a railway timetable was Killord Dubi Selestia, Yellow Heaven. He was the eighth legitimate son of the Selestia family, one of Deltai’s great clans, but by the custom imposed on last-born sons he had been made a eunuch from birth. Within Five Heavens he managed the organization’s contested territories and served as its mediator.
content.html: The man glancing back and forth between meeting papers and a railway timetable was Killord Dubi Selestia, Yellow Heaven. He was the eighth legitimate son of the Selestia family, one of Deltai’s great clans, but by the custom imposed on last-born sons he had been made a eunuch from birth. Within Five Heavens he managed the organization’s contested territories and served as its mediator.

content.text: “Honestly, it was so cold I almost didn’t bother coming! Work in the ‘homeland’ is going wonderfully, and this is what I get for gracing you with my presence. May I go home now?”

The woman pouring hot tea for herself alone was Arsha Pritivitiĕ, Violet Heaven. Publicly she was known as the CEO of Labaquĕda, a Worl synthetic-fiber maker; privately, she oversaw the Worl parish and served as one of the organization’s main earners.
content.html: The woman pouring hot tea for herself alone was Arsha Pritivitiĕ, Violet Heaven. Publicly she was known as the CEO of Labaquĕda, a Worl synthetic-fiber maker; privately, she oversaw the Worl parish and served as one of the organization’s main earners.

content.text: “No. His Eminence is expected today as well. Whether they like it or not, everyone will attend.”
Killord picked up the teapot and poured tea into his own cup too. Two empty cups still remained on the table.
“His Eminence?”
The door burst inward. For a split second the two of them sensed murderous intent and drew their guns. A woman wrapped in bandages peered in through the ruined doorway.
“Oh. So it’s that sort of ‘His Eminence.’ And here I thought you meant my darling husband.”

The woman, clutching a bag plastered with photographs of Ládén Tané, sat down in visible disappointment. She was Koto Tál Aitēl, Blue Heaven—the former second wife of the First Instructor Tané and one of Five Heavens’ foremost field operatives, entrusted with West Altos.
content.html: The woman, clutching a bag plastered with photographs of Ládén Tané, sat down in visible disappointment. She was Koto Tál Aitēl, Blue Heaven—the former second wife of the First Instructor Tané and one of Five Heavens’ foremost field operatives, entrusted with West Altos.

content.text: “Well then. Since our astonishing third guest has arrived, let’s begin the meeting.”

Killord took charge of proceedings. The pressing concerns were how to deal with Nine Earths, which was pressing hard against Five Heavens’ sphere of influence, and what was to be done about Green Heaven.
“The power vacuum in Meldina is now decisive. We have to appoint the next Green Heaven at once and stop that bastard from running wild.”
As he spoke, the scars of four gunshots remained visible across Killord’s abdomen—wounds left there by that very “bastard,” the previous Green Heaven.
“And this mug has my beloved husband printed right on it. If you pour tea into it, my husband drowns, you see!”
Ignoring Killord entirely, Koto showed Arsha her latest goods featuring her “favorite.”
“This design would sell with other characters too, wouldn’t it? I can smell money.”
Arsha ignored Killord just as thoroughly and did her own accounting instead. Anything beyond her own interests and those of her district failed to stir her in the least.
“Will you two listen?”
Killord shot back the obvious retort.
“If killing that woman means I might get to see my darling husband again, then by all means, let’s go play with Nine Earths.”
Koto answered at last, and only grudgingly. “That woman,” needless to say, meant Akau.
“But there’s also the problem of him. We have to consider conserving our forces as well.”
Killord tried to hold the line.
“Oh, spare me. We can do both. Meeting over. That’s all. Everyone out.”
Killord grimaced. Koto resumed worshipping her “husband.” Arsha began checking stock prices on her phone. The discussion seemed fated to run forever in parallel lines.
“Your Highness.”
At that voice, every spine in the room went rigid.
The scarlet-eyed woman climbed the stairs, one step, then another.
“As Count Aitēl says, we will devote ourselves to dealing with Nine Earths.”

Scarlet Heaven—or Latia the Hollow God—Arka “Latia” Melduĕk. She was the ruler of Five Heavens, the one who had transformed the organization into a radical religious criminal syndicate, and the person Killord and the others revered as His Eminence.
“Countess, explain the current situation with Nine Earths once more.”
Latia addressed the question to Koto. Even Koto’s usual madness receded in the presence of Scarlet Heaven.

“In 1992, the Kàlavé National Police Agency expelled the far-right elements from Nine Earths. Now those same elements, as the Nine Earths Apàla faction, have begun invading Deltai on their own initiative.”
content.html: “In 1992, the Kàlavé National Police Agency expelled the far-right elements from Nine Earths. Now those same elements, as the Nine Earths Apàla faction, have begun invading Deltai on their own initiative.”

content.text: After Akau’s downfall, the Apàla faction—centered on disciples of the now-dead Apàla—had broken away from Kàlavé government control and invaded the Five Heavens sphere in Deltai on its own. The Kàlavé government denied involvement, but since it wanted Five Heavens destroyed in principle, it had been content to watch the Apàla faction’s movements.
“And how many troops can Nine Earths spare for this?”
Latia asked Arsha.
“Five thousand regular members, and somewhere between ten and fifteen thousand miscellaneous troops.”

Deltai, unfortunately, was badly placed. It was hemmed in by authoritarian states such as Eltēna and Kàlavé, as well as the socialist state Durguatan, making it difficult to move forces in from outside.
content.html: Deltai, unfortunately, was badly placed. It was hemmed in by authoritarian states such as Eltēna and Kàlavé, as well as the socialist state Durguatan, making it difficult to move forces in from outside.

content.text: “Your Highness, that is the situation. As for Green Heaven, I suppose we wait and see how matters develop.”
The person Latia called “Your Highness” was Killord.
“Can’t be helped, my lord. Then we’ll postpone choosing the next Green Heaven and address Nine Earths first.”
At long last the discussion had begun to move—when an explosion tore through the building and a man in women’s clothing burst in with a gun in each hand.
“Yep, dead♡ Total small fry all the way in here, but what’s this, Arka-chan? Are you small fry too?”
Green Heaven—Kybi Árin. Leader of Kybi Frauzet, the farthest-right faction within Five Heavens, and the traitor who openly sought to usurp Scarlet Heaven’s seat from Latia.
content.html: Green Heaven—Kybi Árin. Leader of Kybi Frauzet, the farthest-right faction within Five Heavens, and the traitor who openly sought to usurp Scarlet Heaven’s seat from Latia.

content.text: “You smell like blood too. You’ve ‘loved’ a great many people, haven’t you?”
Koto instantly closed to knife range. Blind though she was, she alone could read the murderous intent in the air, and she slashed straight for it.
“Eirin-chan just killed a lot of annoying people, that’s all♡ Totally different from you, old lady~!”

Kybi kicked at the arm holding Koto’s knife, knocking it aside. She sprang backward and fired. Koto dodged one shot, but the next took her in the leg. She was weak at long range—that much had already been made plain in her fight with Satella.
“Then how about this?”
Making full use of her wrist’s range of motion, Koto flicked her knife with the smallest possible movement and knocked one of Kybi’s pistols from her hand.
“Oh, come on, you are seriously persistent!”
Kybi’s bullets, sharp with anger, flew at Koto.
“Police!”
The Har-Lopinong police burst into the room.
But no one was there anymore.
One owl slipped in among the Har-Lopinong police: Shàun Akau. He stooped and picked up Koto’s knife.
content.html: One owl slipped in among the Har-Lopinong police: Shàun Akau. He stooped and picked up Koto’s knife.

content.text: “So the ‘settling of things’ still wasn’t over.”
Latia swept out of the Skiĕl building with effortless grace. No one in the world would ever guess that she was Scarlet Heaven.
“Happiness, hm.”
A fearless smile touched her lips.

What if Koto had never left Tané?
What if Satella had never betrayed Karei?
What if Sài had not been killed by Akau?
Let us grant all of it.
The price is the world’s ending—the Circle’s End.

June 6, 1999.
The Paté regime did not, in the end, fall.
Appearing as though to oppose the Second Instructional Nation, which had itself become Biarei, came Pājén・■ (Doa).
Leading the Instructional Guard Corps, he proclaimed the founding of the Final Instructional Nation of Kàlavé and the opening of the last war.
content.html: June 6, 1999. The Paté regime did not, in the end, fall. Appearing as though to oppose the Second Instructional Nation, which had itself become Biarei, came Pājén・■ (Doa). Leading the Instructional Guard Corps, he proclaimed the founding of the Final Instructional Nation of Kàlavé and the opening of the last war.

content.text:
Eucomis. Its flower language is perfection.
Will summer ever come to Kàlavé?
Beyond “the end,” at heaven’s gate.
“We’ll need to confirm your personal information. Even a hero doesn’t get through on face alone, Bïláin Àkané.”
content.html: “We’ll need to confirm your personal information. Even a hero doesn’t get through on face alone, Bïláin Àkané.”

content.text: Lakán Sài handled the paperwork with practiced hands. Today, the “real” Bïláin Àkané had finally fulfilled his allotted fate.
content.html: Lakán Sài handled the paperwork with practiced hands. Today, the “real” Bïláin Àkané had finally fulfilled his allotted fate.

content.text: “So my cause of death was choking on food after aspiration? Sèlàn must be howling with laughter.”
Àkané, in the form of his younger days, waited for permission to pass.
“It is not a bad thing for one’s end to be ridiculous.”
Still turning over the documents, Sài humored Àkané’s idle talk.
“Do you know what it was that got stuck? Another name for it is eucomis. Its flower language is ‘perfection.’ Quite a joke, isn’t it?”
When authorization arrived from above, Sài opened the gate.
“Pineapple, most likely. From now on, it seems, you are under instructions to chew your food properly.”
Sài saw Àkané off.
“Tell me, Sài. Was there anything left undone for you? I’ve wondered about that for a long time.”
Àkané lowered his eyes a little.
“It would take too long to explain. Ask Aya over there for the details. But...”
Sài looked toward the opposite bank.
“I wanted to know what it felt like when everybody laughed together.”
...June 6, 1999—if only. At the Ládén family residence in Flinner Parish.
“This is the six o’clock news. Breaking news: the Supreme Court of the Worl Federation has declared the seizure of major infrastructure assets owned by the Instructional Nation government.”
content.html: “This is the six o’clock news. Breaking news: the Supreme Court of the Worl Federation">Worl Federation has declared the seizure of major infrastructure assets owned by the Instructional Nation government.”

content.text: In this timeline, Tïràun Paté had kept his grip on the Second Instructional Nation of Kàlavé, and its transformation into a puppet of the Worl Federation had known no limit. The Paté government had gone on drawing vast foreign loans from Worl by putting up the profits of the Yìlà coastal gas fields as collateral. For a regime that depended on natural gas for most of its national budget, the fall in prices meant not a mere recession but cardiac arrest for the state itself.
content.html: In this timeline, Tïràun Paté had kept his grip on the Second Instructional Nation of Kàlavé, and its transformation into a puppet of the Worl Federation had known no limit. The Paté government had gone on drawing vast foreign loans from Worl by putting up the profits of the Yìlà coastal gas fields as collateral. For a regime that depended on natural gas for most of its national budget, the fall in prices meant not a mere recession but cardiac arrest for the state itself.

content.text: The loan contract Paté had signed in desperation left no room for doubt: in the event of default, the registered ownership of strategic points including Líunïshèven Base, along with the entire electrical grid, would be transferred to the Worl Federal Investment Bank.
“What utter idiocy! If my husband had been the First Instructor, none of this would ever have happened!”
content.html: “What utter idiocy! If my husband had been the First Instructor, none of this would ever have happened!”

content.text: Koto Tál Aitēl snapped the channel over on the television.
content.html: Koto Tál Aitēl snapped the channel over on the television.

content.text: 《One: in this timeline, Koto never became involved with Five Heavens. She lived as Ládén Tané’s second wife, her violent love unchanged.》
content.html: 《One: in this timeline, Koto never became involved with Five Heavens. She lived as Ládén Tané’s second wife, her violent love unchanged.》

content.text: On another station, the news was reporting a series of earthquakes rippling through the Xāváléqï Corridor.
content.html: On another station, the news was reporting a series of earthquakes rippling through the Xāváléqï Corridor">Xāváléqï Corridor.

content.text: “Hlï-Yíl-Tàu is angry. We will strengthen our stockpiles in preparation for whatever may come.”
In this timeline, Shàun Akau lived as Tané’s secretary. Needless to say, only members of the Ládén house knew she was a woman.
content.html: In this timeline, Shàun Akau lived as Tané’s secretary. Needless to say, only members of the Ládén house knew she was a woman.

content.text: “Your thoughtfulness is welcome, but as long as the people are supplied as well, we can perfectly well go without.”
In this timeline, Ládén Tané was not the First Instructor, but he had lost none of the noblesse oblige expected of one of the Nine Clans.
content.html: In this timeline, Ládén Tané was not the First Instructor, but he had lost none of the noblesse oblige expected of one of the Nine Clans.

content.text: “Come on, everyone, it’s time to eat. Empty stomachs make... what was it again?”
Ládén a Mono invited laughter. Her body was as frail as ever, but her heart was stronger than anyone’s there.
content.html: Ládén a Mono invited laughter. Her body was as frail as ever, but her heart was stronger than anyone’s there.

content.text: “‘You die,’ of course! Why are you forgetting something so obvious?”
The whole room burst out laughing at Koto’s answer. Thus began a happiness too complete to exist in official history.
At nine o’clock, a magnitude-6.0 earthquake ran through the earth. Soon afterward a voice sounded directly inside people’s heads.
“I am the child of the hunter Pàlé, Pājén・■(unintelligible). This is the beginning of the Circle’s End. People who have bowed before Biarei, return the earth to me at once.”
At the Gàtïna Grand Auditorium in Ima Parish, First Instructor Tïràun Paté sat in exhaustion.
“That bastard Ganali’s put something weird in my head again! Agaké! Bring Ganali here!”
But Tággén Agaké, the Shadow Owl of Nine Earths, did not so much as twitch.
content.html: But Tággén Agaké, the Shadow Owl of Nine Earths, did not so much as twitch.

content.text: “My apologies, former First Instructor. From this moment on, Nine Earths reveres Lord Doa as First Instructor. With your death, we hereby proclaim the founding of the Final Instructional Nation of Kàlavé.”
Agaké drew his knife. The Stance of the Void Mind gave Paté no chance to flee. It smashed both his legs and brought the blade’s point to his throat—
—and then a gunshot grazed Agaké’s cheek.
“Worth staking out Mr. Nèkïné without permission after all.”
Lakán Sài stepped into the office.
《One: in this timeline, Sài was assigned to the Public Security Bureau of the National Police Agency without ever confronting Akau.》
content.html: 《One: in this timeline, Sài was assigned to the Public Security Bureau of the National Police Agency without ever confronting Akau.》

content.text: “A mere secular policeman means to obstruct Lord Doa’s sacred execution of the Circle’s End?”
Agaké and Sài closed into knife range.

Any police officer in Kàlavé could use the Stance of the Moderate-Refined. But Sài had learned that technique alone was not enough to keep a man alive. So here, deliberately, he showed the Stance of Rigid Integrity instead.
“This is still, at least on paper, a state governed by law!”
The Stance of the Void Mind excelled at reading an opponent’s feelings, but Sài’s cold offensive was hard to read.
“Agaké. Enough.”
At that single word from ■, Agaké’s presence vanished.
A communication came in from one of Sài’s subordinates. He slapped the fainted Paté awake and reported the situation.
“Your Highness Paté. Nine Earths, under the name of the Instructional Guard Corps, is seizing the major cities one by one. We must respond at once with the national army.”
Paté agreed without hesitation to mobilization. Thus the curtain rose on war between the Second Instructional Nation and the Final Instructional Nation.
At the same hour, at the fast breeder reactor Lafang in Ganei Parish, Niviginasei Province.
Majén Karei, director of the plant, stood in its central control room.
“All control rods fully inserted—scram confirmed! Pony motor started according to sequence!”
While the operators shouted, Karei resisted the shaking and gazed at the monitors with almost tender affection.
“Good. The hot shielding of our carbon friends held, and the sturdy barriers of our iron friend held too. Excellent. You really are reliable.”
The reactor had shut down. Then the staff saw something that froze them where they stood. On the feed from the spent fuel pool, a boy with three tails was visible.
“Majén Karei. I appoint you Second Instructor. In return, surrender this toy to me.”
In the next instant the surroundings became a vacant white expanse. Everything that had been there was erased, space and all.
“A ‘fast breeder reactor,’ is it? I forgive the presumption of challenging Hlï-Yíl-Tàu. I shall refashion this into the Spear of the Earth. Lend me your knowledge.”
What the Spear of the Earth meant was easy enough for Karei to guess.
“No! Lafang is the people’s hope! I won’t let it be turned into a weapon!”

Karei’s refusal drew ■’s wrath. ■ drew a fox-colored knife. The sacred light running along its edge was of a wholly different order from the blade secular First Instructors had passed down through the years.
Then a station wagon came screeching in.
“Karei, get in!”
A blonde woman leaned out from the driver’s seat. Karei hurried into the car.
Satella Long drove at reckless speed.
content.html: Satella Long drove at reckless speed.

content.text: 《One: in this timeline, Satella never became a Witch. She stayed in Kàlavé as Karei’s girlfriend.》
The rest of the Ládén household was in the vehicle as well.
“Karei! I was worried. You’re not hurt?”
Tané spoke first the moment he saw him. Fortunately, Karei was uninjured.
“So that’s the Anchor people whisper about—the Doa of myth.”
Mono watched ■ hanging in the sky beyond the window. To any eye at all, it looked wrong.
“Satella, where are you planning to go?”
“South, obviously. Has becoming all muscle cost you your sense of direction as well?”
“I know it’s south!”
In the back seat, Akau and Koto were hitting each other. It was such a familiar scene that no one even bothered to comment.
“...Líunïshèven.”
Karei finally spoke. Losing Lafang had left him reeling.
“He means to wipe out Biarei—in other words, the Worl army—in one stroke. That power of his isn’t omnipotent. He can’t reach countries too far away.”
Karei went on.
“Doa appointed me Second Instructor. Which means, on the other side of the same coin, that I have the authority to remove Doa from the office of First Instructor.”
“That will be difficult.”

Tané cut in on Karei’s flicker of hope. He opened his mobile phone. A statement had already appeared in the news announcing Tággén Agaké’s appointment as Third Instructor of the Final Instructional Nation.
“The Instructors rule as a collegium. If the Third Instructor exercises his veto, the dismissal is void.”
At Tané’s words Karei’s heart sank again. Between turns of the steering wheel, Satella thumped him on the shoulder.
“Can’t we somehow talk Agaké-kun around?”
Mono, too, pulled out her phone and began looking for Agaké’s contact information.
“...Convince him? Now that’s funny.”
“What, so you’re all muscle too?”
Akau and Koto smiled with the same dangerous look.
“All right, then. Decided.”
Satella jerked the wheel. Their destination was the front line of the Instructional Guard Corps: Sèlàn Directly Governed Parish.
“““We’ll shut him up!”””
The three terrifying women spoke in one voice. Tané covered Mono’s ears and murmured, “Don’t ever turn out like that.”
Setain Parish, Sèlàn Directly Governed Parish.
The city was already under fire from both the national army and the Instructional Guard Corps.
Satella slipped through the gun smoke and brought the car to a halt before the Èmerán Great Hall. This was the center of the directly governed parish; all information in Sèlàn flowed here.
The car doors flew open, and the three women burst out as if competing with one another.
“I’m first in.”

Satella’s knife had the best opening speed. She cut down member after member of the Instructional Guard Corps. But she had a weakness as well: her attacks were swift and shallow. One of the guards went over to the counterattack.
“Don’t hold back, witch-girl!”
Akau’s Stance of Savage Fury sent the guard flying. She was slower than Satella, but stronger; just as Koto always said, she was practically all muscle and weak in any contest of reading intent.
“Oh? And you are hardly holding back yourself.”

Koto resonated her emotions with her allies and widened the effective range of her automatic counterattacks. When an attack was aimed at Akau, Koto deflected it in her stead. Satella and Akau moved as though shielding the more defensive Koto.
“Enough. I’ll handle this myself.”
At Agaké’s word, the other guards fell back. A fox-colored knife rested in his hand.
“Fear me. This is only a fragment of my power.”
■’s eyes settled inside Agaké.
“If you can possess him, why didn’t you do that from the beginning?”

Satella threw out the line lightly. Then all three women were startled. Somehow, before anyone had seen it happen, Satella was already on one knee. For that kind of speed, there was unnaturally little afterimage.
Next Koto took a blow. For a woman with automatic counterattacks to be caught by surprise should have been impossible.
“What sort of trick are you using?”
“I falsify nothing. I am merely there.”

At last Agaké charged straight at Akau. Akau hurled a great slash not toward Agaké but toward the empty space beside him. The centrifugal force of the overcommitted swing made the blade burst somewhere outside the reach of her conscious intent.
“So you govern the unconscious, don’t you? Which means the more our attacks slip outside deliberate thought, the easier they are to land.”
Akau had started checking her own answer. Perhaps because she had guessed correctly, Agaké shifted his priority and went for the already wounded Satella.
And then, for some reason, Satella attacked Koto.
Koto’s automatic counter came back at the place where Satella had just been. Satella slipped straight through it, and the blow struck Agaké, who had been pursuing her.
At last Agaké dropped to one knee. ■’s presence vanished from his eyes. It seemed that his subconscious had been under ■’s control all along.
Karei and the others ran to Agaké. Mono treated the wounds of the three women, while Tané explained the whole course of events to him.
“To dismiss him, we need his true name. Fortunately, it is engraved deep in my consciousness. I can share it here easily enough—but doing so will touch off his wrath.”
Agaké warned them.
“It’s all right. If it’s us, we’ll get through it.”
Akau, Tané, Koto, Mono, Karei, and Satella laid their hands together.

““In the name of the Second and Third Instructors, we dismiss Pājén—
— from the office of First Instructor!””
“No one shall know me!”
In the next instant Lafang deployed overhead.
The whole world went white.
A blank expanse.
Even though ■ had supposedly achieved everything, a sense of wrongness remained. Why had Hlï-Yíl-Tàu not manifested?
“Because you’re an isotope,” said a voice.
The sun blazed down upon ■.
“Accursed thing. A messenger from heaven!”
From beyond the horizon, one man came walking.
“My grandmother used to say Lafang—radishes—have nourishment in their leaves too. You too are going to become a leaf instead of a root.”
Nálén Sālé raised his knife.
content.html: Nálén Sālé raised his knife.

content.text: The Stance of Revolving Flame—Shade. Sālé’s learning state. He endured every attack and studied his opponent’s tendencies.
content.html: The Stance of Revolving Flame—Shade. Sālé’s learning state. He endured every attack and studied his opponent’s tendencies.

content.text: “I am the messenger of Hlï-Yíl-Tàu! I am! I absolutely am!”
Unconsciously, ■ carved Sālé to pieces.
But the sun did not set.
“The truth of this world is simple. It is a ‘what if’ wished for by the people of the real world.”

The Stance of Revolving Flame—Sun. Sālé’s released state. It bewildered its opponent with overwhelming speed. It carried a time limit, one that extended according to the length of the learning state. Once the limit was over, he would require a cooldown period and be unable to fight.
“One minute, thirty point fifteen seconds. That’s the time you have left to shine.”
Sālé’s counterattack began.
Attacks born of consciousness should have failed to connect. And yet Sālé steadily wounded ■.
“Why? What are you?”
“My grandmother used to say this too. I am everything. I dwell in everything I have ever been involved with.”

Heaven Overcomes, Earth Surges—the supreme art of the Stance of Revolving Flame, unleashing a slash at maximum power at the exact instant the time limit expires. Even a moment too soon, and the backlash wounds the user. Even a moment too late, and it comes to nothing.
At exactly one minute, thirty point fifteen seconds, ■ sank to one knee.
“Then if this is a false scripture, what am I to do?”
■ began to cry in little drops. Sālé handed over his handkerchief.
“So what if a false scripture is more entertaining than the canon? Laugh. That’s what you need.”
□ wiped away the tears and returned the handkerchief to Sālé.
“Nálén Sālé. You’re an interesting one.”
□—Moi—laughed, and turned time back to the moment before Lafang was dropped.
On July 1, summer came to Kàlavé.
On the beach at Narata Parish, Yìlà Directly Governed Parish.
Those same three women were now absorbed in a murderous game of beach volleyball, with Mono caught in the middle of it.
“Satella, water gun!”
“Roger!”
Satella shot Koto with a water gun, and in that opening Akau slammed home a vicious spike.
“Oh? I’ve learned to make manual attacks now as well.”
“No no no no no!”
Koto managed the toss, but Mono—who had no athletic sense at all—was giving everything she had merely to cope with it.
From a little way off, Tané, Karei, and □ watched.
“I like that woman,” said □, pointing at Akau. “She looks strongest.”
His supernatural powers were gone. All that remained in his mind was the curiosity and childishness of a fifteen-year-old.
“Akau is a good person. But she is also my precious secretary. Pick someone else.”
Tané pointed at Satella.
“No good. Satella and I are uranium and plutonium. Get the ratio wrong, and we might never have met at all. So I want to treasure that. The blue-haired lady will do instead.”
Karei pointed at Koto.
“Absolutely not. One life wouldn’t be enough. To borrow Karei’s language, the Tané of this timeline is a miracle.”
□ had, unfortunately, arrived at the truth.
“Brat. I’ll punish you later.”
The beach was filled with laughter.
At the same time, Sālé—now serving under First Instructor Paté as provisional Prime Minister, which in practice meant regent—was serving food to his friend Sài.

“A full Trini course, Le Parfait. The amuse-bouche is caramelized pineapple with feta fondant and a scent of za’atar. The appetizer is foie gras poêlé with date confit and cardamom sauce. The fish course is sea bass vapeur in harissa-style bouillabaisse. The meat course is roast lamb with a dukkah crust and red wine sauce. Dessert is pistachio parfait with rosewater jelly and orange-blossom fragrance. And after the meal—”
“All right, I get it. That’s enough.”
Sài cut him off. As he ate the pineapple from the amuse-bouche, he fell for a moment into thought.
“I never eat things this good in my ordinary life. Maybe there was a timeline where I died without ever tasting any of it.”
Sālé gave a faint, embarrassed shrug at Sài’s words, then returned to himself and offered one of his usual sayings.
“My grandmother used to say the peel of a Lafang makes fine kinpira. Every part of the earth’s bounty is exquisite.”
Sài finished the pineapple. Though they had not even reached the appetizer yet, he felt somehow full.

August 1994. Hellén, the university city of the Har-Lopinong Republic. Satella Long was living as the matron of the student dormitory Nort in the Sósos district. But it was the height of summer vacation, and every student had gone home.
content.html: August 1994. Hellén, the university city of the Har-Lopinong Republic. Satella Long was living as the matron of the student dormitory Nort in the Sósos district. But it was the height of summer vacation, and every student had gone home.
