content.text: Satella hated idle hours. They reminded her too sharply of the self she had been two years before, when she had been possessed by the idea of “happiness.”

As one of the “witches” of Guĕnar, the secret society of Har-Lopinong, she had fought to bring “happiness” to the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé—only to be defeated by people who knew what real happiness was. After she was forcibly deported back to Har-Lopinong, she received a sentence of two years in prison, suspended for four. Har-Lopinong was somewhat more developed than Kàlavé, and prided itself on rehabilitating criminals. The state had assigned her a job as a dorm matron. Naturally, it was the sort of work they gave people with baggage.

The Sósos district had been the scene of the Hellén Incident in 1991, when the Hellén mafia lost to Five Heavens and the figure who would later become Scarlet Heaven first began to rise. The town had since been steadily bought up by the organization’s front companies, and in the last few years its people had come to stroll the streets as if they owned them.

And ever since the New Left boom of the 1960s, Sósos had also been a hotbed of student activism. Har-Lopinong had always been an absurdly decentralized state, and when Mancal, the self-governing organization of Hellén’s students, demanded autonomy, the government had actually handed Hellén over to them for a time. But by the 1990s the police had lost patience with Mancal’s inability to stop the rise of Five Heavens, resumed direct rule over Hellén, and begun an armed conflict with them.

Hellén in 1994 was, in short, a lawless zone—so lawless that a country under dictatorship by law could almost seem preferable. No one wanted a job that involved students. That was why there had been a place for her.
That morning too, Satella woke to the distant clatter of riot shields striking protest batons. In the mail slot by the door lay a Mancal newspaper.
“Bring down the university authorities and restore true student autonomy!”
“Restore,” Satella scoffed. When, exactly, had the university ever belonged to them?

Beside the thick protest lettering was a photograph of the secretary-general, Ágre Yall. Ever since Satella had been a child, Ágre had been both “secretary-general” and “student.” The university still treated him as a student. It was collusion, plain and simple. Had they no pride at all as socialists? She tossed the paper aside.
content.html: Beside the thick protest lettering was a photograph of the secretary-general, Ágre Yall. Ever since Satella had been a child, Ágre had been both “secretary-general” and “student.” The university still treated him as a student. It was collusion, plain and simple. Had they no pride at all as socialists? She tossed the paper aside.

content.text: Someone knocked. When she opened the door, she found Police Officer Darc Slingar, the patrolman assigned to the local police box.
content.html: Someone knocked. When she opened the door, she found Police Officer Darc Slingar, the patrolman assigned to the local police box.

content.text: “Morning, Sister Satella. Glad to see you’re looking lively today.”
Every day, the Har-Lopinong police came to “check on” her. Satella answered him curtly.
“This is my weekly report. Since it’s summer vacation, every day looks the same. That sameness is what you people want, isn’t it?”
She handed over the report. She was, after all, a model prisoner.
“No,” Darc said. “The world is changing with an audible roar. Five Heavens is unifying the Northern Hemisphere. The Lurpe Pact is expanding. Change is a wonderful thing.”

He was in an unusually good mood for that hour of the morning. Beneath his words she caught the faint trace of a Worl accent. He probably was not from Har-Lopinong. Worse, he was carefully hiding that fact. Satella had once belonged to Guĕnar; she knew danger when she saw it.
content.html: He was in an unusually good mood for that hour of the morning. Beneath his words she caught the faint trace of a Worl accent. He probably was not from Har-Lopinong. Worse, he was carefully hiding that fact. Satella had once belonged to Guĕnar; she knew danger when she saw it.

content.text: Once she had sent Officer Darc away, she changed and got ready to go out. Today was the day of her regular appointment. Leaving Nort behind, she headed for Sósos West Clinic. Through a quiet residential neighborhood she walked, until at last she reached the shabby mixed-use building where the clinic was housed.
“A pity,” said the doctor when Satella entered. “Physically, you look perfectly healthy.”

Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei, like Satella, was a second-generation Solan immigrant. In 1936, when a socialist government was established in Fengle, great waves of Solan immigrants had poured into Har-Lopinong, the imperial metropole. Now nearly thirty percent of Har-Lopinong’s population was of Solan origin.
content.html: Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei, like Satella, was a second-generation Solan immigrant. In 1936, when a socialist government was established in Fengle, great waves of Solan immigrants had poured into Har-Lopinong, the imperial metropole. Now nearly thirty percent of Har-Lopinong’s population was of Solan origin.

content.text: “The flashbacks are still bad,” Satella said.
Shiyuĕ was one of the very few people to whom Satella could speak honestly about her past.
“That’s how these things are. Two tablets of Ifexer, once a day. Make sure you take them.”

Satella accepted the prescription. Everyone in this city was ill at heart. Even without Shiyuĕ’s own mad-scientist air, anyone could have found the city’s endless stream of psychiatric cases a dreary thing.

When Satella stepped back out into the street, her stomach growled. For all that she had once preached the equalization of happiness, she was still only human. She went into the Solan restaurant next door, Yifeng.
“One. Same as always. Chahan.”

Mon Harc, the Solan second-generation girl who worked there, spoke Aldian in a clumsy jumble, though she made up for it in friendliness. She was a student herself, and worked here every day but Wednesday. Most Solan immigrants were poor like her, and their education often extended no further than Solan.
content.html: Mon Harc, the Solan second-generation girl who worked there, spoke Aldian in a clumsy jumble, though she made up for it in friendliness. She was a student herself, and worked here every day but Wednesday. Most Solan immigrants were poor like her, and their education often extended no further than Solan.

content.text: “Fried rice, yes,” Satella said. “But today I’m in the mood for Hunhunyo.”
Fish-powder noodles—her mother’s Solan cooking from childhood. Rice noodles tangled in a broth rich with stock. The taste brought back the days when she had once been happy.
“Yes. Chahan.”
A plate of fried rice arrived.
He probably had not understood that she had declined it. Satella accepted the dish with as much gratitude as she could manage and ate it all the same.
When she left the restaurant, she took a different route back to the dorm. She had eaten a little too much, and thought a walk might help.
“Stop right there, Satella Long.”
Halfway home, women in black closed in around her. Guĕnar had come for her life.
“I’m a ‘model prisoner’ now,” Satella said. “Sorry, but my action scenes are over.”
She turned as if to leave. One of the women struck her, and as Satella fell, a witch’s knife flashed down toward her.
“That’s far enough!”
A woman in a tokko-style coat appeared out of nowhere and slapped the knife aside. Then she gave the Guĕnar women one kick and one punch each, with startling economy.
“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! Women’s tears don’t belong in my streets!”
content.html: “My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! Women’s tears don’t belong in my streets!”

content.text: Shinan declared it at the top of her lungs. A woman of the underworld, one of the people who controlled Har-Lopinong from the shadows—yet in this city she was loved by children as a hero.
“Five Heavens is another cause of unhappiness,” one of the witches said. “Let us remove it here.”
The witches of Guĕnar drew their knives. Shinan had no weapon at all. Her reason, apparently, was that heroes looked lame if they carried one.
“The sun decides what happiness is!” Shinan shouted back. “Calling it unhappiness is arrogance!”

One witch rolled back her eyes and collapsed under a grappling move Shinan proudly called the “Arsnorva Punch.” The others, seeing that, fled at once. The technique looked less like heroics than brutally efficient professional wrestling, and Satella found herself laughing in spite of everything.
“Now that’s a nice smile!”
Shinan praised the crooked little smile Satella herself had barely noticed. No one had called it a good smile in a very long time. Satella lowered her eyes to the higanbana pin fastened to her bag.
She wondered whether he was doing well.
That evening she returned to the dorm, ate dinner, and prepared for bed. Just then a message came in on her phone.
“Sis! Starting next term, that friend I told you about is moving into Nort!”

It was from Leto Lówa, a second-year student at Hellén National University. He was back home in the Eltēna Union for the summer. In September, his friend Aliĕ was apparently entering Hellén National University as a freshman.
content.html: It was from Leto Lówa, a second-year student at Hellén National University. He was back home in the Eltēna Union for the summer. In September, his friend Aliĕ was apparently entering Hellén National University as a freshman.

content.text: “Then at the very least,” Satella murmured to herself, “I’ll watch over their happiness.”
She closed her eyes and went to sleep.
The story of a new set of protagonists had begun to cry out into the world.
September 1994. Nort, the student dormitory in the Sósos district of Hellén Direct-Controlled City, Har-Lopinong Republic. The dorm matron, Satella, was pounding on two doors.
content.html: September 1994. Nort, the student dormitory in the Sósos district of Hellén Direct-Controlled City, Har-Lopinong Republic. The dorm matron, Satella, was pounding on two doors.

content.text: “You two are the last ones up. Whoever loses gets garbage duty.”

Third-year student Leto and first-year student Aliĕ sprang out of their futons and raced for the table. Provoked into competition by Satella, they wolfed down breakfast while shouting insults at each other.
“This is your fault for staying up late watching anime!”
“And what about you? You were drinking till all hours too, Senpai!”
A breakfast made by Satella, a Solan immigrant, was supposed to mean a heavy meal from the morning on. Yet before they knew it, they were already at the sink doing dishes.
“Honestly,” Aliĕ grumbled, “why does a future employee of the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy have to wash dishes?”
The two of them were on elite career tracks. That was precisely why they had been sent to Hellén to study abroad.
“What ‘republicans’ think is different from what we do,” Leto said, washing plates with practiced hands. “Don’t assume what’s ‘normal’ for us works here.”
The Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy was a state institution that existed to enthrone the monarchs the world ought to have. To people like them, republicanism was the enemy.

“If you hate it that much, you can move out right now,” Satella said softly, resting a hand on each of their shoulders without a sound. “There are plenty of apartments cheaper than this. I won’t promise you your life, though.”
Neither of them had combat training. They did not even notice the killing intent in her touch.
“No, no problem at all!”
Rattled, they sped up their scrubbing. When the dishes were finished, they grabbed their bags and the trash and burst out through the door.
“We’re off!”
Satella waved after them. She was steadily carrying out her duty.
“New life for student self-government! Congratulations on your enrollment!”

At the entrance to Hellén National University, Mancal had turned out in force to greet the incoming students. It was a warped spectacle: the celebration of admission came second; the organization’s advantage came first.
content.html: At the entrance to Hellén National University, Mancal had turned out in force to greet the incoming students. It was a warped spectacle: the celebration of admission came second; the organization’s advantage came first.

content.text: Students were supposed to present identification at the gate. In practice, if someone forgot a student card, all they had to do was state a student number and a name. Security was laughably loose.

The moment they passed through the gate, club recruiters came down on the freshmen like an avalanche. Some circles pushed their pitches aggressively, but those were usually the old clubs with numbers and backing. To the “executive committee” handling new-student orientation—in other words, Mancal—such groups were both sources of money and sources of bodies. No one ever punished them.
Leto grabbed Aliĕ’s hand and pulled her into a school building. Recruitment was prohibited indoors. Being older, he had learned the rules already.

“For example, even in nitrogen metabolism, plants use ammonium nitrogen and nitrate nitrogen selectively according to conditions, and the efficiency of absorption is influenced by soil pH, cation exchange capacity, and even nitrification and denitrification mediated by microorganisms—”

In the back rows of a large lecture hall, Aliĕ yawned. First-year university classes were the same everywhere: the goal was to survive required credits. Major coursework usually did not begin in earnest until the second year. While the professor droned on, Aliĕ looked enviously at Leto’s upperclassman life and idly scrolled through photographs she had taken over the holiday. Photography was her hobby.
“Is this seat taken?”
A young man sat down beside her. At his waist was pinned a badge in the shape of a Pure-White Knife.
content.html: A young man sat down beside her. At his waist was pinned a badge in the shape of a Pure-White Knife.

content.text: “I still haven’t managed to buy the textbook,” he said. “How far have they gotten?”
His accent made her feel an odd flash of familiarity, though she had never heard his voice before.
“It’s just orientation today, so you don’t need a textbook,” Aliĕ replied. “And honestly, I don’t want to stand in that absurd line either.”
The university co-op was overflowing with students trying to buy their books.
“Thanks. I’m Lakán Fàta. Family name first, given name second. I’m Calabar, you see.”
content.html: “Thanks. I’m Lakán Fàta. Family name first, given name second. I’m Calabar, you see.”

content.text: That explained it. No wonder the sound of his name had seemed oddly familiar.
“I’m Aliĕ Namile. Given name first, family name second. I’m from Eltēna.”
content.html: “I’m Aliĕ Namile. Given name first, family name second. I’m from Eltēna.”

content.text: Once the introductions were over, Fàta copied the board in a dense, steady hand. Perhaps because of his ties to Kàlavé, the page beside it was already crammed full of Calabar script.
Aliĕ assumed that would be the end of their conversation. But after class, Fàta handed her a flyer.
“West Altos Researcher’s Club. It sounds perfect for people like us. If you want, shall we go have a look?”
content.html: “West Altos Researcher’s Club. It sounds perfect for people like us. If you want, shall we go have a look?”

content.text: Aliĕ and Fàta met up with Leto and headed to the second floor of Building Five, where the club had its table. The place was a dumping ground for minor circles too small to have clubrooms of their own.
“What, are you here to join!?”
A young woman who seemed to be the club president leaned toward them.
“We’re just looking around,” Leto said calmly. “We’re all from West Altos, that’s all.”
He had already advised the two freshmen not to commit before seeing what else was out there.
“Oh. Right.”

At once the president seemed to shrink in on herself. The three visitors tried to keep her in good spirits while they looked over the club’s odds and ends: a carved wooden fox from Skniune, a horseshoe from Durguatan, a brass instrument from Deltai, traditional clothing from Eltēna, and a Duïlé knife from Kàlavé. When Aliĕ picked up the knife, the president brightened again.
content.html: At once the president seemed to shrink in on herself. The three visitors tried to keep her in good spirits while they looked over the club’s odds and ends: a carved wooden fox from Skniune, a horseshoe from Durguatan, a brass instrument from Deltai, traditional clothing from Eltēna, and a Duïlé knife from Kàlavé. When Aliĕ picked up the knife, the president brightened again.

content.text: “You’ve got a good eye! That’s a Duïlé knife from Kàlavé! Better yet, it’s supposed to be one once carried by the legendary swordsman Shàun Pàlajé! Though it’s probably a fake.”
content.html: “You’ve got a good eye! That’s a Duïlé knife from Kàlavé! Better yet, it’s supposed to be one once carried by the legendary swordsman Shàun Pàlajé! Though it’s probably a fake.”

content.text: Aliĕ and Leto looked impressed. Fàta took the knife from them, weighed it in his hand, and said a single astonishing thing.
“It’s real.”
Everyone in the room drew breath at once. Fàta went on.

“Pàlajé used two knives in his lifetime. One was donated to the National Museum of Kàlavé. The other disappeared. Both were Fitoliĕfiĕnio—double-edged straight blades, with lengths of roughly seventy to ninety centimeters. The hilt bears the Shàun family crest, and a fox-colored ornament that only the First Instructor is permitted to use.”
content.html: “Pàlajé used two knives in his lifetime. One was donated to the National Museum of Kàlavé. The other disappeared. Both were Fitoliĕfiĕnio—double-edged straight blades, with lengths of roughly seventy to ninety centimeters. The hilt bears the Shàun family crest, and a fox-colored ornament that only the First Instructor is permitted to use.”

content.text: Fàta seemed able to feel something in the knife with his fingertips, but Aliĕ and Leto could make nothing of it. The club president had tears in her eyes.
“I’m Minac Arsnorva, third year! I never thought I’d find a comrade in a place like this!”
content.html: “I’m Minac Arsnorva, third year! I never thought I’d find a comrade in a place like this!”

content.text: Arsnorva. Leto had heard the name somewhere before, but decided not to ask.
“Could you give it to me?” Fàta asked.
The request was plainly unreasonable. Minac’s face changed at once, and she shook her head hard. Fàta continued anyway.

“I’m the grandson of Lakán Lài, the former Speaker of Parliament. That knife was the one Ládén Kïnàbé, my grandfather’s teacher, entrusted to Pàlajé. I want to know whether I’m worthy to inherit that will.”
content.html: “I’m the grandson of Lakán Lài, the former Speaker of Parliament. That knife was the one Ládén Kïnàbé, my grandfather’s teacher, entrusted to Pàlajé. I want to know whether I’m worthy to inherit that will.”

content.text: Minac was obviously moved by his story, but equally tormented by the idea of handing the knife over. Watching her struggle, Aliĕ offered a suggestion.
“Then how about you settle it with a match? Winner gets their terms accepted.”
Minac’s lips curled in a fearless grin.
“Heh. Then, young man, let’s settle this with Duïlé!”
Two wooden swords appeared in her hands.
In the university gym, Minac tossed one of the wooden swords to Fàta.
“If I win, you’re buying me dinner!”
Fàta took the weapon and raised it.
“I’ll inherit the will of Kïnàbé, my grandfather, and my father!”
Aliĕ cheered excitedly from the side. Leto turned to her.
“Let’s bet too. Loser buys dinner. I’m betting on Minac.”
Though an amateur, Leto observed coolly. Minac’s core was steady. She slid in through the spaces between Fàta’s emotion-laden strikes, landing blows and gradually closing the distance.
“I’m betting on Fàta,” Aliĕ said. “He’s trying so hard.”
It was true that, raw as he still was, Fàta was reproducing the lineage of Pàlajé’s Stance of Savage Fury. His heavy blows were enough to throw an ordinary opponent off balance.
“Dogmatists don’t survive,” Minac said.

She shifted from Stance of the Void Mind into Stance of Savage Fury. Very few could reproduce the true Stance of the Moderate. Her fighting spirit rose higher than Fàta’s, and in the next instant the ridge of her wooden sword tapped lightly against his throat.
Minac had defended the club’s treasure.
That evening, the group held a party at Yifeng in Sósos. Naturally, the freshmen who had lost their bets were paying.
“Leto-kun, no alcohol. There are minors here.”

In Har-Lopinong, adulthood began at twenty. In Kàlavé it was fifteen; in Eltēna, fourteen. Still bemused by the cultural differences, the four of them cracked open bottles of Neskel, the carbonated drink from Armona.
content.html: In Har-Lopinong, adulthood began at twenty. In Kàlavé it was fifteen; in Eltēna, fourteen. Still bemused by the cultural differences, the four of them cracked open bottles of Neskel, the carbonated drink from Armona.

content.text: “Chahan. Four. Finish.”

Mon Harc, the Solan second-generation waitress, was once again cheerfully mangling the orders. Ever since his first visit, Leto had somehow never been served anything but fried rice, no matter what he ordered.
content.html: Mon Harc, the Solan second-generation waitress, was once again cheerfully mangling the orders. Ever since his first visit, Leto had somehow never been served anything but fried rice, no matter what he ordered.

content.text: “At this rate I’m going to forget the taste of wheat from my homeland. What an unpatriotic man I am.”
He said it in self-mockery and tucked into the fried rice all the same. Beside him, Aliĕ tried to comfort the crestfallen Fàta.
“You looked cool, Fàta-kun. We can’t fight at all.”
Holding the side of his neck where Minac had struck him, Fàta calmly analyzed his defeat.
“Minac-san, your Stance of Savage Fury was a bluff, wasn’t it? For just a moment I saw you switch back to Stance of the Void Mind. I was two moves too slow.”
Minac’s eyes lit up and she launched into excited technical talk. Before anyone noticed, the Neskel cans were empty.
That was the moment Aliĕ and Leto made up their minds.
“Minac-san,” they said, “we want to join the West Altos Researcher’s Club.”
October 1994. Hellén National University gymnasium.
content.html: October 1994. Hellén National University">Hellén National University gymnasium.

content.text: “You don’t have to know how to use a knife. If you understand distance, a handgun is actually the more advantageous weapon.”

Aliĕ was being taught close-quarters combat by Fàta. Back in Eltēna, she had already learned how to fire a handgun for self-defense. What she lacked was a way to get out of an opponent’s reach once a fight became close.
“No. Your vital points are wide open. Set your body so the enemy can’t read your weakness.”
Aliĕ moved the way Fàta told her to.
“Only the one who has mastered form can break form.”

That, according to legend, was what the great swordsman Imaden Ria—the father of modern Duïlé—had once said. Aliĕ still had no means of evading a powerful opponent’s irregular attacks. But before anything else, she needed to learn how to avoid the regular, predictable blows of ordinary thugs.
“Honestly, I doubt I’ll ever get a chance to draw this thing.”

Aliĕ spun her air gun around a finger. She had not told Fàta that she belonged to the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy. The day would come when this weapon mattered. She simply could not yet see the shape of that future.
“My father used to say the same thing,” Fàta said. “But his chance came.”

He meant his father, Lakán Sài, who had died when Fàta was two. Fàta believed one thing with absolute certainty: even strong people died if they were unprepared. That was why, at the very least, he wanted his friends to be ready.
content.html: He meant his father, Lakán Sài, who had died when Fàta was two. Fàta believed one thing with absolute certainty: even strong people died if they were unprepared. That was why, at the very least, he wanted his friends to be ready.

content.text: “When I graduate,” he said, “I’m going to become a police officer.”
He was following in his father’s footsteps.
“Like Officer Darc? A professional slacker?”
Aliĕ was trying to lighten the mood. In truth, the local police box in Sósos did nothing unless ordered from above. It merely stood as the chaperone to the struggle between Five Heavens and Mancal.
“In Kàlavé,” Fàta said with a grin.
“In Kàlavé!?”
At the student affairs office, Minac was currently giving a passionate speech to a staff member on the greatness of Duïlé. Silence had swallowed the room.
“Want me to take over?” Leto asked.

Unable to watch any longer, he stepped in to mediate. The real subject at hand was the present situation: the West Altos Researcher’s Club was officially recognized, and yet had no clubroom. There was also the matter of how subsidy money was being allocated.
content.html: Unable to watch any longer, he stepped in to mediate. The real subject at hand was the present situation: the West Altos Researcher’s Club was officially recognized, and yet had no clubroom. There was also the matter of how subsidy money was being allocated.

content.text: “As I told you,” the staff member said in a flat administrative voice, “those matters should be taken up with the student association.”

The reason the officially recognized club had no room was simple: the existing clubs had occupied them all years ago and never let go. The trick was favoritism toward old groups. As for the subsidy, the university paid it first in a lump sum to the student association—Mancal—and only afterward did it get distributed to the clubs. The amount was whatever Mancal decided it would be, and whether a requested amount was approved depended on one’s loyalty to them.
“No way, no way, no way!”

With Minac kicking up a childish fuss, Leto practically dragged her to the club-building complex. The place spread before them like a jumble pretending to be a fortress: solid enough, perhaps, but with none of a fortress’s dignity.
“You must be from the West Altos Researcher’s Club?”
A man addressed them. He was dressed in a way that made his age impossible to guess. In fact, there was something faintly boyish about him.
“Allow me to introduce myself. I’m Ágre Yall, president of the student association.”
content.html: “Allow me to introduce myself. I’m Ágre Yall, president of the student association.”

content.text: He produced a business card. Naturally, he did not use the word Mancal even once.
“I’m Leto,” Leto replied. “A member of the West Altos Researcher’s Club. This is our president, Minac. We came regarding the clubroom and the subsidy.”
He spoke evenly and without ornament. Ágre was a genius of rhetoric. Leto could not afford to let himself be drawn into the man’s pace.
“As you can see, room is limited,” Ágre said. “I’m terribly sorry. Everyone is doing their best with what little they have. I hope you’ll understand.”
As if, Leto thought. The Historical Studies Society—which was, in reality, nothing but a gathering of antiwar activists—never even seemed to open its door.
“As for the subsidy, we intend to discuss it at the student general assembly. I do hope the two of you will attend.”
Ágre handed them an invitation card.
“Expose the hypocrisy of the Moderate party administration! General Assembly 10/30”

What relation did the Moderate party administration have to university self-government? Then again, according to their logic, a government frightened of Mancal was trying to centralize control over universities.
“Understood,” said Leto, and bowed, preparing to leave.
“That knife is beautiful,” Ágre said suddenly, glancing at the Pàlajé knife on Minac’s person. “Would you mind letting me see it?”
It was an obvious attempt at ingratiation.
“Right! Shiny things are every boy’s dream, after all,” said Minac. “I’m a girl, but still.”
Having been completely led into Ágre’s pace, Minac had to be dragged away by Leto. Behind them, Ágre stood waving with studied friendliness.
“Then why don’t we just go to the general assembly?” Fàta said.
A strategy meeting was convened at Nort. Fàta, earnest to a fault, opened with that simple answer.
“You two don’t know this, but it’s a farce,” Leto said. “The proceedings are fixed from the beginning, and if you’re in favor, you applaud. Even the voting format is distorted.”
He explained how the student general assembly worked. It was a bitter irony: a defender of absolute monarchy lecturing a student self-government body on democracy.
“But it’s unfair that we don’t get a clubroom!” Minac cried, crunching loudly on a snack in someone else’s dorm room without a shred of reserve. “And the subsidy too!”
“Would you all keep it down a little?” Satella called from the next room. “I can’t hear the television.”
She was watching TV in the adjoining room. Perhaps Kàlavé state broadcasting, perhaps Quo Flamei. The clear pronunciation of Calabar drifted faintly through the wall.
“Then first,” said Aliĕ, “let’s follow their ‘form’ and beat them that way.”
She was appealing to a straightforward method. In truth, there was no other option. The four of them steeled themselves.
On the day of the general assembly, Leto and Minac went as representatives of the club. At reception they wrote down their names and phone numbers and were shown to their assigned seats.
Even the seating was a trap.

The places visible to the cameras had been filled with stooges Mancal had summoned from the old clubs. They wanted photographs full of applause and enthusiastic faces. As she sat down, Minac leaned toward Leto and muttered:
“A prepared swordsman always keeps two knives hidden in his coat.”
At the time, Leto had no idea what she meant.
On the stage, Ágre declared the assembly open.

“Five Heavens is indeed a threat. We must fight without compromise against any force that endangers our healthy student life. However! The true enemy is the Moderate party administration, which exploits the threat of Five Heavens in order to justify the abuse of police power!”
The stooges burst into applause. No doubt more than a few real attendees were being swept along by the pressure of the majority.
The scripted meeting rolled on. At last, as the sun was going down, the period for questions began. Leto raised his hand at once.
“West Altos Researcher’s Club. I would like to ask on what standards the student association determines subsidy amounts, and why those amounts are not distributed equally.”
The officer in charge answered.

“Subsidy allocations are determined by a comprehensive consideration of requested amounts and intended uses in each fiscal year. As for the specific differences in sums, we cannot disclose them here, as doing so would risk provoking conflict among clubs.”
It was a supremely bureaucratic answer. But Leto had been unable to ask outright for his own club’s share to be raised. His hands had seemed tied.
Then he realized he still had a second knife to draw.
“One more question. Do you verify, by means of receipts, whether the stated purpose of subsidy applications matches the actual use of the funds?”
Ágre immediately took the microphone himself.
“We choose to trust our comrades in that regard and deliberately leave each club’s accounting to the club itself. Even so, does the West Altos Researcher’s Club consider such verification necessary?”
“Yes,” Leto answered. “Precisely because they are comrades.”
The temperature in the room seemed to drop all at once.
The script had been broken.
At that same time, Aliĕ and Fàta were on their way home.
“I hope things are going well for Leto and Minac-san,” Aliĕ said.
“With Leto-san there, they’ll be fine.”
As the two of them chatted, men in black trailed them through the street.
“Even I can work this out,” Fàta said, stepping in front of Aliĕ and drawing his wooden sword. “Who are you people?”
“We act on the orders of Shinan Arsnorva-sama. Tell us where Pàlajé’s knife is.”
content.html: “We act on the orders of Shinan Arsnorva-sama. Tell us where Pàlajé’s knife is.”

content.text: The men rushed them. They seemed to belong to Five Heavens. Why they knew about the knife at all was a mystery, but Fàta had no time to dwell on it. He caught one man’s punch, then drove the practice sword into the man’s utterly unguarded torso. Their technique was so poor that even Fàta, still inexperienced, could fend them off with ease. It felt strange. Were they really Five Heavens?
But numbers were numbers. Even Fàta could not beat sheer quantity forever. As he was pushed into a corner, a line of BB pellets whipped through the air above his head.
Aliĕ’s air gun.
“What are you doing? Run!”
“The time has come to draw this thing,” Aliĕ said.

The pellets were only painful, not deadly, but they were enough to break the attackers’ attention for a heartbeat. Fàta used that instant to sweep them aside. Then one of the men lunged at Aliĕ with a raised fist.
For one instant it seemed certain she was finished.
Then a woman in a white coat appeared with startling grace and sprayed the man with a mysterious liquid.
“Chloroacetophenone,” she said coolly. “A lethal poison.”
The men turned pale and ran for their lives.
The woman in the white coat was Shiyuĕ Lei. She took Aliĕ by the hand.
content.html: The woman in the white coat was Shiyuĕ Lei. She took Aliĕ by the hand.

content.text: “Well,” she said, “that was a lie. It’s only tear gas.”
After tending the wounds Aliĕ and Fàta had taken, Shiyuĕ gave them a warning.
“You’ve managed to get the attention of people who are truly troublesome.”
At that same moment, the bell rang at Nort.
Officer Darc stood at the door again—but the cheerful mask he always wore was gone.
“If you want to protect the children’s happiness,” he said to Satella, “then cooperate with me.”
There was a knife in Satella’s hand.
November 1994. Yifeng, in the Sósos district.
content.html: November 1994. Yifeng, in the Sósos district.

content.text: “Hunhunyo. Four. Understood.”
Mon, who usually understood no order except fried rice, repeated the request calmly. The atmosphere in the restaurant was that heavy.
“It’s true, isn’t it,” Leto said, breaking the silence at last. “Shinan Arsnorva of Five Heavens is your older sister, Minac-san?”
content.html: “It’s true, isn’t it,” Leto said, breaking the silence at last. “Shinan Arsnorva of Five Heavens is your older sister, Minac-san?”

content.text: Minac was usually bright by force of will, but today she looked darker than ever.

“She is,” Minac said. “My sister’s always been a hell-raiser. Our parents were forever scolding her. Then, in 1990, she got arrested as one of the ringleaders in the large-scale conflict between Five Heavens and Mancal. After that, the family disowned her.”
There were tears standing in Minac’s eyes. She clearly treasured Shinan.
“On the day of the general assembly,” Aliĕ said, “the men who attacked us used Shinan-san’s name.”
“Within Five Heavens, my sister’s one of the moderates,” said Minac. “Using underlings to attack ordinary civilians—that’s not like her.”
She took out the Pàlajé knife.
“I bought this at an antique shop in Sósos. I swear, I didn’t get it through anything dangerous.”
She was not lying. No one present had been inclined to suspect her in the first place.
“I want to ask my sister directly.”

It was true: if anyone could speak to Shinan, it would be Minac. But if Shinan really was after the knife, there could be no guarantee of her safety. Everyone worried for Minac. At the same time, they wanted to trust the faith she still had in her sister.
The following day, on the riverbank in Sósos, the Arsnorva sisters faced each other. Aliĕ, Leto, and Fàta watched from a short distance away.
“Minac. Hand me that knife.”
The words from Shinan’s mouth were the worst possible ones.
Minac faltered. How could her beloved sister—her sister, the hero of the whole town—say such a thing?
“Shinan!” she cried. “You’re not a hero anymore!”
At that desperate cry, a shadow passed over Shinan’s face.
“That’s right,” Shinan said. “At the end of the day, I’m mafia. I can’t go against Lady Scarlet Heaven’s orders.”
She raised her fists. The killing intent in them fixed itself on Minac.
“In that case,” Minac said, “I won’t hold back either.”
She drew Pàlajé’s knife. She was prepared to deliver the final blow to one hero here.

Shinan was overwhelmingly faster. Before Minac could even swing the knife, Shinan’s fist drove into her solar plexus. Minac collapsed, and Shinan flowed straight into a joint lock. Everyone watching thought the match was over.
“This is the second blade!”
Minac pulled out a wooden sword and smashed it into Shinan’s knee.

The lock broke. Minac came up at once and used the Stance of Savage Fury to set the rhythm of the exchange. Shinan knew nothing of Duïlé. The chained attacks of Savage Fury gradually became predictable—that was exactly what made them a bluff. Minac read Shinan’s punch, switched into Stance of the Void Mind, and countered cleanly. A moment later, her wooden sword was aimed at Shinan’s throat.
“Why won’t you use that one?” Shinan asked.
Her eyes were fixed not on the bokken, but on Pàlajé’s knife.
“You idiot, Sis!” Minac shouted. “Shinan is still my precious family!”
And instead of striking, she threw her arms around her sister.
Aliĕ and the others ran toward the two of them as they stood there weeping.
“All right, that’s enough! I’d like to arrest someone for assault in the act—except…”
Officer Darc came rolling up to the five of them on a bicycle. At once, Aliĕ and the others moved to shield the Arsnorva sisters and stood in front of him.
“This is an emotional scene,” Aliĕ said. “Please don’t interrupt. Besides, with the way you’re dressed, you’re off duty, aren’t you?”
Darc laughed.
“Then you haven’t studied enough. There’s such a thing as a private person’s arrest.”
He went on.
“But that isn’t why I’m here. Today, I came to tell you the truth.”
He pulled a folder from his backpack and began handing out documents one by one, like lecture materials at a university class.
“Materials on Guĕnar?” Leto asked.
As an operative of the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy, and as a resident of Har-Lopinong, he knew a little more about Guĕnar than most.
“I borrowed them from someone involved,” Darc said. “In any case, let me give you the conclusion first. The ones who attacked you on the day of the general assembly were Mancal.”
Then he told them the story from the beginning.

It had all begun four years earlier, in 1990. In the middle of its conflict with Nine Earths, Five Heavens had seized from the enemy two knives that were almost perfect doubles of each other. On their hilts were the crest of the Shàun family, and the fox-colored decoration that in Kàlavé only the First Instructor was permitted to use. The members of Five Heavens were convinced they had found Pàlajé’s knife.
content.html: It had all begun four years earlier, in 1990. In the middle of its conflict with Nine Earths, Five Heavens had seized from the enemy two knives that were almost perfect doubles of each other. On their hilts were the crest of the Shàun family, and the fox-colored decoration that in Kàlavé only the First Instructor was permitted to use. The members of Five Heavens were convinced they had found Pàlajé’s knife.

content.text: But Pàlajé had used only two knives in his life. One had been donated to the National Museum of Kàlavé. The other had vanished. Which meant that of the two in their hands, one had to be a fake.
After agonizing over the matter, they sold the genuine article and came to believe that the fake was the real one.

Then, during the conflict between Mancal and Five Heavens, Ágre snatched up that knife in the heat of the moment and killed one of the members of Five Heavens with it. Only later did it come out that the man he had killed was the son of the Commissioner of the National Police Agency.
content.html: Then, during the conflict between Mancal and Five Heavens, Ágre snatched up that knife in the heat of the moment and killed one of the members of Five Heavens with it. Only later did it come out that the man he had killed was the son of the Commissioner of the National Police Agency.

content.text: Mancal had killed someone. The police could not admit, no matter what, that the person involved had been a member of Five Heavens. And Five Heavens, fearing retaliation from Nine Earths, wanted to conceal the fact that the knife had ever been taken. The interests of all three parties aligned in grotesque harmony.
The evidence—the knife—had to be buried in darkness.
So the police arrested a convenient scapegoat, Shinan, and closed the case.

But then, in October of this year, Ágre spotted that knife at Minac’s side. Panicked by the thought that the evidence had resurfaced in broad daylight, he ordered Mancal, the police, and even Five Heavens to recover it—without realizing that Minac’s knife was not the same one.
“So that means,” Aliĕ said, stunned, “we’re being hunted by three different organizations?”
“More accurately, two,” Darc replied.
Everyone frowned in confusion. Darc turned to Fàta.

“Lakán Fàta. In your judgment, this one is real, isn’t it? Which means Five Heavens let go of the genuine article long ago. Once you realize that, the picture changes. Five Heavens now holds leverage over Mancal and the police.”
content.html: “Lakán Fàta. In your judgment, this one is real, isn’t it? Which means Five Heavens let go of the genuine article long ago. Once you realize that, the picture changes. Five Heavens now holds leverage over Mancal and the police.”

content.text: Shinan exhaled in relief and pulled Minac close again.
“Who are you, exactly?” Leto asked Darc.
“I’m a messenger of freedom,” Darc said. “Young people—throw the dice with your own hands!”
Then he mounted his bicycle and rode away into the light of the setting sun.
At the same time, at Sósos West Clinic—
“I’ve decided I should try facing my past, at least a little,” Satella said.
In her hand she held the higanbana pin. The love she had left behind in Sansonle—she meant to try, once more, to pick it up.
“I hate to be cruel,” said Shiyuĕ, “but you should assume he has already found a happiness that does not include you.”
She forced the truth on Satella. Once Satella had tried to equalize happiness for all; now she had found herself jealous of the different happiness Qàlé had obtained.
“I know,” Satella said. “Even so, someday I want to be able to say that his happiness makes me happy too.”
She had learned what it meant to watch over someone’s happiness. The children at Nort had taught her that.
“I see,” said Shiyuĕ. “Then I can give you the go-ahead. Go on and get your heart broken properly.”
She slapped Satella lightly on the shoulder. Satella bowed, left the clinic, and opened the mail app on her phone. From the depths of a long-buried inbox she pulled up Qàlé’s email address.
“Please,” she murmured. “Let it reach him.”
She offered the prayer across the far sea.

One week later, in front of the offices of Skiĕl, a front company of Five Heavens, members of Mancal stood in ranks with their protest batons. There was no sign of any Five Heavens personnel. Ágre, visibly impatient, gave orders to his followers.
“The target is the executives. Above all else, silence Shinan Arsnorva.”
He barked commands to the Mancal members. Ironically, he did not seem to notice that he himself had become the very thing they claimed to hate: a little army.
The doors of the building were smashed open, and Ágre led the charge inside. There was not a single member of Five Heavens waiting there.
Instead, they found Fàta and Minac.
“Sorry,” Fàta said, “but this one’s the first blade. The second knife is on its way to the university.”
The office building had been a bluff. The true operation was Aliĕ and Leto’s exposure of the truth on campus.
“It’s revenge for my sister,” Minac said, gripping the Pàlajé knife—held, of course, with the flat ready. “So I hope you’ll forgive me for making this hurt a little.”
“Since you now know the truth,” Ágre said, “you can serve as offerings for the revolution.”
He ordered his subordinates to head for the university, then stayed behind to face Fàta and Minac alone. His weapon was a club, its dried bloodstains a mute testimony to the violence he had lived by.
At the same time, at Hellén National University—
content.html: At the same time, at Hellén National University">Hellén National University—

content.text: The bulletin boards throughout the school buildings were plastered with flyers exposing Ágre’s murder. Under ordinary circumstances, student affairs would never have approved the posting of such material.
That was where Leto’s gift for negotiation had shone.

“In the past,” he told them, “the ‘student association’ permitted posters that described a secretary’s suicide, caused by a minister of the Moderate party administration, as ‘murder.’ That was mere gossip. Our claim rests on evidence.”
On the student affairs desk, the documents Darc had provided were piled in stacks.
“Or would you like to claim that you yourselves are complicit in this three-way conspiracy?”
Pressed that far, student affairs gave no immediate answer. But that day, permission to post the flyers was finally granted.
“Extra edition! Extra edition! President Ágre Yall of the student association conspired with the police and Five Heavens to commit murder!”
content.html: “Extra edition! Extra edition! President Ágre Yall of the student association conspired with the police and Five Heavens to commit murder!”

content.text: Aliĕ handed out the flyers at the main entrance. In her chest burned a fierce sense of justice, and the will to avenge what had been done to the Arsnorva sisters. This, for her, was a way of fighting too.
Then the Mancal members sent by Ágre came charging in with protest batons.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
One of Shinan’s locks dropped a man instantly. Five Heavens had been deployed from the beginning to make sure Aliĕ and the others survived.
“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! I’ll protect the innocent girl brave enough to tell the truth!”
Her voice rang out.
The hero had returned to the city.

Ágre’s fighting ability was overwhelming. The youthfulness that hid his true age had been forged by relentless training. Worse, he was cunning. He had identified Minac as the more dangerous opponent and focused on her without mercy. Duïlé was never meant to withstand a heavy weapon like a club. Minac gradually lost her footing—and at last, the knife flew from her hand.
Minac went down. The Pàlajé knife skidded across the floor to Fàta.
“I don’t want to lose any more people I care about!”
Fàta took up the knife. The instant he touched the hilt, he felt he understood how it was meant to be used.
This is a ruler’s knife, he thought. A ruler with a heart always keeps one step back and sees the whole field.
It was almost as though he could hear Pàlajé whisper it in his ear.
Fàta widened the distance between them and looked around. A path opened before him.
He struck Ágre’s exposed leg. The instant the man lost balance, Fàta slipped around behind him without a sound and set the knife to his throat.
“Stance of the Void Mind—completed.”
But just as that victory threatened to erase all the others’ efforts, the tread of a police special unit—sent on the orders of the Commissioner—echoed through the building.
“So the reinforcements are here,” Ágre murmured.
Fàta and Minac were both covered in wounds. They knew, in that instant, that they were finished.
Then a flash grenade flew into the room.
At the same moment, a white owl dropped out of the air.
It was Shàun Akau.
content.html: It was Shàun Akau.

content.text: While the special unit reeled from the light, a Pure-White Knife flashed through them. Fàta and Minac could not even read her blade; it moved too quickly for the eye to follow.
content.html: While the special unit reeled from the light, a Pure-White Knife flashed through them. Fàta and Minac could not even read her blade; it moved too quickly for the eye to follow.

content.text: Once she had finished off the special unit, Akau sent a communication to someone unseen. Then she walked toward Fàta. Instinctively he raised the knife to protect the fallen Minac.
In this foreign land, Akau had seen enough to know that his grandfather’s soul still lived on.
Satisfied of that much, she slipped away from the building without a sound.
The witch’s prayer had, indeed, reached its destination.

A week later still, Ágre was arrested on suspicion of murder. The victim’s identity, however, was kept under the strictest seal. The Worl Federal Preparatory Commission intervened and pressured Five Heavens not to reveal that the dead man had been both the son of the Commissioner of the National Police Agency and a member of Five Heavens. In exchange, the Preparatory Commission gained one more hold over the Har-Lopinong police.
content.html: A week later still, Ágre was arrested on suspicion of murder. The victim’s identity, however, was kept under the strictest seal. The Worl Federal Preparatory Commission intervened and pressured Five Heavens not to reveal that the dead man had been both the son of the Commissioner of the National Police Agency and a member of Five Heavens. In exchange, the Preparatory Commission gained one more hold over the Har-Lopinong police.

content.text: “I’m going to make a BL doujin about Lord Akau and His Eminence Tané!”
At Yifeng, the West Altos Researcher’s Club was holding another strategy meeting. This time the agenda was whether to participate in the dōjin fair coming up at the end of December.
content.html: At Yifeng, the West Altos Researcher’s Club was holding another strategy meeting. This time the agenda was whether to participate in the dōjin fair coming up at the end of December.

content.text: “Minac-san,” Fàta said, “that is irreverent even for you. And besides, BL is forbidden in Kàlavé.”
Minac visibly deflated.
“In the end,” Leto said with a sigh, “Mancal only changed the head on top. Nothing else.”
Ágre had been arrested, but the dark customs that surrounded the university remained.
“Even so,” Aliĕ said, bright as ever, “doesn’t it feel a little cleaner now?”
She meant it. The clubroom and the subsidy could still be won. They would get them together. She believed in her friends.
“Order if you’re ordering! Or leave!”
Mon pressed them for an answer. Whatever they ordered, fried rice would probably arrive anyway. The four of them burst into laughter.
At the Skiĕl office building, a blind woman stood clutching a tasteless bag covered with photographs of the First Instructor Tané. At her feet lay a torn-up picture of Akau.
“Just wait for me! I’ll kill you soon enough, my beloved husband!”
Blue Heaven was spreading over Har-Lopinong.

sections.id: The Street Business Regulation Act Arc
sections.title: The Street Business Regulation Act Arc

content.text: March 1995. The plaza in front of Hellén Central Station, Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City. To the commuters and students streaming past, it was no more than a place to cross through. In the middle of that passing current stood a single Durguatan woman with a guitar in her hands.
content.html: March 1995. The plaza in front of Hellén Central Station, Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City. To the commuters and students streaming past, it was no more than a place to cross through. In the middle of that passing current stood a single Durguatan woman with a guitar in her hands.

content.text: "Kura"

On the plains of Asetilo,
a lone wolf steps forward.

Into the pack called democracy,
a lone wolf bares its fangs.

Once you step in, hunt the foxes to the last!
Once you bite down, drag out their entrails!

Never forget the pioneer’s name!
Never forget the lone wolf’s name!
Kura! Kura! Kura!

The sound of Hōmī—throat-singing—spread across the square. The people coming and going looked like a pack of wolves racing over the Asetilo plains. When the song ended, the performer turned to her only two listeners, Aliĕ and Leto.
“That was ‘Kura,’” she said, “a piece based on traditional Durguatan music, with my own arrangement added to it.”
Aliĕ and Leto applauded.
“That raspy part in the middle,” Aliĕ asked eagerly, “how do you do that?”
“Hōmī,” the woman said. “If you draw back your tongue and round your lips, another set of vocal folds—the false vocal cords—starts to vibrate.”
She demonstrated on the spot. Aliĕ tried to imitate her, but no matter what she did, the sound refused to come out right.
A policeman from Elben District approached the three of them while they were still laughing together.
“You’re in violation of the Street Business Regulation Act,” he said. “If you leave immediately, I’ll let it slide.”
The woman began putting away her instrument with an apologetic look.
“But she wasn’t asking for tips,” Leto protested.
The woman told him it was all right and turned to go.
“My name is Kura Mogroi,” she said. “I’m a second-year at Hellén National University. I hope we meet again somewhere.”
content.html: “My name is Kura Mogroi,” she said. “I’m a second-year at Hellén National University. I hope we meet again somewhere.”

content.text: The next day, in one of the university’s moot-court classrooms, Aliĕ of the law department was attending a trial debate event jointly hosted by several seminars. Third-year seminar placement would begin soon, and this was her chance to see what they were like.

“All right, then,” said the professor. “Let’s have our two first-years try a debate. The topic will be the merits and demerits of the Street Business Regulation Act. On the negative side, Namile-kun. And on the affirmative side…”
A white-haired woman with blue eyes raised her hand.
“Hal Perort. Second year.”
content.html: “Hal Perort. Second year.”

content.text: The professor called on her. Even so, he doubted anyone could appear who truly surpassed Alka Melduĕk, the prodigy who had belonged to the university four years earlier.
content.html: The professor called on her. Even so, he doubted anyone could appear who truly surpassed Alka Melduĕk, the prodigy who had belonged to the university four years earlier.

content.text: “This law ends up restricting even artistic activity like street performances,” Aliĕ began. “I think that is a plain violation of freedom of expression. Are we really allowed to take away the kind of place where anyone can casually bring music to the public?”
As she spoke, she remembered Kura. Even with emotion running high inside her, she forced herself to keep her tone composed.

“First,” Hal replied, “I’d like to ask that you not misunderstand the law. It does not impose a total ban. If someone wants to perform live on the street, all they have to do is obtain the prescribed permit. What is needed is the bare minimum of public safety.”
She was practiced. Born one of the Harlays—the majority group in Har-Lopinong—and advanced on to the attached high school of Hellén National University, she was, in other words, an elite.
“But obtaining that permit takes both money and time,” Aliĕ said. “For young artists, that’s a serious burden. It’s a system that could crush talent before it ever blooms.”
Hal did not give an inch. Instead, she spread out her materials and answered in the same cool voice.

“You’re looking only at street performances. The real focus of this law is the management of street business in general. Recently, in particular, the streets have been used more and more for the sale of quasi-legal narcotics and the solicitation of illegal gambling. Street performances are increasingly being abused as a cover for those activities.”
The air in the room changed.
“…Those are only a handful of malicious cases,” Aliĕ shot back. “They have nothing to do with sincere artists.”
“Only a handful? Then should we leave that ‘handful’ alone and put the public in danger?”
Hal pressed on without pause.

“What’s more, a permit system also benefits artists. It gives them an environment where safety is secured, prevents noise disputes, and ensures a proper distance from the audience. There are major advantages in being able to work within an official framework.”
A shadow crossed Aliĕ’s face.

“To begin with,” Hal said, “freedom of expression is not absolute. It is only natural for certain restrictions to be imposed for the sake of public order, public morals, and safety. Freedom and lawlessness are not the same thing. Your argument sounds very much like, ‘Because I dislike regulation, I’m willing to look the other way while crime happens.’”
A murmur of agreement spread through the audience.
That evening, at Yifeng in Sósos—
“What was with that woman?” Aliĕ burst out. “She acts like she speaks for the majority, and it makes me so mad!”
A wounded-hearted Aliĕ was being comforted by Fàta.
“All I can do is fight in the literal sense,” he said. “So I think it’s incredible that you can appeal to people with words the way you do.”
Leto, reading the town news on his phone, put the matter into calmer terms.

“To be fair, Five Heavens has been acting strangely lately. In the districts where Shinan-san and the other ‘moderates’ came in, they had built up a certain coexistence with the city. But now the ‘hardliners’ are undoing that by getting involved in quasi-legal drugs and illegal gambling. Anti–Five Heavens sentiment among ordinary citizens is rising. The majority is probably willing to sacrifice even decent, legitimate artists like Kura-san if that is what it takes.”
The heavy mood in the restaurant changed all at once when Minac appeared.
And she had Kura with her.
“It’s been a while,” Kura said. “I got interested in the West Altos Researcher’s Club, so I came and talked to Minac-san.”
content.html: “It’s been a while,” Kura said. “I got interested in the West Altos Researcher’s Club, so I came and talked to Minac-san.”

content.text: The two of them sat down. On the table, though no one had ordered it, plates of chahan had already been set out.
“We’re entering the Sósos Festival!” Minac declared.
The moment she opened her mouth, she began distributing a handwritten proposal she had apparently stayed up all night preparing.

The Sósos Festival was a neighborhood festival held every April in Sósos Central Park. Five Heavens had long served as a sponsor there, helping reconcile the district with the organization through festival stalls and similar efforts.
“We’re going to sell Sashyk!”

At that suggestion, Kura’s eyes lit up. Minced mutton wrapped around skewers and grilled over charcoal. The image of the fragrant meat rose in everyone’s mind, and with that, all thought of alternative proposals vanished.
“And how,” Leto asked, “do you plan to secure the funds and supplies?”
“We can whittle away at our subsidy,” Minac said, “and after that… we’ll just have to rely on my big sister…”
She called Shinan. There was no answer. She was probably busy.
“First,” Aliĕ said, “let’s make a trial batch of Sashyk.”

At that very same time, in the building of Skiĕl, a front company of Five Heavens, the organization was holding a meeting under heavy guard. The topic was how to clean up the mess left by the Pàlajé Knife incident.

“The members’ failure arose from a lack of unified command,” said one of the Farned, Tor Secmet, laying out the new policy. “From here on, the main body of Five Heavens and the practical operations of the Har-Lopinong parish will be brought under one line of control.”
content.html: “The members’ failure arose from a lack of unified command,” said one of the Farned, Tor Secmet, laying out the new policy. “From here on, the main body of Five Heavens and the practical operations of the Har-Lopinong parish will be brought under one line of control.”

content.text: Transferred in from the West Altos parish, Tor was one of the most hardline figures in Five Heavens. She did not hesitate to harm ordinary civilians.
“Wait,” Shinan shot back. “We’ve lived alongside this city. Even our racket has brought in more than enough money without making trouble for decent people!”
She loved Hellén from the bottom of her heart. She had no intention of surrendering the initiative to some ‘management consultant’ who knew nothing of the city.
“Count Aitel, Blue Heaven, supports my policy,” Tor said. “You no longer have any role to play as a Farned.”

As she spoke, she glanced toward Koto for confirmation. Koto, meanwhile, looked so thoroughly bored that she could scarcely keep her eyes open, passing the time by gazing at photographs of Tanéi on her phone.
“Well,” she said at last, “do try not to drag the organization’s name through the mud.”
The moment Koto left, the members all lowered their heads. From that point on, the split between Shinan and Tor became absolute.
“And why,” Satella demanded a week later, “are you making ‘my home’ smell like sheep?”
She was in the garden of Nort. Several skewers from their trial run were already lined up on plates.
“The coriander seeds work better than the leaves,” Kura said with practiced ease. “And if you use carbonated water, the meat stays tender.”
Apparently she cooked for herself at her own dorm.
“It resembles the Kijebiu of Kàlavé,” Fàta observed. “But the spice mix is slightly different. Without fenugreek, I feel like it’s missing just a little sweetness—and a little bitterness too.”
Fàta knew his way around a kitchen as well. Ever since his father Sai had died when he was two, he had helped his mother with the household work.
“Aliĕ, that one’s burning!”
“And yours is practically raw, Senpai!”
Aliĕ and Leto were in charge of the grill. Hurling insults at each other all the while, they kept sending skewer after skewer onto the fire.
“I’m full already…” Minac groaned. “At this rate I’m going to turn into a sheep…”
She had been assigned to taste-testing. For a moment she had tried to help with the cooking, only to be met with collective disbelief at how catastrophically clumsy she was.
“Oh? I thought I smelled something good. Isn’t that Sashyk?”
Officer Darc called out as he approached. Satella immediately moved to chase him off, but he slipped neatly past her and devoured a skewer in an instant.
“This is excellent,” he said. “Makes me want a beer. If you’re going to sell it with drinks, you’d do well to buy from the Altos goods shop in East Sósos.”
He had scarcely settled into making himself at home when a message came in.
“You lot, get to Sósos West Clinic immediately. I’ve just received word that Shinan Arsnorva has been attacked.”
content.html: “You lot, get to Sósos West Clinic immediately. I’ve just received word that Shinan Arsnorva has been attacked.”

content.text: “Big sis!”
Minac cried out. Everyone dropped what they were doing and ran for Sósos West Clinic.
Three hours earlier, Shinan had been patrolling Sósos as usual.
“No, no, it’s not illegal,” a broker under Five Heavens was telling a student. “And it’s less addictive than tobacco, with fantastic effects.”
A sale of quasi-legal drugs. Tor’s doing.
“No, thanks!” the student snapped. “I’m telling Sister Shinan on you!”
That being the case, it was time for the hero to appear.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
Shinan’s grappling technique caught the broker cleanly.
“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! A brave boy who knows how to say no is under my protection!”
The broker foamed at the mouth and crumpled to the ground. After thanking her, the student ran off.
Then members of Five Heavens blocked her way.
“That won’t do,” one of them said. “You’re interfering with our business.”

They all attacked at once. Shinan seized a nearby bicycle and hurled it into them, then immediately locked one of the men pinned beneath it into a joint hold. She swept another member’s legs out from under him and smashed him into the pavement.
Just as she raised her fist toward the last one, a single round was fired into her.
“That should help cool your head a little.”
Shinan fell without ever seeing the face of the one who had fired.
At Sósos West Clinic, she had fortunately regained consciousness.
“That mark is from a nonlethal round,” Shiyuĕ said. “No evidence left behind. Perfect for an ambush.”
Access to nonlethal rounds was extremely limited. Most likely, they had been diverted to Five Heavens by corrupt police officers back in the days of the three-way standoff.
“Minac. Everyone. Sorry for the trouble.”
Shinan tried to sit up, but Minac stopped her.
“But this settles it,” Shiyuĕ said. “Five Heavens is definitely going down the hardline route now. The public’s anti–Five Heavens sentiment is only going to grow stronger.”

She looked down at the newspaper. A vote on revisions to the Street Business Regulation Act was coming up next month. In Har-Lopinong, where direct democracy had been introduced, decisions were made on the basis of approval and rejection tallied at local Civic Assemblies held in each district’s public hall.
“If street-business regulation gets tightened,” Aliĕ said anxiously, “then immigrant food stalls and street performances by foreign students could disappear too, couldn’t they?”

A law that was meant to crack down on quasi-legal drugs and illegal gambling was baring its fangs at innocent people instead. The majority shut the lid on it by telling themselves it had nothing to do with them. Freedom was beginning to slip away.
“There is one way,” Leto said at last. “The student debate at the Civic Assembly.”
The right to vote in the Civic Assembly belonged only to Har-Lopinong nationals, but non-citizens were still allowed to take part in the student debate.
“The problem,” Fàta said, borrowing the paper from Shiyuĕ, “is that our opponent is Hal Perort.”
The article reported that Hal had just taken first place in the Har-Lopinong Speech Tournament.
“She’s been in demand for student debates ever since attached high school,” Minac said. “Ugh. If this were Duïlé, I wouldn’t lose.”
“But we have to do it,” Aliĕ said. “If we want Kura-san to be able to sing freely.”
She took Kura’s hand.

Into the pack called democracy,
a lone wolf bares its fangs.
Once you step in, hunt the foxes to the last!
April 1995. Sósos Central Park.
Kura stood on the special stage at the Sósos Festival.
“Playing Revolution”

Sunset glow,
home-time sky,
and a chorus of slogans.

Children
go on strike
against going home.
“Hamburg steaks for dinner tonight.”

Purge
the traitors.
Self-criticism.

A fortress made
a jungle gym.
To the sound of guitar and Hōmī, Kura belted out her radical anti-Mancal song, and Leto burst into helpless laughter.
“Leto-kun, don’t just stand there laughing—help!”

The West Altos Researcher’s Club was doing brisk business selling Sashyk. The rich smell of grilled meat drew customers in one after another. Minac handled the cash box, Fàta shaped the meat onto the skewers, and Aliĕ and Leto manned the grill. Being such a small club, their work efficiency was not exactly ideal. More than that, though, all sorts of people from around Sósos had come to help them, and as a result they were seeing more customers than they could really handle.
content.html: The West Altos Researcher’s Club was doing brisk business selling Sashyk. The rich smell of grilled meat drew customers in one after another. Minac handled the cash box, Fàta shaped the meat onto the skewers, and Aliĕ and Leto manned the grill. Being such a small club, their work efficiency was not exactly ideal. More than that, though, all sorts of people from around Sósos had come to help them, and as a result they were seeing more customers than they could really handle.

content.text: “So you two can cook now after all,” Satella teased Aliĕ and Leto. “Maybe I don’t need to make breakfast anymore?”
Both of them shook their heads with violent speed. They smelled so strongly of sheep by now that the very thought of more cooking made them want to cry.
“Fàta-kun, a boy who can cook is popular, you know,” Shiyuĕ said.
She was already pleasantly drunk, taking shameless advantage of the clinic’s day off. Fàta blushed from being praised by her.
“Only chahan! Chahan only!”

Mon had a stall out as well. Her menu consisted of fried rice, and fried rice alone. It was such clean, ruthless commitment that it almost inspired awe, and her line was every bit as long as the West Altos Researcher’s Club’s.
“Minac, this is good! Sell me some for my men too!”
Busy as she was with helping oversee the festival, Shinan kept dropping by to see Minac. She was a sister who doted on her sibling down to the bone.
A little after noon, when the crowd had finally begun to thin, a white-haired woman with blue eyes arrived.
“Three Sashyk,” she said, “and one Durg coffee.”
Minac froze. The woman standing there was Hal Perort.
content.html: Minac froze. The woman standing there was Hal Perort.

content.text: Every member of the West Altos Researcher’s Club stopped what they were doing and stared.
“Three Sashyk and one Durg coffee,” Minac repeated, handing them over.
Hal tried to bite into a skewer on the spot.
“Hot.”
Aliĕ thrust a cup of water into her hands at once and guided her around to the back.
“I’m Hal Perort,” she said once they were seated. “Other than Aliĕ-kun, I suppose this is our first meeting.”
Into her Durg coffee—which was sweet enough to begin with—she dropped one sugar stick after another.
“I heard from the professor,” she said. “Aliĕ-kun, you’re entering the student debate too, aren’t you? I’m looking forward to facing you.”
She blew on her Sashyk and took a bite.
“Hal-san,” Fàta said, unable to hold himself back, “are you really in favor of the Street Business Regulation Act? Don’t you think there’s something wrong with that law?”
Leto stopped him at once.
“Don’t misunderstand,” Hal said. “I’m nonpolitical. Debate is a sport. Personal feelings should not be brought into it.”
Aliĕ took in Hal’s perfectly reasonable answer, then replied just as calmly.
“That may be true. But the student debate is different. The future of Hellén—no, the future of Har-Lopinong—is hanging on it.”
content.html: “That may be true. But the student debate is different. The future of Hellén—no, the future of Har-Lopinong—is hanging on it.”

content.text: Seeing the resolve in her face, Hal quietly rose.
“Then I’ll see you there,” she said. “And could I have two more Sashyk? Thinking makes me hungry.”
Once Minac had handed over the extra order, Hal left the back area.
“She can really eat,” Minac muttered.
She looked at Aliĕ. Aliĕ was shaking from nerves.
The next day, back at Nort, Aliĕ and Leto were practicing for the debate.
“It’s going to turn out exactly the same as last time,” Aliĕ said weakly. “Senpai Hal’s whole ‘safety’ argument is going to crush me again…”
Leto let out a sigh, kicked a chair around, and sat down in it.
“Aliĕ, what you’re saying is right. But being merely right won’t win you the debate. Debate is a contest won by the person who presents a perspective broader than the other side’s.”
Aliĕ looked up.
“A broader perspective…?”

“That’s right. Last time you fixated on a single breakthrough point: ‘street performances are freedom of expression.’ But Hal came in with ‘safety,’ a value that speaks to society as a whole. What you were trying to defend was individual freedom. What Hal was trying to defend was social order. That isn’t a fair fight.”
His words were sharp, but the smile that followed them was gentle.
“That’s exactly why this time you have to use a larger frame.”
He stood and wrote a single line in large letters across the whiteboard.
Do not eliminate the street—evolve it.
Aliĕ tilted her head.
“…Evolve it?”
“You answer the defensive logic of ‘regulation’ with a higher-order idea: design. Leap ahead and talk about how the cities of the future should handle public space.”
His pen moved again.
A lightweight permit system granting instant approval by phone.
A safety network that maps and visualizes street activity.
Urban design that turns street culture into a tourism resource.
Citizen-participation management of public space.

“If you propose this kind of innovative street infrastructure, then Hal’s old-fashioned logic of ‘just tighten things down’ starts to look stale. In other words, don’t fight on her ground. Redraw the ground itself.”
Something changed in Aliĕ’s face. The shadow of hesitation began to disappear.
Their debate training went on until the sun had set. By the time Satella came by carrying snacks, the two of them were snoring over the desk.
“So,” she murmured, “you’ve learned how to wield weapons too.”
Then she laid blankets gently over their backs.
A week later, in a boxing gym in Sósos, Tor stood alone before a punching bag.

Tor had been born in the Autonomous Parish of Dāfure in the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé, and her life had always been shadowed by oppression. In Kàlavé, women were consistently treated as lesser. On top of that, she belonged to a Deltai minority, and was always a target of oppression from the Calabar majority and the believers of the Dilar sect. She had long since concluded that violence was the only way a minority could force a majority to its knees.
content.html: Tor had been born in the Autonomous Parish of Dāfure in the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé, and her life had always been shadowed by oppression. In Kàlavé, women were consistently treated as lesser. On top of that, she belonged to a Deltai minority, and was always a target of oppression from the Calabar majority and the believers of the Dilar sect. She had long since concluded that violence was the only way a minority could force a majority to its knees.

content.text: The one who had saved her was Koto, Blue Heaven. Koto had recognized Tor’s ability and appointed her leader of an elite unit deployed against Nine Earths. In October 1992, when Nine Earths was driven back to the Kàlavé side in a major war between the two groups, Tor had been granted the rank of Farned. The truth, though, was that she had taken a savage pleasure in subduing with her own hands the very Calabar people who had tormented her in childhood.
content.html: The one who had saved her was Koto, Blue Heaven. Koto had recognized Tor’s ability and appointed her leader of an elite unit deployed against Nine Earths. In October 1992, when Nine Earths was driven back to the Kàlavé side in a major war between the two groups, Tor had been granted the rank of Farned. The truth, though, was that she had taken a savage pleasure in subduing with her own hands the very Calabar people who had tormented her in childhood.

content.text: No matter how hard she struck the punching bag, it would not fall. That only deepened her irritation.
“Lady Secmet! We’ve received word that Lady Arsnorva is on her way to Hellén Central Station to seek an audience with His Highness Yellow Heaven!”
Tor punched the subordinate who had brought her the message.
“‘Lady’ Arsnorva? She is a traitor to the organization! Crush her at once!”
Under Tor’s command, soldiers were summoned. She stared at her own bloodied fist and laughed aloud.
“Come, then. Let’s decide who stands at the top.”
At the same time, in the Hellén Central Public Hall in Elben District, the chair rang the bell before an audience of city residents.

“We will now proceed to the student debate. On the question of whether to approve or reject Proposed Revision Number Three to the Street Business Regulation Act, and on the applicable scope of the present law, speaker Hal Perort will argue in favor, while speaker Aliĕ Namile will argue in opposition.”
content.html: “We will now proceed to the student debate. On the question of whether to approve or reject Proposed Revision Number Three to the Street Business Regulation Act, and on the applicable scope of the present law, speaker Hal Perort will argue in favor, while speaker Aliĕ Namile will argue in opposition.”

content.text: As before, Hal opened calmly.
“This law is a rational means of protecting public safety and preventing illegal sales. As I explained during the deliberations over Proposal Number Two, a permit system ensures healthy activity.”
Everyone in the room assumed Hal would win again. But Aliĕ did not answer at once. Instead, she slowly took hold of the microphone.
“Safety is certainly important,” she said. “But by whose standards do you define ‘healthy activity’? The people affected by this law are not necessarily criminals.”
A stir ran through the room.

“In practice, it is the livelihoods and cultures of minorities—immigrant street vendors, foreign students performing on the street—that are quietly being pushed out by complex procedures and fees. By the logic of the majority and the convenience of the administration, the places where they work and express themselves are being shaved away. This is social selection wrapped in the paper of public safety.”
Hal’s brow twitched ever so slightly.
“But whether minority or majority,” she said, “people must act under rules. If we allow exceptions—”

“This isn’t about exceptions,” Aliĕ cut in. “The real issue is that administrative-cost arguments make procedures more complicated, and the result is a system in which the weaker a person’s position is, the less able they are to apply at all.”
She pointed to the materials laid out on the table.

“You say ‘for safety.’ But the real target of this law—the street sale of quasi-legal drugs—is hardly affected by permits in the first place. Those people were already underground. In practice, the permit system singles out only those who are trying to live legally.”
The mood of the audience turned upside down. This time, unlike before, the air in the room was being pulled along by Aliĕ’s words.
And she pressed further.

“I am not trying to deny all regulation. But your thinking begins and ends with regulation as a way to reduce danger. Street problems can no longer be solved in an age like this by removal and suppression alone.”
Her voice gained force.
“Around the world right now, more and more cities are redesigning the street as participatory civic infrastructure.

Instead of excluding street performances and street stalls, they combine public space with digital technology—introducing instant permit systems that let anyone file a simple request by phone, and using smart street maps to visualize locations and safety conditions. In other words, the idea is this: it is safer to manage things openly than to ban them.”
The hall began to buzz.

“This law relies only on the old-style logic of tightening the screws. But the cities of the future are moving toward treating visible spaces of civic creativity and culture as assets to be cultivated. This law runs completely against that tide.”

Hal opened her mouth to speak, but the rebuttal would not come. The structure by which stricter permits led to minority exclusion. The fact that the law did little against crime that had already gone underground. And above all, the innovative urban policy of developing spaces of civic activity as managed infrastructure. All of it had risen above Hal’s argument as a higher-order frame.
The moderator announced the end. Realizing the match was decided, Hal held out her hand.
“I lost,” she said. “You were looking at things from a more macro perspective than I was.”
Aliĕ shook her head.
“No,” she said. “I’m the micro one. If it’s to protect my precious friends, I’ll use whatever means I have. That’s all.”
