When the residents’ vote was counted, Hellén rejected the proposed revision to the Street Business Regulation Act and also decided to relax the scope of the existing law. The nationwide result of the Har-Lopinong Republic might still overturn that outcome, but in this country local decisions were treated with weight. Immigrants and foreign students alike celebrated.
content.html: When the residents’ vote was counted, Hellén rejected the proposed revision to the Street Business Regulation Act and also decided to relax the scope of the existing law. The nationwide result of the Har-Lopinong Republic might still overturn that outcome, but in this country local decisions were treated with weight. Immigrants and foreign students alike celebrated.

content.text: “All right, then—one song for the occasion!” Minac cried.
Back in the plaza before Hellén Central Station, Kura was holding her guitar once more. The sound of Hōmī should have filled the square again.
But Tor’s subordinates arrived first.
At the sight of Five Heavens gathering in force, the citizens who had come to the square began to scatter.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
Shinan emerged from the shadows and caught one of the men.
“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! A beautiful singing voice is under my protection!”
content.html: “My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! A beautiful singing voice is under my protection!”

content.text: She charged straight for Tor.
“Fine by me,” Tor snarled. “I’ll beat you to death!”
Shinan slipped past Tor’s straight punch and drove one into her face. At once Tor ordered her men to go after Minac.
“Not happening!”
A wooden sword cracked against one of the attackers’ heads. Fàta had arrived. He tossed a second wooden sword to Minac.

Minac’s Stance of the Moderate switched irregularly between attack and defense, disrupting her opponents’ tempo. Fàta, by contrast, still had not mastered the transition between the Stance of Savage Fury and the Stance of the Void Mind.
“Think of it as holding fire in your right hand and water in your left!” Minac shouted.

The advice was abstract, but somehow Fàta felt that he understood just a little. He began feeding emotionless strikes into the gaps between the flurries of the Stance of Savage Fury, as though sewing them shut. In that instant, he grasped the rhythm of battle whole.
At the same time, Tor’s furious assault rained down on Shinan. She allowed no opening for the Arsnorva Punch.
“This is how I won my position—with power,” Tor said. “You did the same, didn’t you?”
Shinan answered without flinching.
“I gained power to protect what I love—and I use my place for the same reason!”
She slipped Tor’s enraged fist and hurled her aside.
“New move! Arsnorva Kick!”
It was, strictly speaking, an iriminage throw and not a kick at all.
“What are you doing?” Tor screamed from the ground. “Kill every last one of them!”
At that moment the sky opened blue above them.
A rain of countless knives came pouring down, pinning Tor’s subordinates one after another. It was Koto Tál Aitēl—Blue Heaven.
content.html: A rain of countless knives came pouring down, pinning Tor’s subordinates one after another. It was Koto Tál Aitēl—Blue Heaven.

content.text: She planted a foot on Tor and addressed Shinan.
“An audience with His Highness Yellow Heaven will not be necessary. Her Eminence Scarlet Heaven has chosen your moderate line.”

Five Heavens had highly valued the success of the Sósos Festival, as well as the profits being generated by the housing complex for immigrants and foreign students that was under construction in the redevelopment district.
“And compared with that,” Koto went on, grinding her heel down, “you swaggered about how you would ‘flush out Nine Earths’—and you have produced absolutely nothing.”
Tor had stopped crying out by then. Seeing her like that, Shinan felt the terror of the organization all over again.
“Well then, goodbye. Now where ever could my dear husband be~?”
Taking Tor’s subordinates with her, Koto left the square. In that way, Shinan restored stability to Sósos once more.
That night, the West Altos Researcher’s Club gathered at Yifeng for a victory party.
“Chahan. Six. Okay?”
A white-haired, blue-eyed woman blocked Mon’s aggressive sales push without hesitation.
“One tapioca milk tea,” Hal said in Solan, “and three sweet buns, please.”
She was an elite in languages as well.
“Thank goodness somebody who understands has finally come,” Mon replied in Solan, her eyes shining. “These people are all idiots.”
“What do you think they’re saying?” Fàta asked.
He lifted a spoonful of chahan with the expression of someone who had made peace with his fate. For him, this had been the day he moved one step closer to his master, Minac.
“Well then—cheers to our two new members!”
Minac gave the toast. She had never once imagined that her club would grow this large, and she ended up crying from sheer emotion.
“Aliĕ, seriously, you did great! With this, the future’s secure,” Leto said, thumping her on the shoulder.
“Thanks to all of you, I’m really happy,” Aliĕ said, tears spilling over in turn. “It really feels like youth.”
“Since we’re all here,” Kura said, “would it be all right if I sang one song?”
She asked Hal to interpret, obtained Mon’s permission, took out her guitar, and began to sing.
“Farewells Come Suddenly”

Beyond the wind,
you set out on your journey,
still holding
memories of the sand.

Morning star,
please light the way.

Even on a road
that never returns,
I will not forget you.
Just then, a notification arrived on the phones Aliĕ and Leto were holding.
Emergency: In light of the extraordinary session convened by the Parliament of the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy, prepare at once to return home.
content.html: Emergency: In light of the extraordinary session convened by the Parliament of the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy, prepare at once to return home.

content.text: May 1995. Nort, Sósos District.

A single note had been left on top of the two futons that had long since been folded away. The four remaining members of the West Altos Researcher's Club pressed their dorm mother, Satella, for Aliĕ and Leto's whereabouts, but she would not talk. Minac wept openly. Kura held out tissues. Fàta nearly came to blows with Satella. Hal tried to pull him back. The room had gone sour with the worst kind of silence.
The four of them went to the Sósos police box. If anyone might know something, it would be Officer Darc, with his endless well of gossip.
"I do have an idea," the officer said. "But information doesn't come free."

That answer only sharpened everyone's irritation. Minac started hurling insults—"corrupt cop," "lazy bum," "fatso"—and Darc laughed at the sight of her. Fàta put a hand to his head. At a time like this, the two people who should have been their brains were, of all times, nowhere to be found.
"If a price is what you want," Hal said, unexpectedly taking the lead, "we can pay it."
Darc's eyes narrowed.
"The fixers aren't the only ones who know what happened during the days of the 'Three-Way.' If we made that public, you wouldn't get out of it clean either, would you?"
The four of them did not know that Darc was in fact an intelligence agent of the Worl Federal Preparatory Committee.
content.html: The four of them did not know that Darc was in fact an intelligence agent of the Worl Federal Preparatory Committee.

content.text: "If you want to live a long life," he said, his cheerful look vanishing, "you'd do better not to underestimate adults."
His hand slipped inside his coat. In the same instant, Fàta and Minac reached for their knives.
"Oops. Looks like I accidentally misplaced some classified information. See you around."

What Darc drew out was not a weapon, but an investigative report from the Public Security Bureau of the National Police Agency. After making sure all four had bowed their thanks, he headed off on patrol as though nothing had happened.
content.html: What Darc drew out was not a weapon, but an investigative report from the Public Security Bureau of the National Police Agency. After making sure all four had bowed their thanks, he headed off on patrol as though nothing had happened.

content.text: At Yifeng Restaurant, Mon waited without taking their order, careful not to interrupt them. Minac spread the report across the table.

"The Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy," she read, "an intelligence organization that seeks to place the rulers it deems proper upon the thrones of the world. Aliĕ and Leto were its agents."
Fàta filled in the rest. The two of them had never told their friends who they really were. The others were left with the same bruising question: why had they never trusted them with the truth?
"And Aliĕ has gone back to the Union of Eltēna to become the Princess of the Principality of Lenetiĕ," Kura continued, reading on, "while Leto has returned to become its Grand Vizier."
She, too, owed those two her life. This time, she wanted to be the one who saved them.

"The real question," Hal said, coolly sorting through the facts, "is whether they even want to come back. In any normal case, nobody could juggle offices like those with university life. And they were intelligence officers to begin with. It's entirely possible they have no unfinished feelings tying them to us at all."
Even her beloved tapioca milk tea barely diminished that day.

A heavy mood settled over the restaurant. All four of them were trapped between two things: the ideal of wanting to bring Aliĕ and Leto back, and the reality that doing so might go against what those two actually wanted.
"Then we'll go to Eltēna and find out!" Minac burst out.

It was, as usual, absurd. Eltēna was an absolute monarchy famous for tightly restricting the entry of foreigners. Anyone with the slightest grasp of world affairs knew that. Minac herself understood as much and hastily took it back. Ordinarily, Aliĕ and Leto would have been the ones to translate one of Minac's wild declarations into something actionable. They were not here now.

At Sósos West Clinic, Shiyuĕ was taking a break in the examination room with the television on when the four of them arrived. She brought out round stools, switched the channel to Kuo Hramé—Kàlavé's satellite broadcaster—and turned on subtitles in Aldia. A breaking report was running there: former First Instructor Shàun Àya had died.
content.html: At Sósos West Clinic, Shiyuĕ was taking a break in the examination room with the television on when the four of them arrived. She brought out round stools, switched the channel to Kuo Hramé—Kàlavé's satellite broadcaster—and turned on subtitles in Aldia. A breaking report was running there: former First Instructor Shàun Àya had died.

content.text: "I don't know whether it's connected or not," Shiyuĕ said, "but every West-Altos-related broadcast today seems to be this."
The four of them watched the screen closely. For one brief moment, the flag of the Union of Eltēna appeared beside a formal message of condolence from Eltēna's ambassador to Kàlavé.
"A condolence delegation!" Fàta said at once.
Kàlavé was one of the very few countries on friendly terms with Eltēna. If a former head of state had died, then of course a delegation of condolences would be sent.
"That makes sense," Hal said. "A condolence visit is political as much as ceremonial. A meeting between the newly crowned Princess of Lenetiĕ and the First Instructor of Kàlavé isn't impossible."
"Are you really thinking of going all the way to Kàlavé?" Shiyuĕ warned them. "There's no guarantee Aliĕ and Leto will even be there. And even if they are, that doesn't mean you'll get to see them."
The four bowed in a rush and were gone before she had finished. Shiyuĕ could only marvel at the reckless force of youth.
At Shinan's apartment in East Sósos, Minac threw herself to the floor.
"Please lend us the travel money!"
The sight of Minac dropping even her usual casual speech made Shinan tell her at once to lift her head.
"Right now," Shinan said, tears springing to her eyes, "I am overwhelmed. I understand, truly understand, the feelings of a club president who wants to save her friends!"
That set Minac crying too. Watching the pair of them, Hal—the operation's strategist—decided the emotional approach had worked.
"But," Shinan added, coming back to herself, "if you're going to borrow money from a member of the mafia, then show me some proper sincerity in return."
She handed them a pamphlet for an apartment complex under construction for immigrants and foreign students. She wanted the West Altos Researcher's Club to promote it.
"In that case," Kura said, reaching for her guitar, "how about I write you a commercial song?"
She improvised a melody on the spot. Shinan broke into applause. Deal done.
"One more thing," Shinan said. "Don't do anything you'll regret."
With that, she handed over the check. The four accepted it, bowed, and left.
A week passed. Àya's funeral was almost at hand. The four members of the West Altos Researcher's Club met at Hellén Central Station.
content.html: A week passed. Àya's funeral was almost at hand. The four members of the West Altos Researcher's Club met at Hellén Central Station.

content.text: "I was so excited I couldn't sleep at all last night," Minac declared, dragging along a suitcase stuffed fit to burst.
"This is not a sightseeing trip," Fàta told her.
That said, he too could not entirely hide his excitement. He was about to return to his homeland, Kàlavé, and this time not alone.
"I ended up leaving the guitar behind after all..." Kura said mournfully.
Everyone told her at once that of course she had.
"Senpai," Hal asked, "how many Bónt of sweets are we allowed to bring?"
Her own suitcase was packed to the brim with candy.
"All right then!" Minac cried. "Operation Take Back Aliĕ and Leto begins now!"
All four voices rose together. Whether Aliĕ and Leto would actually come back or not, at least their hearts were united.

Their train pulled out from Hellén Central Station and headed for Hellén International Airport on the outskirts of the city. From there they would board Foll Airlines, Kàlavé's flag carrier, for Yìlà, the largest city in the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé. Once in Yìlà, they would transfer to a domestic flight bound for the capital, Hāké. That was the plan.
content.html: Their train pulled out from Hellén Central Station and headed for Hellén International Airport on the outskirts of the city. From there they would board Foll Airlines, Kàlavé's flag carrier, for Yìlà, the largest city in the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé. Once in Yìlà, they would transfer to a domestic flight bound for the capital, Hāké. That was the plan.

content.text: "Airport security always makes you feel guilty somehow," Kura muttered, "even when you've done absolutely nothing wrong."
Among the four of them, she alone looked truly uneasy after the screening. She was Durguatan. Relations between Kàlavé and Durguatan were far from warm, and she knew it.
content.html: Among the four of them, she alone looked truly uneasy after the screening. She was Durguatan. Relations between Kàlavé and Durguatan were far from warm, and she knew it.

content.text: "You'll be fine," Fàta said. "Since the Tané administration took power, the two countries have been pursuing reconciliation. And if we pass ourselves off as mourners, getting all the way to Hāké should be easy enough."
This was precisely the sort of moment when the title "grandson of former Parliament Speaker Lakán Lài" became useful.
content.html: This was precisely the sort of moment when the title "grandson of former Parliament Speaker Lakán Lài" became useful.

content.text: "Could you teach me a little Calabar on the plane?" Hal asked him.
Her intellectual curiosity knew no limit.
"Is that ours?" Minac said, peering through the window.
Outside, an AK-777—the mainstay aircraft of Foll Airlines—was drawing up to the boarding bridge.

"Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for flying with Foll Airlines today. We will soon depart Hellén International Airport in the Republic of Har-Lopinong and make our way to Yìlà in the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé. Our flight time today is scheduled to be approximately eight hours. This is your captain, Akari Sanfán. Please sit back and enjoy the flight."
The engines roared as the plane prepared for takeoff. Kura put in her earphones and shut the world out with music. Hal stared at the in-flight menu until her stomach growled.
"Fàta-kun, can I ask you something?" Minac said.
She sounded small in a way she rarely did.
"It's my first time on a plane. I'm a little scared."
"You'll be all right," he told her. "We're all here."
They clasped hands. Their faces reddened just a little—enough to make them forget the fear of takeoff.
At the same time, at the Embassy of Eltēna in the Hāké Direct-Controlled Parish, Aliĕ stood at the window, gazing up at the sky.
"I wonder if we'll ever see them again."
Leto drew the curtains shut.
"Forget Hellén," he said. "This is our fate now."
The two of them turned away from the window, burying their memories beneath duty.
May 1995. Guest Wing of Gàtïna Grand Hall, Hāké Direct-Controlled Parish.
content.html: May 1995. Guest Wing of Gàtïna Grand Hall, Hāké Direct-Controlled Parish.

content.text: In the First State Guest Room, First Instructor Ládén Tané was in conference with Princess Aliĕ Namile of Lenetiĕ.
content.html: In the First State Guest Room, First Instructor Ládén Tané was in conference with Princess Aliĕ Namile of Lenetiĕ.

content.text: "Allow me to offer my congratulations on Your Highness's accession," Tané said. "May our two countries continue to walk together in friendship."
Having heard that Aliĕ's hobby was photography, he presented her with a camera made by Knarl of Skniune.
"You honor me with such consideration even while the country remains in mourning for the former First Instructor," Aliĕ replied.
She fought down the urge to pick up the camera and examine it. She was no longer merely a university student. She repeated that to herself and kept her face composed.
"And what do you intend to do about your studies from here on?" Tané asked.
Aliĕ faltered. Seeing that, Tané went on.
"When I was younger, I too devoted myself to inquiry. Prime Minister Akau and Second Instructor Qàlé were friends from childhood. They taught me something simple: cherish what is before you now."
Aliĕ looked out the window. What she had before her now—Fàta, Minac, Kura, Hal, and all of Hellén. Would they still accept her, now that she had become a princess? That fear would not leave her.
content.html: Aliĕ looked out the window. What she had before her now—Fàta, Minac, Kura, Hal, and all of Hellén. Would they still accept her, now that she had become a princess? That fear would not leave her.

content.text: Meanwhile, in the Second State Guest Room, Prime Minister Shàun Akau sat facing Grand Vizier Leto Lówa.
content.html: Meanwhile, in the Second State Guest Room, Prime Minister Shàun Akau sat facing Grand Vizier Leto Lówa.

content.text: "Your Excellency Akau," Leto asked abruptly, "if someone precious to you learned the truth about a secret you were keeping, what would you do?"
Akau looked momentarily taken aback. Yet her answer came easily. She too possessed a secret she could not reveal to just anyone.
"If it were me," she said, "I would trust that person. If my feelings for them weighed more than the burden of the secret, then the pain of confessing it would be lighter."
A knock came at the door. Tané and Aliĕ's meeting appeared to have ended.
Leto bowed to Akau and walked over to Aliĕ. There too stood Tot Kelnz, Director of the Security Bureau of the Ministry of the Preservation of the Monarchy.
"Your Highness Namile. Your Excellency Lówa. Do be careful not to 'misunderstand' yourselves."
The chains binding Aliĕ and Leto were stout ones. The right wing of the Ministry had stationed Tot over them like a watchdog, lest the two become any more infected with republican thinking.

The pair left the guest wing with lowered eyes, got into their car, and headed for Hāké Central Hotel. Along the way, in the bazaar of Ragwar Parish, they thought they saw the shadows of their friends. But both of them dismissed it as a mirage and shut their hearts tight.
At the same time, in Ragwar Parish, Kura was playing with local street musicians. The sound of Hōmī racing over the grasslands had made its way even into the desert of Kàlavé.
"I Look Forward to the Day We Meet Again"

Whenever the wind that crosses the desert
shakes your name,
it is as though red higanbana bloom,
and a light kindles in the depths of my chest.

Even if these days are no more than a mirage,
the warmth that lingers in my palm
becomes a map I will not lose,
and leads me toward a new world.
When the young drummer finished, Fàta applauded. He shook the musician's hand, then moved on to what he really wanted.
"We need to get past the checkpoint at Hāké Central Hotel. If you know this city well, do you know a good way in?"
The young man listened, then fetched another youth.
"He works there as a cleaner," he said. "If you want to slip in, the service gate is your best bet."
Fàta and Kura thanked them and rejoined Minac and Hal, who had been collecting information elsewhere.
"I can't do this anymore," Minac groaned. "I'm going to throw up..."

The two of them had been questioning the owner of a wildly famous kiremén shop. He had agreed to share what he knew only if they drew him a crowd. Minac had proposed a kiremén eating contest. This was the result.
"I can still do ten more," Hal said calmly.

She continued eating with total composure. The plates piled higher than Pinorl Tower testified to the abyss that was her stomach. The crowd pointed and muttered, "That's an elephant." "No, it's a vacuum cleaner." Hal won by a landslide, and seeing how deliciously she ate, the audience formed a long line at the register.

"Thank you, Miss Vacuum Cleaner!" the shopkeeper cried. "You've made me a fortune. And yes, just like you guessed, I delivered catering to the room reserved for the Princess of Lenetiĕ. The princess herself should have been there too."
They retrieved the collapsed Minac and the still-eating Hal from the crowd, then ducked into a nearby café to plan.
"So," Fàta said, opening a floor map of Hāké Central Hotel on his phone, "we disguise ourselves as cleaning staff, enter through the service gate, and make for Room 1403."
"And after that?" Hal asked.

After that—after they stood face to face with Aliĕ and Leto—what exactly were they going to say? What one wanted to say and what one ought to say were not always the same. Hal, seasoned in debate, understood that very well.
"We tell them the truth," Kura said. "That we want them to come back."
Honesty was always her method; she turned sincerity itself into song.
"We came so we wouldn't have any regrets," Minac said.
She remembered her sister's words. Even if Aliĕ and Leto chose not to return, she had already made up her mind to hold the club together so nobody would crumble.

Passing the service gate turned out to be easier than expected. With all the mourners in town, the hotel had hired extra day laborers for cleaning. Once inside, they took the service elevator to the fourteenth floor. At Room 1403, the four of them knocked as planned.
"Housekeeping, 'ssu!"
"Idiot, drop the ''ssu'!"
Fàta clapped a hand over Minac's mouth, but too late. Leto had already heard the voice. He flung the door open.
"Everyone—why are you here?"
In a hurry, he pulled the four inside. Fortunately, no one else was in the room.
"We'll say it plainly," they told him. "Please come back to Hellén—to the West Altos Researcher's Club!"
All four lowered their heads. Leto was clearly moved that they had crossed the sea for his sake, yet after a pause he straightened his tie and answered in a controlled voice.
"I hid my true identity from you. And now I am the Grand Vizier. Do you even understand what you're asking?"
Minac answered first.
"That doesn't matter! I don't care who you are or what you've become. I still haven't accepted your withdrawal form!"
Tears spilled down her face. Hal stepped in with the practical path forward.

"You still have one year of university left. If someone who serves as the Grand Vizier of an entire country also becomes a university dropout, wouldn't that do far more damage to the Principality's honor?"
Leto's heart had already tilted toward Hellén. All that remained was Aliĕ's decision.
"The place we ought to return to is Eltēna, Your Excellency Lówa."
The door opened. Aliĕ stood there with a pistol in her hand.
"'The Republic of Har-Lopinong'? There is no such country on any map of Eltēna. Which means its citizens ought not exist here, either."
Fàta drew his wooden sword. From the very start, this had been his own choice without regret.
"I don't want any West Altos Researcher's Club that doesn't have you in it. If you've got the resolve, then shoot me."
Aliĕ read the swing and stepped back. Nine months of training had borne fruit. She took distance, found her timing, and fired.
The shot struck Fàta square in the chest. He collapsed without a sound. Aliĕ collapsed too. Nobody in the room could do anything but stare.
"Princess of Lenetiĕ," Tot said coldly, turning to leave, "has that severed the temptation of republicanism at last?"
Minac stopped him. Her face held a fury so pure that all her usual playfulness seemed to have been a lie.
"Was this you? Was this you? Was this all you?"

For the sake of the club—just in case anything ever happened—she had bought a knife in the bazaar. Now she snatched it up and crossed the room in a heartbeat. Tot yanked out his pistol, but Minac knocked it away with absurd ease. Blindly, instinctively, she reproduced Fàta's natural Stance of Savage Fury, turning rage itself into the weight behind her knife.
"You little brat!"
His shout vanished beneath hers. She carved one slash into each leg, then one into each arm. Tot lost his balance. Minac threw herself on top of him and drove the point toward his throat.
"Pay for it with your life, you bastard!"
"That's enough."
The one who stopped her was Second Instructor Majén Qàlé.
content.html: The one who stopped her was Second Instructor Majén Qàlé.

content.text: "Your Highness Namile. And you as well, grandson of Chairman Lài. That's enough playacting to buy time."
Aliĕ lifted her head. Fàta rose to his feet.
"It was a BB pellet," Aliĕ said. "You know the saying—if you want to fool the enemy, start with your own side."
She and Fàta slapped hands in triumph. Minac dropped her knife and threw herself at him.
"Idiot! I was worried sick! Were you trying to make me a widow?"
She did not even know what the word meant; she said it anyway through tears. Fàta, mortified, covered his face.
"Aliĕ," Kura said, taking her hand, "you were the one who gave me the freedom to sing. This time it's our turn to give freedom back to you."

From the start, Aliĕ had intended to return to Hellén. But to do that, she had first needed to shake off Tot, the leading hardliner in the Ministry. That was why she had informed First Instructor Tané beforehand. Still, the rebellion could never have succeeded without Fàta and the others coming all the way to Hāké.

"Just so," Qàlé said. "I received word from an old friend—the woman serving as your dorm mother in Hellén. The Instructional Nation of Kàlavé will guarantee the safety of Your Highness Namile and His Excellency Lówa once they are back in Hellén."
"This is interference in domestic affairs!" Tot protested.
Qàlé stooped, picked up Tot's pistol, and answered in a flat voice.

"And what, precisely, is this? An impurity that would damage relations between Eltēna and Kàlavé. I think the Union of Eltēna will hear a very strong protest from us over it—unless, of course, you decide to save face while you still can."
Then Qàlé turned to Aliĕ.
"As the Princess of Lenetiĕ, give the order. Tell him this: you and Lord Lówa will leave for Hellén at once. The Ministry is to be informed accordingly."
Tot lowered his head and said nothing. It was the moment the children of Hellén won.
In Ima Parish, the six members of the West Altos Researcher's Club stood in the exact middle of the Bridge of Conversion at Mïsénïtíu Grand Hall.
content.html: In Ima Parish, the six members of the West Altos Researcher's Club stood in the exact middle of the Bridge of Conversion at Mïsénïtíu Grand Hall.

content.text: "Since you'd come all the way to Hāké," Qàlé said, "I wanted you to see this place."

She explained that the passage from the Parliament Tower to the Instructor Academy Tower signified not only the academic conversion of the jurists of Ismizlark, but also the country's own conversion from republicanism to Instructionalism—just as Shàun Pàlajé had once overthrown his foster brother Ládén Kïnàbé here.
content.html: She explained that the passage from the Parliament Tower to the Instructor Academy Tower signified not only the academic conversion of the jurists of Ismizlark, but also the country's own conversion from republicanism to Instructionalism—just as Shàun Pàlajé had once overthrown his foster brother Ládén Kïnàbé here.

content.text: "So this is where Kïnàbé fell?" Minac said, eyes bright. "How in the world did he survive that?"

She and Fàta stared at the historic structure with undisguised excitement. Kura, thinking of Pàlajé, began scribbling lyrics into her notebook. Hal noticed something else: the Instructor Academy Tower was in fact slightly taller, yet from Gàtïna Grand Hall perspective made the two towers appear exactly level.
"I suppose," Leto said, stepping toward the Instructor Academy side, "that after all, this is where we ought to stand."
"Yes," Aliĕ answered. "That's our homeland—Eltēna. But..."
She looked back toward the other four, standing nearer the Parliament Tower.
"Isn't it this balance that holds the whole structure up?"
The setting sun lit Mïsénïtíu Grand Hall in gold. The shadows of the two towers stretched long across the bridge, and in that light they were the same height.
June 1995. Sósos West Clinic, Sósos District, Republic of Har-Lopinong.
Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei had turned on the television, stuffing the dead air of the afternoon with information.
content.html: Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei had turned on the television, stuffing the dead air of the afternoon with information.

content.text: Most of what came through her door were psychiatric cases. Many of the immigrants and foreign students living in this city had been driven half-ill by poverty and strain. In the story called the Republic of Har-Lopinong, people like them were no more than supporting players there to make the protagonists shine. Shiyuĕ had long since taught herself to look at that fact without flinching.

A breaking report cut into the news. A new geyser had erupted at Eamil Lake, on the outskirts of Hellén Direct-Controlled City. Eamil Lake was famous as a natural hot spring, a place that drew tourists because warm water welled up from its depths. But it was also an ill-starred lake. The last time geysers had erupted there, an extremely lethal pathogen known as the Glúck virus had seeped in through the underground watercourses, killing many and earning the lake a reputation as the source of the city's endemic curse. Even so, thanks to the spread of the Mob Vaccine, the terror of the Glúck virus was supposed to belong to the past.
content.html: A breaking report cut into the news. A new geyser had erupted at Eamil Lake, on the outskirts of Hellén Direct-Controlled City. Eamil Lake was famous as a natural hot spring, a place that drew tourists because warm water welled up from its depths. But it was also an ill-starred lake. The last time geysers had erupted there, an extremely lethal pathogen known as the Glúck virus had seeped in through the underground watercourses, killing many and earning the lake a reputation as the source of the city's endemic curse. Even so, thanks to the spread of the Mob Vaccine, the terror of the Glúck virus was supposed to belong to the past.

content.text: Shiyuĕ switched off the set and resumed her afternoon consultations. The boredom began again.
At the same time, at Hellén National University, Aliĕ and Fàta were studying for their final exams in the campus café. Ethics 1B handouts lay scattered all across the table.
content.html: At the same time, at Hellén National University, Aliĕ and Fàta were studying for their final exams in the campus café. Ethics 1B handouts lay scattered all across the table.

content.text: “Among the liberties argued for by Vakranalt Leitnashe Lalpel, what do you call ‘the individual’s right to make a choice without interference, even if others think that choice foolish’?”
Aliĕ put the question to him. Fàta raked his fingers through his hair and thought.
“Time’s up. The answer is ‘the right to folly.’”
She showed him the card with the answer. Fàta was brilliant with the sword and miserable in the classroom.
“Must be nice,” he grumbled. “You’re the Princess of Lenetiĕ. I still have to take the civil-service exam.”

In Kàlavé, there were two examinations for those aiming at police bureaucracy. One was the regular civil-service examination of the Instructional Nation. The other was the Higher Catechization Examination. Fàta intended to sit for the latter, the more difficult of the two, because he meant to rise to the same rank his father Sài had once held.
“I can’t neglect study either,” Aliĕ said. “Not if I’m going to become the ruler I ought to be.”

With that, she flicked through her stack of vocabulary cards. Being a princess and keeping up with schoolwork was no easy thing, but if she wanted to become an enlightened sovereign, she needed both. The two of them had already promised each other that they would walk that thorny road side by side until their dreams took shape.

Three hours later, back at Sósos West Clinic, Kura, Hal, and Dahia Opfar—former president of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, now a fourth-year student—had come in for vaccinations ahead of their trip to Eamil Lake the following week.
content.html: Three hours later, back at Sósos West Clinic, Kura, Hal, and Dahia Opfar—former president of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, now a fourth-year student—had come in for vaccinations ahead of their trip to Eamil Lake the following week.

content.text: “So the girl over there’s a new face,” said Shiyuĕ.

She was giving Kura her shot while glancing over at Dahia. Dahia had drifted away from the club when she entered her fourth year, but once graduation was safely in sight she had started showing up again, wanting to leave herself with a few good memories.

“My family was poor,” Dahia said. “I worked part-time all the time, so I hardly ever showed my face. But the West Altos Researcher’s Club was loose about all that. It was one of the only places I had.”

She said her clothes had all been hand-me-downs from her older sister, and that she had always bought used textbooks. Deeply moved by such tearful effort, Shiyuĕ mercilessly plunged a needle into Hal’s arm.
“Ow.”
Hal covered the injection mark with watery eyes. Shiyuĕ immediately told her not to touch it. While that was going on, Kura noticed something in the examination room and spoke up.
“You became a lead researcher at the National Institute of Medical Science? Congratulations!”
A newspaper clipping celebrating Shiyuĕ’s promotion had been pinned to the wall.
“Well, I suppose there was some reward in sticking with it this long.”
But her status and reputation still did nothing at all for the happiness of the patients in this town. She remained as empty as ever.
“Anyway,” Shiyuĕ said, taking Dahia by the wrist and reaching to roll up her sleeve.
Dahia slapped her hand away and hurriedly covered her arm.
“I’m fine. I’ve already had mine.”

For the briefest instant, Shiyuĕ caught sight of an angel tattoo. It was the emblem of Sait, a new religious sect. Sait believers refused injections on grounds of faith. Understanding everything at once, Shiyuĕ did not press the matter. She only waved the three of them out.
“‘The right to folly,’ huh.”
She let out a breath and called in the next patient.

At the same time, outside the office building at Hellén National University, students who had reluctantly answered Mancal’s “call for support” were marching in demonstration, demanding lower tuition. At the tail end of the line, Leto and Minac walked along looking thoroughly bored.
“In times like these, they know the university’s already about to lower tuition,” Leto muttered. “This whole thing’s a fixed match.”

Minac hurriedly clapped a hand over his mouth before anyone from Mancal could hear. At the head of the procession was Aryany Opfar, Dahia’s father and the successor to Ágre Yall. This time it really was an actual middle-aged man calling himself a student, and just as before, the university was pretending not to notice.
content.html: Minac hurriedly clapped a hand over his mouth before anyone from Mancal could hear. At the head of the procession was Aryany Opfar, Dahia’s father and the successor to Ágre Yall. This time it really was an actual middle-aged man calling himself a student, and just as before, the university was pretending not to notice.

content.text: “This has nothing to do with anything,” Minac whispered, “but what kind of girl do you think Fàta-kun likes?”

Leto nearly choked. Minac’s confession had come out of nowhere. She was shrinking into herself, uncertain and fidgety. Searching for the right thing to say, he remembered what had happened at the Sósos Festival.
“He blushed when Dr. Shiyuĕ praised him.”
Minac sagged in disappointment. So Fàta liked mature older women.
“That’s not what I meant,” Leto said quickly. “You’re two years older than Fàta. I was saying maybe you could aim for mature older-woman territory yourself.”
At once Minac brightened. Restored by sheer momentum, she joined in the chant with the rest of the marchers.
“Really!? You think I could be a mature older woman!? Yes! Lower the tuition! Get the president out here!”
Leto quietly let out a breath. Even diplomatic expedients, apparently, were part of a grand vizier’s work.
That night, the ladies of Sósos had gathered at Yifeng Restaurant. Satella was already thoroughly drunk and pestering Mon in Solan.
“Mon! Bring me the most expensive liquor in the place and the cheapest one too! I’m making an average-value cocktail!”
Mon calmly set down a glass of water instead.
“Hey, ‘model prisoner.’ If you screw up before your suspended sentence is over and get tossed back in the clink, Aliĕ and Leto are going to end up living here and working in the restaurant.”
Shinan, the only one at the table who did not understand Solan, was drinking Neskel. She said she stayed away from alcohol to set a good example for healthy young people.
“Mon, another bowl of fish-powder noodles. Patrol made me starving.”

Mon treated Shinan with obvious warmth and, as always, ignored the order completely and brought her fried rice instead. Shinan accepted it with generous good humor. As long as it filled her stomach, she had no complaints.
Unlike usual, Shiyuĕ was not drinking much. If she got too drunk, she felt she might let today’s “confidentiality obligation” slip. She was carrying the burden by herself.
“What do you think ‘happiness’ even is?” she asked at last, half to herself.
Satella answered without a moment’s pause.
“What we’ve got right now, obviously! Aliĕ and Leto came back, that idiot law got repealed, and the hardliners in Five Heavens are gone. Tell me there’s a happier Hellén than this!”

Shiyuĕ gave a faint nod and poured more liquor down her throat as though trying to convince herself. As a doctor, she had restored “happiness” to Satella’s story. But because she was a doctor, she also knew too much. What about the supporting cast suffering in the gaps between scenes? Were they supposed to go unrewarded forever?
By the time the party had worn itself out, the alcohol had barely touched her. She left first and returned alone to her cheap apartment in West Sósos.
A Sait recruitment flyer had been pushed into her mailbox.
“Life is tragedy. Only tragedy chooses you as its protagonist.”
Who would be foolish enough to read a thing like that and convert? Yet she remembered, unwillingly, the small warmth that had risen in her chest when she heard Dahia speak of poverty and hardship.
Shiyuĕ stepped into the shower and let the water cool her head.
I’m a doctor, she told herself. My happiness has to be ethical.
Her emaciated body reflected back at her from the mirror. She dressed, ate a meal-replacement ration, and went to bed.
That night, the ceiling felt lower than usual.
June 1995. Eamil Lake, on the outskirts of Hellén Direct-Controlled City.
content.html: June 1995. Eamil Lake, on the outskirts of Hellén Direct-Controlled City.

content.text: The seven members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club had changed into swimsuits and were enjoying a day at the lakeside.
content.html: The seven members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club had changed into swimsuits and were enjoying a day at the lakeside.

content.text: “It’s warm, isn’t it... Fàta-kun thinks so too, right?”
Minac sidled up against him. In order to help her “win him over,” Aliĕ and the others had presented her with an adult-looking swimsuit that left rather little to the imagination.
“Yes,” Fàta said. “It reminds me of the hot springs back home in Kàlavé.”
He was thinking of the hot springs of his homeland. Everyone watching sighed inwardly. It might still take some time before Minac’s feelings ever reached him.
“There is data suggesting that bathing improves workplace efficiency,” Leto remarked. “Eltēna should consider introducing the practice as well.”
Even while soaking in the lake, he was reading papers on a waterproof phone and reflecting on the efficacy of bathing. His browser tabs were packed with information for the governance of a state.
“Senpai, right now you’re in student mode, so put the phone away!”
Aliĕ fired a water pistol at him. Leto instantly returned fire, but the sense of timing Fàta had taught her served her too well. In the end, Leto was the one who got soaked from one side only.
“Twenty springwater buns, and coffee. Extra milk and sugar.”

Hal had skipped the water entirely and devoted herself to the food stalls. Earlier, Kura had told her that swimsuits made her stomach stand out, and Hal was already combing through obesity papers on her phone so she could strike back later.
“I wrote a song for the doctor who always takes care of us,” Kura announced. “Please listen.”
Together with a troupe of rainbow-colored performers from the western coast of Worl, she sent the sound of Hōmī ringing out along the shore.
content.html: Together with a troupe of rainbow-colored performers from the western coast of Worl, she sent the sound of Hōmī ringing out along the shore.

content.text: “Beyond the Line”

Beyond the line, you fight alone.
I send this message out to you.

Don’t carry someone else’s pain
all by yourself.
Your pain is my pain too.

Even if you go far away,
please don’t forget—
your place is here.
Toward evening, Dahia started to climb out of the lake when Aliĕ and the others called to her.
At that moment she slipped and fell. One of the nearby rainbow-clad performers caught her before it got worse, but she still scraped her leg.
“Senpai! Are you okay!?”
Minac and the others rushed to her side.
“I’m fine,” Dahia said. “It’s just a scrape.”
She headed with them toward the changing rooms.
The encroachment of “happiness” had already begun.

At the same time, at the Hellén National Institute of Medical Science, Shiyuĕ was in a meeting with officials from Har-Lopinong’s Health Ministry concerning approval for Tránen, a treatment for the Glúck virus.
“What do you mean, it can’t be approved!?”
Her palm came down hard on the desk. Completing a treatment for the Glúck virus—Hellén’s endemic disease—had been supposed to be the first real step toward saving her city.

“The problem,” a ministry official replied flatly, “is that there are too few comparable studies. Many of the available reports are observational studies with low levels of evidence. No large-scale randomized controlled trial has yet demonstrated efficacy.”

Their reasoning was sound, as far as it went. But that was not the whole truth. A cheap drug like Tránen would never earn a pharmaceutical company much profit. As things stood, that meant no one had either the money or the incentive to carry out the kind of large-scale trials required.
“And what is the state doing,” Shiyuĕ pressed, “about the geyser eruption at Eamil Lake the other day?”
“It is true that Glúck virus was detected in underground water there in the past,” the official said, “but the degree of movement was extremely slight.”
Of course it was. The Glúck virus activated only under narrowly warm conditions—hot water, the human body. It did not spread easily in Har-Lopinong’s cold climate.
“In any case,” the official concluded, “that does not overturn the reasons I stated earlier.”
It was a perfectly logical answer. Shiyuĕ was a doctor. She saved lives. She did not possess the talent this bureaucrat had for defeating people by argument.
“...Understood.”

She sat back down. A savage helplessness seized her. That official, she herself—both of them had simply been assigned their roles. No protagonist appeared during the intermission. She let herself go numb.
A week later, on a day of heavy snow, an emergency patient was brought into Sósos West Clinic.
Female, twenty-two years old. Dahia Opfar. High fever. Consciousness clouded.
content.html: Female, twenty-two years old. Dahia Opfar. High fever. Consciousness clouded.

content.text: The PCR test came back exactly as Shiyuĕ had feared: positive for Glúck virus.

After administering the bare minimum of emergency treatment, Shiyuĕ began searching for a larger hospital. L’Kork National University Hospital in the capital would have been the best option, but with this snowfall there was no getting there. Hellén National University Hospital was much closer—and the National Institute of Medical Science had “that.”
content.html: After administering the bare minimum of emergency treatment, Shiyuĕ began searching for a larger hospital. L’Kork National University Hospital in the capital would have been the best option, but with this snowfall there was no getting there. Hellén National University Hospital was much closer—and the National Institute of Medical Science had “that.”

content.text: Once Dahia had been loaded aboard the ambulance, still strapped to the stretcher, Shiyuĕ climbed in after her.
“Dahia! Can I give you an injection? An injection!”
She had to shout over the siren.
“I want... to go... to Kàlavé... with everyone...”
Dahia’s voice was no more than a thread.
“You can go! You can! So let me give you the injection!”
But Dahia could no longer hear her. Even so, Shiyuĕ kept shouting.
Then the ambulance lurched to a stop.

In the middle of this blizzard, of all times, Mancal and Five Heavens had clashed in the road ahead. Shiyuĕ told the driver the detour to take, then jumped out of the vehicle and ran toward the Mancal line herself. She wanted one thing only: permission from Dahia’s father, Aryany, to administer an injection of an unapproved drug.

“Mr. Aryany Opfar! I’m Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei of Sósos West Clinic. Your daughter, Ms. Dahia Opfar, has contracted the Glúck virus. She is in grave danger. I want to begin treatment immediately, but the drug has not yet been approved. Please—please give me permission to inject it!”
content.html: “Mr. Aryany Opfar! I’m Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei of Sósos West Clinic. Your daughter, Ms. Dahia Opfar, has contracted the Glúck virus. She is in grave danger. I want to begin treatment immediately, but the drug has not yet been approved. Please—please give me permission to inject it!”

content.text: Shiyuĕ dropped to the ground and bowed to him.
Aryany said no.
He shook off the doctor clinging to him and kicked her away without the slightest mercy.
“Dahia has begun walking the Heavenly Way. From here onward, this is her finest stage. Please do not interfere.”
There was an angel tattoo on his arm. Dahia had been born into Sait as a second-generation believer.
“Shiyuĕ! Are you all right!?”
Shinan came running.
“Shinan, I need a favor. Make that piece-of-trash father say the words ‘Give her the injection.’”
Shinan listened, nodded once, and raised her fists. After seeing Shiyuĕ run off after the ambulance, she sprang at Aryany.
“I heard from a friend who runs a dorm that you’re scared of shots. It’s bad enough being selfish yourself. Don’t force it on everybody else.”
Aryany’s weapon was a sickle. Once he caught you, he would reap the arm clean off. Shinan dodged in wide motions, careful never to brush the blade, narrowing the distance little by little.
“The virus is the angel that summons my daughter onto the tragic stage. To use a medicine that would murder that angel—such a thing can never be permitted!”
His mad stroke came down as though to drag Shinan into his world. She redirected it and tried to flow into an entering throw.
Then Aryany kicked up snow.
A feint.
The sickle slashed across Shinan’s arm. As she grimaced in pain, he moved at once to follow with another blow.
“Sorry, Shiyuĕ. I don’t think there’s much hope for treating this guy’s brain.”
Shinan smashed her forehead into his face, pinned him under her weight, and pounded him one blow at a time while shouting:
“Say it! Say, ‘Give her the injection’!”
“We are the protagonists of tragedy!”
The exchange continued until Aryany finally lost consciousness.
Three hours later, at Hellén National University Hospital, Dahia’s life had been maintained by the narrowest margin in intensive care.
But there was no time left.
Shinan dragged Aryany into the waiting room and kept asking the same question over and over, yet he answered like a broken record, parroting doctrine and nothing else.
“He’s no use,” Shinan muttered. “So then...”
She could not bring herself to say it. She could not say, “Then just give it to her.”

Under Chapter 13, Section 2 of the Third Amended Basic Medical Act, if the head of household explicitly refuses a medical procedure on behalf of an unconscious patient, that procedure must not be carried out.
Shinan had not known the law. Even so, it took her no effort at all to imagine whose responsibility that would become.
Then the six members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club came running in.
“How is senpai!?” Minac cried first.
Shiyuĕ explained Dahia’s condition to all of them—everything except the matter of the treatment drug.
“Maybe... maybe it was my fault for saying we should go to Eamil Lake,” Kura whispered, eyes downcast.
No, Shiyuĕ thought. It was my fault. I knew, and still I did not urge the injection there on the spot. I did not stop her. Yet she soothed Kura instead.
“Dr. Shiyuĕ, please,” Aliĕ and Leto said, bowing their heads together. “Please save senpai.”
At that moment, Shiyuĕ felt as if someone had draped the sash of the protagonist across her shoulders. It was too heavy, far too heavy, for someone cast only as a supporting role.
The next day, Dahia’s condition improved with startling speed.
But she did not wake.

The initial treatment had come too late. Even so, once she had been moved to a general ward, the members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club gathered around her and thanked Shiyuĕ from the bottom of their hearts for saving her life, if only that much.
Their voices did not reach her.
Neither the happy ending Aliĕ and the others wanted, nor the bad ending Aryany had desired—Shiyuĕ had granted neither. Above all, she herself remained unfulfilled.
Because she had crossed the line as a doctor and injected the treatment on her own authority.
The day after that, a woman with scarlet eyes came to see Dahia: Arka Marduk. She said she had once been Dahia’s senpai.
Seeing Shiyuĕ keeping vigil beside the unconscious girl in a haze of defeat, Arka spoke softly.
“Dahia-kun was a second-generation believer of Sait. Sait refuses injections on religious grounds. And yet she managed to come back from death’s edge. Curious, isn’t it?”
Shiyuĕ’s knee began to bounce without her willing it to. Arka’s presence was impossible to read.
“The National Institute of Medical Science was developing a new drug against the Glúck virus. The Health Ministry refused to approve it.”
Arka stepped closer.
“Sait is making one tremendous mistake. Life is tragedy—yes, that part is true enough. But then jump off the stage. Take the megaphone into your own hands.”
She held out a memo with the location of Sait’s hideout on it, as though throwing a knife.
At that moment, Dr. Shiyuĕ Lei died.
A week later, the police surrounded Sait’s hideout. The sect had been conspiring with Mancal to commit acts of terror.
“People of Sait! Warrants have been issued for your arrest on suspicion of preparation for insurrection! Surrender at once!”
The believers answered the police warning with gunfire. When the special unit stormed the building, the air filled with the death-cries of the faithful.
One man was dragged out from inside.
Aryany Opfar had resisted to the very end as one of the ringleaders, and the special unit had riddled him with bullets.
Waiting for him in the medical tent was the mad scientist Shiyuĕ Lei.

“I have always sympathized deeply with the tragedy you speak of,” she said. “So I wrote a script. A Change of Directors. I shall take the megaphone from your old director—that is, from the god you people believe in.”
Without the slightest hesitation, she drove a syringe into Aryany.
“This was once developed in Valaria: a shoddy doping agent. For a brief while it grants bottomless strength through forced excitation. Later, its side effects reduce the user to ruin.”
content.html: “This was once developed in Valaria: a shoddy doping agent. For a brief while it grants bottomless strength through forced excitation. Later, its side effects reduce the user to ruin.”

content.text: Shiyuĕ removed his handcuffs. Aryany burst from the tent with terrifying force.
“Live or die, as you please. I’ll leave the ad-lib to you.”
Her laughter rang high enough to drown out the gunfire.
“Our transaction is complete,” said Officer Darc.
He helped Shiyuĕ into a vehicle. As her “stage usage fee,” she handed over confidential documents from the National Institute of Medical Science.
The next day, back at Sósos West Clinic—
“You seem better somehow,” Aliĕ said.
Shiyuĕ’s complexion was brighter than before. Aliĕ noticed it at once.
“My work at the Institute of Medical Science has become rather fulfilling,” Shiyuĕ said. “I’m sorry, but I probably won’t be able to show my face here very often from now on.”
She passed a calendar to Aliĕ and Leto with future consultation dates marked on it.
“Oh, right—this is a CD of the song Kura wrote for you, Doctor.”
Shiyuĕ accepted the disc, slipped it into the player, and pressed play. The sound of Hōmī spread through the examination room.
“It’s a good song,” she said. “Very good.”
Kura’s singing would never reach the Shiyuĕ who had already stepped outside the stage.
Even so, she kept singing.

Even if you go far away,
please don’t forget—
your place is here.
August 1995. Hellén National University Hospital.
content.html: August 1995. Hellén National University">Hellén National University Hospital.

content.text: Arka “Latia” Melduĕk wrapped both hands around the hand of Dahia Opfar, who lay in her bed, and spoke to her gently.
content.html: Arka “Latia” Melduĕk wrapped both hands around the hand of Dahia Opfar, who lay in her bed, and spoke to her gently.

content.text: “You were the protagonist. You were dazzled by your own brilliance.”

Arka remembered those radiant days.
At the center of them, there had always been Dahia.

June 1990. Hellén National University.
Food stalls lined the campus. It was the day of the university festival. At the tent of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, students from a generation older than Dahia’s were busily grilling Sashyk.
content.html: June 1990. Hellén National University. Food stalls lined the campus. It was the day of the university festival. At the tent of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, students from a generation older than Dahia’s were busily grilling Sashyk.

content.text: “Why does someone of Deltai royal blood have to stand here grilling skewered meat from those Durguatan louts!?”
content.html: “Why does someone of Deltai royal blood have to stand here grilling skewered meat from those Durguatan louts!?”

content.text: Els Ma N'Garsha, a graduate student, turned the meat over the fire in mounting irritation. He was a royal of the Ma N'Garsha dynasty of Deltai.
content.html: Els Ma N'Garsha, a graduate student, turned the meat over the fire in mounting irritation. He was a royal of the Ma N'Garsha dynasty of Deltai.

content.text: “Master, your grilling is amazing! I want to be just like you!”

Second-year student Shinan Arsnorva laid the flattery on thick. At that point she was still a mere underling in Five Heavens, but the Pàlajé’s Knife incident would bring her into the spotlight, and after her release she would win the rank of Farned.
content.html: Second-year student Shinan Arsnorva laid the flattery on thick. At that point she was still a mere underling in Five Heavens, but the Pàlajé’s Knife incident would bring her into the spotlight, and after her release she would win the rank of Farned.

content.text: “Els-senpai’s are overdone, and yours are half-raw, Shinan! All you have to do is match the sample photo! It should be easy, shouldn’t it!?”

Third-year student Yuĕm Harc shoved up his glasses and glared at the two of them. He was the current president of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, the man who had rebuilt the ghost club Arka had originally founded.
content.html: Third-year student Yuĕm Harc shoved up his glasses and glared at the two of them. He was the current president of the West Altos Researcher’s Club, the man who had rebuilt the ghost club Arka had originally founded.

content.text: “Three Sashyk and one Durgu coffee—right this way!”
First-year student Dahia was handling the customers with bright good humor. She was the club’s promising newcomer, and thanks to her natural kindness she had become its center almost immediately.
“On the point of ‘what could possibly be enjoyable about this,’ I fully agree with Prince Els. I’m only helping because there’s staff food.”
Graduate student Arka “Latia” Melduĕk sat there pressing meat onto skewers. The skewers she assembled came out in oddly crooked shapes.

Els made fun of Arka’s skewers, Shinan piled on, Yuĕm barked at them to follow the example, and Dahia smiled.
It was, by any ordinary definition, a happy stretch of time.
Arka alone could not quite feel it.
“Ah—sorry!”
Dahia gave a little cry. She had spilled coffee on a customer. Instantly she offered her handkerchief and tried to refund the money.
“It’s all right. I came because I wanted to see Arka’s friends.”
Estiĕ Shmelmalk was a young man who worked at a flower shop in Sósos. That flower shop was where he had met Arka, and where Arka had fallen in love at first sight.
content.html: Estiĕ Shmelmalk was a young man who worked at a flower shop in Sósos. That flower shop was where he had met Arka, and where Arka had fallen in love at first sight.

content.text: “Arka’s!? That nasty little loner had other friends!?”
Els sounded genuinely astonished, so Arka jabbed him in the cheek with her meat-scented hand.
“I want to hear about Arka-senpai’s friend too!”
Shinan hustled Arka into the back.
“After hearing Arka talk about it, I got interested in the club.”
Estiĕ picked up a wooden fox carving and examined it. He said he was fascinated by West Altos—Skniune in particular—and by the rocket components made there. In other words, he was a space nerd.
“Intercollegiate members are technically not allowed. But we’re loose about that kind of thing here, so it probably won’t get found out.”
Making sure Yuĕm could not hear, Dahia cupped a hand beside Estiĕ’s ear and whispered. While the two of them laughed together, Arka pressed the meat onto her skewer a shade too hard.
July 1990. The crack of wooden swords rang through the university gymnasium.

Yuĕm, in the Stance of the Void Mind, was taking on Els and Estiĕ at the same time. He had attended a neighborhood sword school since childhood. Els had at least been taught the blade as a royal; Estiĕ’s self-taught swordsmanship could not begin to measure up.
Yuĕm disarmed Estiĕ with effortless ease. Shinan took the fallen Estiĕ by the hand.
“He’s a monster. There’s no way anybody beats him.”
Estiĕ had his head down. Shinan leaned in and said,
“Master’s real strength starts now.”
Yuĕm opened the distance and raised his bokken again.
“Els-senpai, you know, it doesn’t have to be ‘this.’”
At those words, Els snapped a kick upward. Bare-knuckle fighting was his true specialty.
“To hell with that! Put the fire in your limbs and let it fly!”
Els’s sharp straight punched deep into Yuĕm’s abdomen. The speed of that blow outstripped even Yuĕm’s cut. As Yuĕm lost his balance, Els sprang at him.
But in a motion so natural it looked as though Els were rushing onto the strike of his own accord, Yuĕm quietly reset his bokken.
“That’s the match! Victory goes to Yuĕm-senpai!”
Shinan grabbed both their hands and raised them.
At the same time, in front of the club-building row—
“The Moderate Party regime is a totalitarian regime! Stop exporting war!”

Ágre Yall stood there with a loudspeaker, shouting. It was the midday one-man Mancal performance for which Hellén National University was notorious. The usual custom was to look away and stay uninvolved.
Only one person ever broke that rule.
content.html: Ágre Yall stood there with a loudspeaker, shouting. It was the midday one-man Mancal performance for which Hellén National University was notorious. The usual custom was to look away and stay uninvolved. Only one person ever broke that rule.

content.text: “May I say something? Your struggle is remarkably deficient in class perspective. Petty bourgeois, if I had to put a name to it. No matter what you say, it sounds like positional talk from intellectuals.”
Arka had started a mock debate. Dahia tried to stop her, but the warning never reached her ears.

“For that matter, why are you lot, who call for violent revolution, so preoccupied with the framework of parliament? Isn’t parliament itself a product of bourgeois democracy, and therefore something to be overthrown?”

Ágre’s face turned bright red, but Arka went on regardless. Reinforcements were just beginning to emerge from the club rooms when Dahia suddenly clutched her head. Supporting her, Arka withdrew from the scene.
“Running away, are you? Coward!”
At Ágre’s parting sneer, Arka turned and stuck out her tongue.
In a café on campus, Arka rubbed Dahia’s back as Dahia fought to get her breathing under control, then handed her some water.
“If you hadn’t collapsed back there, I would have won decisively. Honestly—what happened to you?”
Arka poured an absurd quantity of milk and sugar into her own coffee.
“...My father was there.”

Dahia finally spoke.
Her father, Aryany Opfar, had abandoned his home life and thrown himself into the student movement. Her mother had fallen into despair over that and become devoted to the new religious movement Sait. Dahia herself had been raised in the faith from the time she first became aware of the world, and had taken all of that for normal.
“There’s nothing for you to fear,” Arka said. “You’re truly radiant. If this were a story, you’d be the protagonist.”
Dahia lifted her face and drained the water.
“To me, senpai, you’re the one who looks like a protagonist. You were brave back there too.”

Arka drank her coffee.
It tasted cloyingly sweet.

A week later, at Eamil Lake on the outskirts of Hellén Direct-Controlled City.
For the students of Hellén, it was one of the few leisure spots they had.
“Everybody, don’t skip your stretches.”
Yuĕm alone was carefully going through his warm-up exercises.
“Master, let’s play volleyball!”
Shinan spiked the ball with terrifying force and sent it smashing straight into Els.
“You idiot! Who exactly are you aiming at!?”
Els hurled the ball back, but Shinan let it slide past her so smoothly that it flew on and hit Estiĕ standing behind her.
“...Fine, then. I’ll crush every last one of you.”
Before long, all three of them had swollen faces. Under a parasol, Arka and Dahia watched and laughed.
“Senpai, which one of them do you like?”
Arka was thrown by the abruptness of the question.
“‘Like,’ is it? I don’t stake myself on uncertain phenomena like that.”
Yet even as she said it, her eyes had fixed on one young man.
“Really? I’m perfectly sure. I like Estiĕ-san.”
Dahia began listing all the things she liked about him: how pure and straightforward he was, how gentle. Once she began, she could not stop.
“Arka-senpai? Is something wrong?”
Arka drained her coffee and turned back toward Dahia.
“It’s time to pack up.”
Scarlet clouds were gathering under the red evening sky.
August 1990. Yifeng Restaurant.
“Let me introduce you. Mon Harc—my younger sister.”
content.html: “Let me introduce you. Mon Harc—my younger sister.”

content.text: Yuĕm sent Mon out from the kitchen. At the time, she was still in high school.
“What, order? Fried rice?”
The members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club each placed their orders, and Yuĕm immediately began cooking himself. As long as he was on the floor, it did not look as though Mon would get a turn.
“The roast pork you make is incredible. I can never reproduce it at home.”
Estiĕ lavished praise on Yuĕm’s cooking.
“Do you cook too, Estiĕ-san? I want to try your cooking sometime!”
Shinan was stuffing roast pork into her mouth at great speed.
“Exactly! Some slovenly woman around here could stand to learn from you!”
Els shot Arka a glance. Arka glared right back.
“Senpai, I need your advice.”
Arka moved with Dahia to a two-person table and listened.
“My older brother was injured in an accident at work. He can’t work anymore. But I still have younger brothers and sisters to support.”
Dahia went on.
“I love this club. I really do. But I have to support my family. What should I do?”
When she had finished, Arka asked a question of her own.
“What is it that you want?”
Dahia looked over at Estiĕ, chatting and laughing at the next table.
“There’s still something I haven’t done.”
Arka drove her fork into a piece of roast pork. For an instant the uproar of the restaurant seemed to cut out.
“I see.”
She chewed slowly, her scarlet eyes pinning Dahia in place.
“Regret will come either way. And you already possess one kind of happiness: your siblings. Chasing two hares will be difficult.”
Dahia lowered her gaze. Arka reached for another slice of roast pork. At the next table, the laughter continued as though nothing had changed.

That night became the last supper of the West Altos Researcher’s Club.
Dahia filed for a leave of absence and decided to devote herself to supporting her family. The next time she would show her face at the club was two years later, long after Arka and the rest had already gone.
August 1990. A movie theater in Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City.
content.html: August 1990. A movie theater in Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City.

content.text: Arka and Estiĕ had started seeing each other. The first step had come from Estiĕ himself, unable to stand watching Arka fumble and fail whenever she tried to put her feelings into words.

“‘Enraged by mankind’s overfishing, the man-eating sturgeon has begun its invasion of Earth! The key to saving the world is a nuclear missile Varalia was supposed to have sealed away during the Cold War! Human sin and human sin now collide!’”
“And what, exactly, was that choice of film?”
Arka sat holding the leftover caramel popcorn all by herself and eating it by the handful.
“The launch sequence for the missile was realistic, wasn’t it? The Polar Space Agency supervised it.”
Estiĕ’s eyes were shining as he gave his review.
At the concession shop, the two of them browsed the merchandise.
“This stuffed sturgeon is rather cute.”
Arka picked it up, then discovered the grotesque gimmick that made caviar spill out from its belly and quietly returned it to the shelf.
For once, Estiĕ’s shopping basket contained nothing but the program booklet.
“I’m short on money these days.”
At that, Arka slipped a pair of matching “Mr. and Mrs. Sturgeon” straps into the basket.
“A present from me.”
Saying that, she took the basket, paid for everything, and afterward the two of them split the straps between them.

Two weeks later, once Dahia’s absence had become undeniable, another blow fell: Estiĕ disappeared as well.
In order to repay a debt he owed to Mikarla, the Hellén mafia, he had entered Mikarla’s world himself. Perhaps he was trying to spare Shinan, who belonged to the rival Five Heavens, an awkward position.
The members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club were summoned by Yuĕm to Yifeng Restaurant. It was the restaurant’s day off. None of the usual cheer remained.
content.html: The members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club were summoned by Yuĕm to Yifeng Restaurant. It was the restaurant’s day off. None of the usual cheer remained.

content.text: “I’m thinking of suspending the club.”
At Yuĕm’s first words, Shinan and Els objected at once. With Dahia gone and Estiĕ gone too, Yuĕm said, he could not keep things going in the atmosphere they had now.
“Five Heavens and the club are separate! I’m not turning Estiĕ-san away!”
Shinan slammed a hand down on the table. Els clapped her on the shoulder in approval.
“As for Dahia, and Estiĕ-san too, those were each decisions they made of their own will.”
Yuĕm’s explanation was calm. It was not as though the club had meant nothing to him.
“And what does the woman over there think?”
Els threw the question at Arka.
“If Estiĕ comes back, then the story changes, doesn’t it?”

Arka asked Yuĕm.
She was smiling—but not because of anything in Yuĕm’s answer.
The sheer drama of the situation was filling the hollowness inside her.
Only one thing was missing: the prince a proper story required.
She made up her mind.
This, then, was the moment to change puppets.
West Sósos. The Arsnorva residence.
“So you’re my sis’s friend! Nice to meet you! I’m Minac Arsnorva!”
content.html: “So you’re my sis’s friend! Nice to meet you! I’m Minac Arsnorva!”

content.text: Minac set tea before Arka and Shinan, then hurried from the room. Beyond the doorway came the sound of Minac chatting happily with her parents. They were clearly delighted that Shinan had brought a friend home.
“So what is it you wanted to ask me about?”
Arka took a sip of tea and began speaking about Dahia.
“Her father belonged to Mancal. That was what drove her mother into a new religion. Dahia told me she suffered terrible abuse from both parents.”
At Arka’s confession, Shinan fell silent.
“The root of everything is her father, Aryany Opfar, who abandoned his home. Or perhaps Mancal, which bewitched Aryany.”
Arka resorted to sophistry, working the strings of her puppet with exquisite care.
“What do you want me to do?”
A cloud passed over Shinan’s face.
“I want to save Dahia. I couldn’t. But you can. You’re the hero.”
Shinan took Arka’s hand and promised that she would make that wish come true.
Scarlet light poured in through the windows of the Arsnorva house.

Three days later, just as Arka had intended, the truce conference between Mancal and Five Heavens collapsed.
From the roof of a building, Arka looked down on Five Heavens and Mancal facing one another, on Shinan and Aryany standing opposed. She had even bribed corrupt police officers, to make sure Shinan would be granted a properly heroic final scene.
Everything had gone according to plan.
Right up until that moment.
“So. You really picked a fight with me the other day.”
Ágre Yall had come to her. In his hand he held a club stained with blood.
content.html: Ágre Yall had come to her. In his hand he held a club stained with blood.

content.text: “According to your logic, from what point onward did violence against civilians become acceptable?”
Arka was flustered now that the board had been disturbed. She had no weapon except provocation.
“Starting today.”

Ágre swung the club.
Arka ran.
She ran desperately. She had no wish whatsoever to get dragged into violence herself.
She tripped on a step and went sprawling. Ágre raised the club overhead.
“What kind of karma are you carrying?”
Estiĕ caught Ágre’s arm.
“Estiĕ—why are you here!?”
“That’s my line. Mikarla only sent me to observe the situation.”

Estiĕ’s knife aimed for Ágre.
Thanks to all the training he had done against a formidable opponent named Yuĕm, Ágre’s attacks now looked many times slower than they really were.
“No matter what you’re plotting, I’ll bear the sin with you!”
Shielding Arka, Estiĕ parried blow after blow. But Ágre’s strikes were heavy, and little by little Estiĕ’s strength began to give way.
Arka snatched up a stone at her feet and hurled it at Ágre. In that instant’s distraction, Estiĕ managed to pin him.
“There he is! It’s Ágre!”

Members of Five Heavens came rushing up.
Estiĕ seized Arka’s hand and ran.
Arka’s emptiness was full again.
The scenario had veered wildly off course, but as improvisation this was excellent.

The next day, Shinan was arrested as the ringleader of the clash between Five Heavens and Mancal.
Els took the loss of the girl who had always called him master hard.

Arka waited for Estiĕ on the roof of Building Five.
The story had already passed its climax; all that remained was the winding-down.
“Estiĕ-san isn’t coming.”
Yuĕm climbed the stairs. His eyes looked as if they had already understood everything.
“I heard it from Shinan’s younger sister, Minac. You had some kind of consultation with Shinan, apparently. What did you tell her?”
Yuĕm advanced on Arka.
“Nothing much. Career advice.”
One glance at Yuĕm’s face told Arka that lies would no longer suffice.
“I don’t know what your purpose was, but I’ve more or less confirmed that you urged Dahia to leave the club. It’s only a matter of time before people learn what you fed Shinan as well.”
Yuĕm’s voice was growing sharper.
“All I did,” Arka replied, “was prepare shining moments for everyone. And what is that in your hand? Don’t tell me you mean to put an end to everyone’s happiness out of your own ego.”
In Yuĕm’s hand was the notification suspending club activities.
“Yes. My ego. And my responsibility as president as well. Your puppet show ends here.”

Yuĕm turned and left the roof.
For the first time in her life, Arka had been overpowered by another person’s force of will.
She slammed her half-empty can down onto the rooftop floor.
In front of the office building, Els stepped into Yuĕm’s path.
“Where do you think you’re going, Yuĕm?”
A bokken was in Els’s hand.
“Dahia vanishing from the club, Shinan getting arrested—Arka-senpai set all of it up so she could manipulate the club.”
Yuĕm clenched the suspension notice tighter.
“Then why are you carrying that? If Dahia and Shinan are ever going to come back, we ought to leave them a place to return to!”
Els could still hear that earnest voice calling him master.
“I’m ending this so nobody gets hurt anymore.”
Els threw a second bokken to Yuĕm.
“Let’s do this somewhere else.”
University gymnasium.
“Come on, Solan bastard.”
Answering Els’s provocation, Yuĕm drew his bokken. The anger and grief he had kept suppressed came crashing out through the Stance of Savage Fury.

Els, for his part, met him with every weapon he had—cuts, punches, kicks.
Yuĕm’s strength had always rested on his calm.
Which meant the Yuĕm of now was far weaker than the one Els had once fought.
“You’re scared of that gloomy woman? Then the two of us should just outwit her together!”
Els drove a straight punch home. Yuĕm’s emotions surged higher.
“Then you carry it!”

Yuĕm sprang.
Els quietly angled his bokken so that Yuĕm would all but run himself onto it.
“That settles it.”

Els took the suspension notice from the collapsed Yuĕm and ripped it to pieces.
In its place, he left his bokken beside him and walked out of the gym.
The next day, Yuĕm left the club, and the West Altos Researcher’s Club slid back into being a ghost club once more.

September 1992. Yifeng Restaurant.
Dahia Opfar, back from her leave of absence, came to visit.
content.html: September 1992. Yifeng Restaurant. Dahia Opfar, back from her leave of absence, came to visit.

content.text: “Dahia. Wait. I give things.”

Mon set a cardboard box on the table. Yuĕm had entrusted the club’s things to her. A wooden fox from Skniune. A horseshoe from Durguatan. A brass instrument from Deltai. Traditional clothing from Eltēna. All of it had been handed down from Yuĕm’s generation.
There was one more thing inside the box.
A letter.
content.html: Mon set a cardboard box on the table. Yuĕm had entrusted the club’s things to her. A wooden fox from Skniune. A horseshoe from Durguatan. A brass instrument from Deltai. Traditional clothing from Eltēna. All of it had been handed down from Yuĕm’s generation. There was one more thing inside the box. A letter.

content.text: To the one who inherits this,
I’m Yuĕm Harc, the second president.
content.html: I’m Yuĕm Harc, the second president.

content.text: In trying to protect the club as a whole, I overlooked something important.
The selfish one, the emotional one, the devoted one...
I underestimated the miracle of people that different all doing the same thing together.
Please, cherish that miracle.
Please, take care of the younger members. Take care of the miracle.
Minac. Aliĕ. Leto. Fàta. Kura. Hal.
It was as though the miracles themselves were calling out.
August 1995. Hellén National University Hospital.
content.html: August 1995. Hellén National University Hospital.

content.text: The six members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club had come to visit Dahia.
“Why is fruit the standard get-well gift anyway?”
Minac set a basket of fruit on the table.
“Because it’s nutritious,” Leto explained, “and because it doesn’t require much energy to digest.”
“...Looks good.”
Hal reached out toward the fruit, only for Leto to stop her at once.
“By the way, did you bring ‘that’?”
When Aliĕ remembered, Fàta started rummaging through his bag.
“It’s a video of the scenery in Kàlavé. I filmed it when I went home.”
Fàta slipped a DVD into the player, and the footage began to roll.
“Can you play the bazaar scene in Arkéi? And turn the volume up a little.”

Kura worked the remote.
A street musician was singing a cover of the song Kura had once given him.
“I Look Forward to the Day We Meet Again”

Whenever the wind crossing the desert
stirs your name,

it is as though red higanbana bloom,
and a light is lit deep in my chest.

Even in days that shimmer like a mirage,
the warmth left in my palm
becomes a map I cannot lose,
and leads me toward a new world.
The others carried the landscape Dahia had wanted to see to her through her ears.
For a moment, just a little, Dahia’s lips moved.
“Being with everyone... feels like a dream.”
August 1995. Hellén Central Civic Hall, Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City.
content.html: August 1995. Hellén Central Civic Hall, Elben District, Hellén Direct-Controlled City.

content.text: The citizens’ debate of the civic assembly was in turmoil over whether to approve the Third Amendment Basic Research Act.
