“Everyone, the present situation—in which universities are being turned into little more than preparatory schools for employment—is weakening this country’s research foundation over the medium and long term. The purpose of the Third Amendment Basic Research Act is only one thing: to return universities to their proper role as academic institutions, and to protect society’s long-term interests. Not crude short-term employment figures, but the future technological strength of our country, and the potential of the young people who will bear our international competitiveness...”

Hal Perort, speaking for the affirmative, laid out her case with cool precision. Members of the educated class in the audience bent over their notes.
But the faces of the laboring majority remained hard.
content.html: Hal Perort, speaking for the affirmative, laid out her case with cool precision. Members of the educated class in the audience bent over their notes. But the faces of the laboring majority remained hard.

content.text: On the opposing side stood Full Somiolm, president of the employment agency Realia. He loosened his tie and mocked Hal’s “lecture.”
“That was certainly an impressive little explanation. But tell me, everyone—were universities ever really such lofty places?”
A murmur rippled through the hall. Full pressed on.

“Eighty percent of the young people who come to my company can’t even explain what they did at university. Research? Scholarship? That’s a pastime for a tiny privileged class. We workers need to eat today and live tomorrow. Isn’t it only natural that universities should support people in finding jobs? Isn’t that what it means to stand with ordinary folk?”

Applause rose from the laboring class.
Full had a grip on their real fears, their real sense of precarious life.
“The role of a university cannot be decided by short-term benefits alone. If research capacity declines, then in ten years all of your jobs will—”
Hal countered at once. But Full cut across her.
“Ten years? The people in this room care more about next month’s pay!”
Laughter spread through the hall.

The bell rang, ending the debate.
Hal understood then: this man had never intended to debate in the first place. All he cared about was moving the majority vote in the citizens’ ballot that would come at the end.
That evening, at Yifeng Restaurant, there were only two customers: Minac and Hal. The foreign-student members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club were all away in their hometowns for the break.
content.html: That evening, at Yifeng Restaurant, there were only two customers: Minac and Hal. The foreign-student members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club were all away in their hometowns for the break.

content.text: “Order? Fried rice?”
Mon was as impatient as ever.
“Twenty sesame balls, three bean buns, and tapioca milk tea.”
Hal ordered a flood of sweets. She must have taken the day’s defeat badly.
“If you’ve got time to hold chopsticks, you’ve got time to move that pen,” Minac muttered.
A deadline for a manuscript was bearing down on her. The table was piled with draft pages for an RPF dōjinshi of Shàun Akau aimed squarely at the self-insert fangirl crowd.
content.html: A deadline for a manuscript was bearing down on her. The table was piled with draft pages for an RPF dōjinshi of Shàun Akau aimed squarely at the self-insert fangirl crowd.

content.text: At the same moment, both their phones chimed with Tasvar notifications.
“Here. Stare at a picture of your ‘future husband’ and get your motivation back.”
Hal thrust a photo of Fàta in front of Minac’s face.
“Stop it! My whole market value depends on being a ‘plain nerd girl’!”
Minac dropped her pen and covered her face with both hands.
“Aliĕ and Leto-senpai are off in Eltēna on official business. Fàta’s in Kàlavé doing Duïlé training. And Kura’s posting photos of herself busking in Durguatan and getting chased by the authorities.”
content.html: “Aliĕ and Leto-senpai are off in Eltēna on official business. Fàta’s in Kàlavé doing Duïlé training. And Kura’s posting photos of herself busking in Durguatan and getting chased by the authorities.”

content.text:
Hal read through the Tasvar posts aloud.
Each of them was living a different life now.
“Putting jokes aside,” Hal said, “what do you want to become in the future, senpai? I’m going to be a lawyer.”
That question made Minac’s pen stop all over again.
“...I want to catch myself a wonderful husband and spend the rest of my life as a complete slob doing nothing but draw dōjinshi.”
Hal burst out laughing at Minac’s confession. The restaurant filled with the sound of just the two of them laughing.
“Hal. I’ve decided I’m going to apply to the graduate program at Hellén National University.”
content.html: “Hal. I’ve decided I’m going to apply to the graduate program at Hellén National University.”

content.text:
Mon dropped a stack of reference books onto the table.
Hal stared in amazement; when he translated from Solan into Ardia, Minac reacted a beat late and stared in amazement too.
“I got jealous of everybody else. At my university all anybody ever talks about is booze, cigarettes, and girls.”
Great Altos University produced large numbers of the laboring-class majority of Hellén—but it was also infamous as the laughingstock of the city, thanks to episodes of breathtaking stupidity such as:
“So you’re going to try for one of the recommendation slots.”
Hal pulled up the Hellén National University website on his phone. The gate was narrow, but still more realistic than trying to enter through the general examination.
“The problem is Ardia,” Minac said.
She crumpled one of her failed manuscript pages into a ball. Mon, meanwhile, looked smug. She said Satella—an expert in languages—had been teaching her.
“I’m just naturally smart, unlike you two certified idiots.”
The sudden venom in Mon’s Solan made Hal choke. Minac, not understanding a word, only blinked.

At the same time, in the entertainment district of Sósos, Shinan was making her usual rounds.
Ironically, the ones keeping this part of the city safe were moderate mafiosi like her.
“I’m sorry! I swear I’ll pay it next month!”
A rough-looking debt collector knocked down the owner of an izakaya. Loan collection was indeed one of Five Heavens’ ways of making money, but Shinan had never told her people to do it like this.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
Shinan’s joint-lock technique caught the collector clean.

“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! The places where this city comes to breathe are under my protection!”
content.html: “My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! The places where this city comes to breathe are under my protection!”

content.text: The collector fled. As the owner tried to flee too, Shinan caught him and forced the whole story out of him. He might have been a civilian, but he was drowning in debt; she was not going to go soft on him.
“I borrowed 1,000 Bónt, and they’re demanding 250 Bónt every month! And if I miss a payment, they say I have to hand over the business rights to the shop!”

The terms were blatantly extortionate. Shinan snatched the moneylender’s business card from him.
It belonged to a Realia subsidiary.
Lately Realia had been expanding through Five Heavens territory without permission.
“If you’re going to borrow,” Shinan said, “borrow from us.”
She left behind a card for a Five Heavens-affiliated lender and walked out. The slogan printed on it read: “When times are hard, we help each other! Gentle interest, total peace of mind.”
The next day, at Nort in Sósos District—
Satella dragged out some old textbooks from her university days and ordered Mon to answer Question One.
“A reactor that generates electricity while breeding more nuclear fuel.”
Mon answered perfectly—in Solan. Language was the only place she was truly hopeless.
“Now translate that into Ardia.”
Satella frantically tried to summon up memories from her student days. She had always been excellent at languages, but in engineering—her formal major—she had hovered just above failing.
“Burn more, grow more, make electricity.”
At Mon’s answer, Satella buried her face in her hands. There was still a year left before the exams, but she could not envision any future in which Mon passed.

The doorbell rang.
When Satella opened the door, there stood Officer Darc.
“First dorm mother, now teacher? Your rehabilitation is progressing nicely.”
Satella tried to thrust the week’s report into his hands and send him away, but he let himself inside anyway.
“No lazing around, young lady, all right?”
Darc dropped himself down beside Mon and spread out a newspaper.
“Your university was Great Altos, wasn’t it? Heard the news? Sounds like they’re planning to scrap the recommendation slots into Hellén National University.”

Mon and Satella both turned.
The town paper carried the headline:
“Great Altos University to Enter Major Partnership with Realia—Aiming for 100% Employment Rate”
At that same time, in the chairman’s office at Great Altos University—
Full tore the research materials laid out on the desk to shreds.
“I was born into a poor family in Sósos. Even so, there was happiness in that house. Then one day my father decided he wanted to become a researcher, and the family collapsed.”
Full pulled the chairman right out of his chair.
“You people of privilege are the ones in the wrong for showing people dreams. Steady, practical happiness—that is my ideal.”
The chairman clung to him.
“The recommendation slots are this city’s hope! Please—don’t take them away!”
Full sighed, then kicked him off.
“You collect referral fees, and we take money and data from students. We’re already accomplices.”
Then he turned and left the room.
“I’ll kill the Third Amendment Basic Research Act and bring this city a solid, practical happiness.”
The day after that, at Hellén Central Civic Hall, the rematch between Hal and Full arrived.
Buoyed by the success of the previous week, Full began in an easy, relaxed tone, one hand in his pocket.

“Everyone, I’ve no objection to universities putting effort into training researchers. But if that makes it harder for students to find work, then that is another matter. Our lives are not numbers. Universities should support tomorrow’s dinner table. That’s what I believe.”
His words carried the persuasive force of a lived, working person’s viewpoint. People in the hall nodded. From the laboring class came open cries of agreement.

Hal took up the microphone, then paused for a moment.
It was less to steady his breathing than to meet the audience’s eyes head-on.
“...As for President Full’s ‘tomorrow’s dinner table,’ I have no wish to deny its importance. But there is one thing I absolutely must say.”
He tapped his tablet and brought up a slide.

“This graph places the industrial growth rate of the Republic of Har-Lopinong beside changes in university research funding. As you can see, every time research funding was cut, employment worsened three years later. That is not coincidence.”

The look on the audience’s faces began to change.
The subject was difficult, but the graph let them see a tangible reason their daily lives were vulnerable.
“I am not saying research must be protected for its own sake. I am saying that if we want to protect employment, then we must maintain research. That is what the data shows.”

The hall fell silent.
Slowly, Hal turned his gaze toward the workers in the audience.
“Many of you here work every day, support families, and wear down your time and your bodies to keep living.”

The audience drew in breath.
Hal did not usually speak this way, from the perspective of lived labor.

“And the first thing that betrays that effort is politics that refuses to prepare for the future. I was raised in a wealthy household. I have never had to worry about what would be on the table. Precisely because of that, I have worked desperately to understand the anxiety that lies behind the numbers.”
No theatrics entered his voice. What came through instead was sincerity.

“Everyday life is upheld by technologies somebody researched in the past—medicine, transportation, communications. Your lives stand upon somebody else’s effort for the future. And you would have us cut away those future efforts ‘for the sake of jobs today’? In a country like that, can anyone truly say the jobs of ten years from now will be secure?”

Silence answered him—but not the cold silence of the previous week.
Here and there, people murmured agreement.
Full forced a laugh, but the ease of the week before was gone.
“Everyone! Don’t be fooled! Life isn’t theory! The suffering in front of your eyes is what’s real!”
Hal cut in immediately.
“And isn’t the suffering in front of our eyes the very result of refusing to prepare for the future? If your answer is ‘today is hard, so throw tomorrow away,’ then hardship will never end!”

The audience stirred. Someone began to clap, softly at first.
Hal pressed on, using to the fullest his strength for joining reason and feeling.

“I want to protect the future in order to protect your today. The Third Amendment Basic Research Act is not a law for people who enjoy lofty ideals. It is a law to protect the lives of working people ten years from now.”

Applause rose.
Some among the laboring class even leaned forward as though to say, Keep going.
Then the bell rang.
The Third Amendment Basic Research Act passed.

A week later, in the president’s office of Realia’s Sósos branch, trash lay scattered all over the floor, a measure of Full’s foul temper. Barking, “Move faster,” he struck the secretary who was stooping to clean it up.
“There’s a big piece of trash right here too.”

Shinan, entering the room, pointed straight at Full.
Realia had gotten a little too bold. Five Heavens had come to collect the bill for poaching in their territory.
“If you’re going to talk trash,” Full said, “look in the mirror, Shinan Arsnorva. All your talk about loyalty and human feeling—your pretty little ideals make me sick.”

He raised his fists in a boxing stance.
Shinan, deliberately, took his straight punch head-on—and thereby commenced her “legitimate self-defense.”

Full tried to break her guard with jabs.
Shinan slipped outside his line of sight and tangled up his leg. He swung desperate hammer blows, trying to shake her off, but Shinan did not yield an inch.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
Her joint-lock landed cleanly. Full thrashed and flailed, but at last he raised a white flag.
After that, Full withdrew from Sósos, and Realia’s partnership with Great Altos University was dissolved. The chairman announced that the recommendation slots would be restored.

A week later still, at the International Exhibition Hall in L'Kork Direct-Controlled City, Minac and Hal were running a booth at a dōjinshi fair under the name of the West Altos Researcher’s Club.
On the table lay books with titles such as “His Grace the First Instructor of Tané Anthology (Officially Approved by the Kàlavé Embassy)” and “A Forbidden Love with Lord Akau.”
content.html: A week later still, at the International Exhibition Hall in L'Kork Direct-Controlled City, Minac and Hal were running a booth at a dōjinshi fair under the name of the West Altos Researcher’s Club. On the table lay books with titles such as “His Grace the First Instructor of Tané Anthology (Officially Approved by the Kàlavé Embassy)” and “A Forbidden Love with Lord Akau.”

content.text: “Good day, little sister of Shinan. I wonder if I might have one copy of the Tané anthology?”
Koto Tail Aitēl of Blue Heaven had slipped in incognito to buy dōjinshi.
“Three Bónt! Don’t you want the new Lord Akau book too?”
At Minac’s question, Koto’s face clouded over.
“Do not ever utter that name in front of me again. My hand might slip.”
Taking the book in hand, Koto left the booth.
“Oh, my lord... I want to kill you for your own sake as soon as possible...”
Hal could only stare, speechless, at Koto’s madness.
“‘My lord,’ huh.”
Minac took out her phone and gazed at the photo of Fàta on its screen.

“‘And yet it moves.’ So said Tella FrĭeForlz, once condemned by the Inquisition. Sadly enough, no matter what genius may appear, an age will always shave him down to the scale of what its science can bear.”

August 29, 1996. The plenary chamber of the House of Representatives, L'Kork Direct-Controlled City.
Jen D'Ark, a young woman legislator of the Moderate party, had slipped neatly into the role of an outraged citizen.
content.html: August 29, 1996. The plenary chamber of the House of Representatives, L'Kork Direct-Controlled City. Jen D'Ark, a young woman legislator of the Moderate party, had slipped neatly into the role of an outraged citizen.

content.text: “In a few years’ time, opposition to immigration will be accepted as the obvious public mood. But by then it will be too late. The time has come to choose: will we run ahead of the age, or be leveled by it?”
When Jen finished her response, a tremor ran through the chamber. There it was. This was the leveling. And so another genius was thinned from the world.
“Desperado.”

She murmured it into her secretary’s ear.
The endgame was drawing near with every passing second.

On August 30, Loid Gorstpiĕss, a Deltai exchange student, was playing acoustic guitar in the student dormitory Nort. There was not a wasted note in her chords. She herself, by contrast, felt like nothing more than a piece set upon the board. Her home, Hologant, had suffered comparatively little from the war, and both of her parents were still alive. But in this city, the gaze turned upon immigrants with no “urgency” to them was a cold one. All she had wanted was to set out on a voyage.
content.html: On August 30, Loid Gorstpiĕss, a Deltai exchange student, was playing acoustic guitar in the student dormitory Nort. There was not a wasted note in her chords. She herself, by contrast, felt like nothing more than a piece set upon the board. Her home, Hologant, had suffered comparatively little from the war, and both of her parents were still alive. But in this city, the gaze turned upon immigrants with no “urgency” to them was a cold one. All she had wanted was to set out on a voyage.

content.text: “Keep the noise to proper hours, would you?”
Satella unlocked the door and stepped into the room. A guarantor had no patience for the melancholy of someone with too much idle time on her hands.
“They’re chords. Proof that I exist.”
Loid struck a C major.
“The people who understand it will understand it. Things like that matter.”
Satella handed her a paper bag. Inside was a chocolate cake.
“Leto’s making the rounds to thank people, apparently. Though even without this sort of thing, I’m the one who ought to be grateful.”

With that, Satella quickly left the room.
Loid licked a little cream from the cake. Sweet. To someone who had learned to look down on the world from above, it felt like a youth far too sweet.
At the same hour, on the campus of Hellén National University, in the gymnasium—
content.html: At the same hour, on the campus of Hellén National University">Hellén National University, in the gymnasium—

content.text: “This is the last round. If I win, then hear me out.”
Fàta drew his bokken. After two years of passing through one trial after another, he had already reached much the same state his grandfather Lài once had.
“Tell me, Lakán Fàta-kun. Once, White Owl Shàun Pàlé wiped out the elite of Kïnàiny with absurd ease. What do you think he did?”
content.html: “Tell me, Lakán Fàta-kun. Once, White Owl Shàun Pàlé wiped out the elite of Kïnàiny with absurd ease. What do you think he did?”

content.text: Minac smiled with room to spare as she raised her own bokken. At her waist hung the fox-colored knife known as Pàlajé’s Knife. Then she drew a photograph from her pocket. In it was a seal.
“A... seal?”

Fàta faltered.
In the next instant Minac’s knife came at him with murderous speed.
There was no point in making an enemy who was already tense even more tense. At such moments, what mattered was the opposite—to snap him awake.

Fàta stumbled, and Minac’s prepared attack came down on him. He dodged it, then, killing his presence, threw a second knife.
But Minac knocked even that aside by instinct alone.
Fàta widened the distance between them and settled into a running start.
“Iai, huh? Fine by me!”

Minac answered by taking her own stance.
In sheer ability, the gulf between them was heaven and earth. Even so, Fàta charged without flinching.

His knife was knocked spinning into the air.
Yet he drove straight into Minac’s guard.

The kiss came out of nowhere.
Tension, then release.
Minac’s face flushed scarlet.
“Please go out with me. That’s all I’m asking.”
Minac’s hand had already started toward the fox-colored knife at her waist, but she stopped herself.
“I surrender, my darling.”

That night, at Yifeng Restaurant, the final farewell party held by the members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club had overflowed the place well beyond capacity.
Part of the reason was that the five future members of the girls’ band-and-dance unit produced by Minac were all current first-years in the club.
content.html: That night, at Yifeng Restaurant, the final farewell party held by the members of the West Altos Researcher’s Club had overflowed the place well beyond capacity. Part of the reason was that the five future members of the girls’ band-and-dance unit produced by Minac were all current first-years in the club.

content.text: “Then let’s raise a fresh toast—to Senpai Minac and Senpai Fàta on their marriage!”
The toast was led by Rámar, from Duru. Everyone lifted their glasses except for Minac and Fàta, both of whom looked mortified.
“Hey, come on, you’re making the two of them miserable!”

That retort came from Chiel, from Skniune.
Nypro, from Nigracio, was quietly stacking dessert plates together with Hal.
“Aliĕ, you lot are the last of our moral conscience. If this thing lasts a year, I’ll count it as a triumph.”

Already dead drunk, Leto started in on Aliĕ.
No doubt he wanted, if only for a little while, to forget the “reality” waiting for them the day after tomorrow.
“That’s not fair... don’t look at me, make Meu do the hard work.”

Meu, from Solan, was going around pouring drinks.
Kura plugged a guitar into the restaurant’s audio system and began to sing a song of parting.
“To the Nigren Coast”

Farewell, my friend—raising clouds of dust,
The time has come to fold up this old tent.
To the unseen map, to the coast of Nigren,
Let the hooves of the soul ring on.
Until then.

The conversations that had all been scattered and separate a moment before were transformed by Kura’s voice into a single pulse of clapping.
All except for one person.
Loid alone sat apart.
“Chahan, kuuka?”
Even Mon’s offered hand of rescue could not reach her now.
August 31, 0:00.
Two women stood upon the rooftop of Skiĕl.

“In the eighteenth century, Har-Lopinong saw men of science preaching Enlightenment hunted as witches. Tonight, here in Sósos District, we shall carry out a ‘population reduction plan,’ offering up the unilluminated as sacrifice so that humanity may evolve. You are to serve as its Arbiter.”
One of the women was dressed in mourning black: Jen.

“‘Auditio.’ The masterpiece of all my experiments. Citizens whose strength and fighting instinct have been enhanced by drugs will be driven into a war of all against all. You may call it the breeding of kodoku, if you like.”
The other woman was dressed in white: Shiyuĕ.
“Most citizens who have not received the Mob Vaccine will simply weaken and die. There is no point expecting more than that.”
Jen sneered at the last remnants of hope in Shiyuĕ’s words. Shiyuĕ looked back at her with chill, exhausted eyes. In the end, Jen and Sait were alike. Both were creatures of pessimism.
“There’s a saying, you know. Fools and smoke both rise.”

The voice that cut across the rooftop belonged to Satella.
She had dug out the old uniform she had worn in her days as one of Guĕnar, and had come clad in it to seek atonement.
“From here on, I’m going to give the unilluminated—those who live meaningless lives—the meaning of death. Stay out of this, former witch.”

Jen drew her blade.
The Jet-black Knife.
The knife said to have belonged to Shàun a Lei, the fixer who had helped found the Instructional Nation of Kàlavé—a blade that pruned history.
“Dr. Shiyuĕ, this is the city’s fault. This city is the one that never faced your loneliness. This time, we in Hellén are the ones who’ll save you!”
Satella raised her own knife.
“White moves first.”

At Shiyuĕ’s signal, Satella shot forward at her highest opening speed and drove straight at Jen.
A cigarette butt flicked away by Shiyuĕ lit the first fire in the night of Sósos.
“I’m sick of this whole farce!”

Loid’s cry tore through the farewell party.
She was from Deltai. A direct party to the Deltai civil war. And here she was, seated at the same table with students from countries meddling in that war. It made her want to retch.
No—perhaps the reason hardly mattered. The truth was simply that she wanted to break.
content.html: Loid’s cry tore through the farewell party. She was from Deltai. A direct party to the Deltai civil war. And here she was, seated at the same table with students from countries meddling in that war. It made her want to retch. No—perhaps the reason hardly mattered. The truth was simply that she wanted to break.

content.text: “It’s all right. Loid still matters to us.”

Meu was the first to go to her.
The other first-years hurried after her.
“Look—Miss Twin Vacuum Cleaner.”
At the place Rámar pointed, Nypro and Hal were vacuuming up sesame balls at incredible speed.
When Chiel opened the window, she realized the direction of Viĕss District was unnaturally bright.
“There’s a fire at Saint Gal Cathedral, apparently. The ordinary citizens who came for the Night of Saint Arena must have been caught up in it too.”
Leto checked the news. Here and there one could already see wild rumors claiming that Deltai people were using the fire as cover for theft.
“I wonder if my sister’s awake...”

Minac tried to get in touch with Shinan.
Everyone in the room was thinking the same thing.

The restaurant door opened.
Officer Darc had arrived.
The moment he saw Loid’s condition, he took medicine from his bag.

“That stuff was something I swiped from a certain back-alley doctor once. The girl over there is infected with Auditio. It won’t spread from person to person, but the medicine is limited. Hospitals are in a panic from the number of infected.”
The officer sat down in a chair.
“I wonder if Dr. Shiyuĕ is all right...”
Aliĕ murmured.
“Even if that very doctor is the one chiefly responsible this time?”
The officer laughed scornfully and drained what remained in a bottle on the table. Fàta snatched the empty bottle away and glared at him.
“If you’re that worried, then go find out. That’s what the West Altos Researcher’s Club is for, isn’t it?”
content.html: “If you’re that worried, then go find out. That’s what the West Altos Researcher’s Club is for, isn’t it?”

content.text: Leaving behind documents related to Shiyuĕ and a map marking where he believed she was, the officer departed. In his eyes, just for a moment, there seemed to be a hint of expectation for what the young might yet accomplish.

Deltai Town, Sósos District.
The fighters mobilized by Guĕnar were moving through the streets, setting fires as they went.
“Arsnorva Punch!”
One of them fell victim to Shinan’s joint-lock technique.

“My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! The people driven from their homeland—I’m the one who’ll protect them!”
content.html: “My name is Shinan Arsnorva! A Farned of Five Heavens, one of Lady Scarlet Heaven’s own, and the ridiculously cool hero of this town! The people driven from their homeland—I’m the one who’ll protect them!”

content.text: At Shinan’s cry, the combined forces of Five Heavens and Mancal rose up.
“A temporary alliance! I’m not about to let the yakuza hog all the glory!”
Kura, Hal, and the first-years threw themselves into guiding civilians to safety.
At the same time, at Saint Gal Cathedral—
“Too bad for you. There’s no back-alley doctor here. My assignment is simply to make as much trouble as possible.”
Blocking the path of Fàta and Minac was Lanjiĕt, a Farned executive under Kybi Frauzet of Five Heavens.
“Whatever happens, we’re stopping this mess!”

Minac drew her bokken.
Lanjiĕt shattered it with humiliating ease.
“Come at me meaning to kill.”

Seeing Minac flung aside like a doll, Fàta flew into a rage. The posture of savage ferocity ran into the Pàlajé’s Knife Minac had entrusted to him, and with every strike he steadily narrowed his enemy’s options.
Lanjiĕt’s strength, meanwhile, was monstrous even to the eye. If he got hold of you once, that alone would likely end the fight.

One slash from Fàta opened the man’s leg.
All that remained was to bring down the finishing blow with the back of the blade against the neck.
By calculation, Fàta had already won.

Then Lanjiĕt threw a straight punch that cared nothing for his own wound.
Fàta dropped.
Lanjiĕt strode toward him.
But what Fàta held in his hand now was only a wooden sword.
In that instant Minac, who had concealed herself within the flames, struck the finishing blow with Pàlajé’s Knife.
“It has been a long time since battle felt enjoyable.”
Recognizing in the young man and woman before him the farthest reach of the true Stance of the Moderate, Lanjiĕt lost consciousness.
At the same time, on the rooftop of Skiĕl—
Though wounded, Jen still stood there wearing a look of complete composure.
“Thanks to the antibodies from Auditio. Even if I were burned at the stake, I’d go without feeling a thing.”

Though she held the upper hand, Satella was the one gasping for breath. It was not that she had grown rusty. She had never once neglected her training, because she meant to protect the children. The problem was that she still lacked a decisive blow.
“Satella-san!”
Aliĕ and Leto came running to her side. Then they looked toward Shiyuĕ and understood at once that Officer Darc had been telling the truth.
“I’ll say this now—trying to persuade me is pointless. This time, I’ve decided to play the heel.”

The moment their attention wavered at Shiyuĕ’s words, Jen lunged.
Satella could barely spare a hand to keep the children out of harm’s way, and the first strike landed.
No matter what happened, she had to protect Aliĕ and Leto behind her.

And suddenly she remembered.
A woman, four years before, in a foreign land, who had stood in front of danger to protect her spouse.
“To turn love into power... ridiculous.”

Satella sealed away her greatest weapon—her speed—and brought her knife down with all the strength in her body.
Jen might not be able to feel pain, but fatal wounds still existed.
“Listen up, fire and smoke. Listen good. You lot are somebody’s protagonist, somebody’s heel, somebody’s mob too!”

The back of Satella’s blade drove into Jen’s throat.
The fight was over.
“And you’re our irreplaceable friend as well.”

Satella stepped toward Shiyuĕ.
Aliĕ and Leto watched in silence.
Then—
“Hya-ha☆ Time for pickup!”

Gunfire split the air between Satella and Shiyuĕ.
Kybi Árin, the figure who would later be known as Green Heaven, dropped onto the rooftop.
content.html: Gunfire split the air between Satella and Shiyuĕ. Kybi Árin, the figure who would later be known as Green Heaven, dropped onto the rooftop.

content.text: “Dr. Shiyuĕ—don’t forget our promise, okay?♡”
The cute manner and feminine speech could not hide the threat beneath.
“Ah, of course. I still haven’t repaid you over her, either.”

No one there could stop the pair as they withdrew from the roof.
If they tried, nobody would have come away unhurt.
Even so, Shiyuĕ paused for the briefest instant.
“Satella, Aliĕ, Leto. Take care of yourselves.”
And with that, the doctor Shiyuĕ Lei descended the dark stairwell.
content.html: And with that, the doctor Shiyuĕ Lei descended the dark stairwell.

content.text: A few weeks later, in the plaza before Hellén Central Station, the people of the city had gathered to see Leto off.
content.html: A few weeks later, in the plaza before Hellén Central Station, the people of the city had gathered to see Leto off.

content.text: “Aliĕ, I’m leaving the rest to you. With your debate skill, you can manage on your own now.”
Leto encouraged Aliĕ. Beside them, Hal was still munching on hot snacks as usual.
“Fàta, give my regards to Minac. And if the two of you really do get married one day, invite me.”
Minac was now a proper working adult, employed by Har-Lopinong’s music label ArNoct.
“Kura, Mon, thanks for the souvenirs. I’ll treasure them.”
Leto’s suitcase was packed with Kura’s CDs and Solan sweets from Mon.
“Satella-san, Shinan-san, thank you for everything. Please keep taking care of Hellén.”
Satella and Shinan smiled with a touch of embarrassment.
“I’m truly sorry about the farewell party. Senpai are senpai. Country and war don’t matter—you’re important to us.”

Loid bowed her head.
The other first-years stood watching beside her.
“It’s all right. If anything, your outburst was what made us realize the danger we were in.”

Leto patted her shoulder.
When the time came, he disappeared through the ticket gate. After seeing him off, the rest of the group gradually dispersed as well.

Officer Darc watched the whole affair from a distance.
Jen had been arrested. But Shiyuĕ had vanished from Hellén together with Kybi Frauzet.
Then again, Shiyuĕ had outplayed Jen by a step from the beginning.
Auditio had never been lethal in the first place.
“‘Audition,’ huh.”
The officer stooped to pick up a discarded cigarette butt and dropped it into the trash.
“So you really did love this city.”
July 1999. Kindong City, Fitoliĕfiĕnio Province, Worl Federation.
content.html: July 1999. Kindong City, Fitoliĕfiĕnio Province, Worl Federation">Worl Federation.

content.text: Under a blistering sun, beneath a sky of blue and violet, people could be forgiven for assuming the heat had cooked their brains. Why else would they stand there admiring two ladies in swimsuits as they strolled across the beach? Those two, after all, were now charismatic villains with half the world in their grasp.
“Ugh, it’s hooot!”
Purple Heaven—Arsha Pritivitiĕ—hooked a hand inside the neckline of her swimsuit and tugged at the fabric with scandalous frankness.
content.html: Purple Heaven—Arsha Pritivitiĕ—hooked a hand inside the neckline of her swimsuit and tugged at the fabric with scandalous frankness.

content.text: “How slovenly, you counterfeit lady.”
Blue Heaven—Koto Tál Aitēl—snapped open a parasol with perfect elegance, shielding Arsha from the eyes of the “audience.”
content.html: Blue Heaven—Koto Tál Aitēl—snapped open a parasol with perfect elegance, shielding Arsha from the eyes of the “audience.”

content.text: After slipping Nine Earths’s pursuit in Hellén, the two of them had fled to Worl, Arsha’s home ground. For women whose days were usually consumed by work and bloodshed, this counted as a rare vacation.
content.html: After slipping Nine Earths’s pursuit in Hellén, the two of them had fled to Worl, Arsha’s home ground. For women whose days were usually consumed by work and bloodshed, this counted as a rare vacation.

content.text: “Suppose, for example, that a dress costs 100 Bónt,” Arsha said, launching into a lecture. “With single-entry bookkeeping, you would simply write: ‘Expense, 100 B.’ But with double-entry bookkeeping, you would record both ‘Assets increased, 100 B’ and ‘Cash decreased, 100 B.’ Double-entry bookkeeping looks at both cause and result.”
She was a merchant by trade. This sort of talk was her natural habitat.
“Well, I’m no wiser at all,” Koto said. “You cannot purchase things with a dress.”
Koto was a real lady; matters of liquidity had always been left to the butlers.
“So that is an objection about convertibility,” Arsha replied. “A fair point. But the value of a thing cannot be measured by ready cash alone. Double-entry bookkeeping looks farther ahead than that.”
The explanation entered Koto’s right ear and streamed politely out the left. Having finished her Tiĕliss Lokuĕmiss frappuccino, she moved on to her usual favorite subject.
“Never mind that. Look at this news article. My beloved husband is as beautiful as ever. If only he were dead, he would be more beautiful still.”
On her phone screen, an enormous photograph of Tané’s face glowed like a private shrine.

“I do quite understand the desire to preserve one’s beloved forever in words,” Arsha said, tactfully diluting the homicidal edge of Koto’s devotion. She had a favorite of her own: Tiana Mellén, the head of Bai Cong Hui, the mafia outfit currently fighting Five Heavens for turf in Worl. In the matter of love, the two women were equally strange creatures.
content.html: “I do quite understand the desire to preserve one’s beloved forever in words,” Arsha said, tactfully diluting the homicidal edge of Koto’s devotion. She had a favorite of her own: Tiana Mellén, the head of Bai Cong Hui, the mafia outfit currently fighting Five Heavens for turf in Worl. In the matter of love, the two women were equally strange creatures.

content.text: “Tell me, Count Aitēl,” Arsha said, “are you the sort who refuses to share your favorite? When I see shallow little women screaming over mine, I immediately want to kill them.”
“For me, killing is love,” Koto replied serenely. “I have no interest in killing people I do not love.”
They were alike in many respects, yet the vector of their madness pointed in opposite directions. Arsha, for all her defects, remained the nearer thing to a person of common sense.
“But really, I am cross,” Koto went on. “That woman was supposed to have resigned as Public Relations Officer, and now she is Prime Minister. Whatever is my husband thinking?”

She seized a knife and began stabbing the empty cup to ribbons. In 1994 Akau had assumed the office of Prime Minister, as part of the large-scale political reforms undertaken under First Instructor Tané with an eye toward expanding the Lurpe Pact.
content.html: She seized a knife and began stabbing the empty cup to ribbons. In 1994 Akau had assumed the office of Prime Minister, as part of the large-scale political reforms undertaken under First Instructor Tané with an eye toward expanding the Lurpe Pact.

content.text: “Easy, easy,” Arsha said at once.

If Koto exploded in the middle of Arsha’s own territory, it would be a nuisance of the highest order. Unfortunately, that was precisely when several men with no instinct for danger wandered up to their parasol.
“Hey there, ladies. Cute swimsuits. Want to have some fun with us?”
One of them reached for Koto’s shoulder.
“Tanuki,” Koto said coldly, “I am in a foul mood. This body belongs to my beloved husband alone. Your beastly hands have no right to touch it.”
She started to raise the knife, but Arsha caught her first. In Arsha’s hand was a thick wad of bills.
“One thousand Bónt in total,” she said. “It is yours. In exchange, make the books balance. With those turnip brains and those sorry little bodies of yours, what exactly are you capable of producing?”
She brandished the money at their throats the way Koto might have leveled a blade.
“No thanks...”
The men blanched and fled at once. Koto bowed her head, looking faintly apologetic. Arsha beamed.
“This,” she declared, “is double-entry bookkeeping!”

August 1999. Pinorl District, Rifrēz, capital of the Kingdom of Armona. The Pinorl Tower that rose over the middle of the city was now its proudest landmark, but when it had first been completed, artists had mocked it with the line that the only way to avoid seeing the Pinorl Tower was to climb it.
content.html: August 1999. Pinorl District, Rifrēz, capital of the Kingdom of Armona. The Pinorl Tower that rose over the middle of the city was now its proudest landmark, but when it had first been completed, artists had mocked it with the line that the only way to avoid seeing the Pinorl Tower was to climb it.

content.text: At the National Theater beneath that notorious monument, the Armona-born singer-songwriter Chisa Tál Kolon was in the middle of a live performance.
*Beautiful Choice*
“The bells of Sazány ring.

The faithful cry aloud:
‘Choose! Choose! Choose!’

Destiny binds you.
Two swords cross.
Comrades die.
‘In the future... in the future... in the future...’
Fate lays its curse on me.”

To a brisk beat descended from R&B, the Battle of the Bridge of Conversion was reborn upon the stage. In 1993, First Instructor Tané had revealed the long-concealed truth of the nation’s founding and put the fate of the Instructional Monarchy to the judgment of public opinion. More than eighty percent of the people had voted for the survival of the Instructional Nation. Since then, the Battle of the Bridge of Conversion had become one of those stories everyone knew, endlessly reworked in plays and songs.
content.html: To a brisk beat descended from R&B, the Battle of the Bridge of Conversion was reborn upon the stage. In 1993, First Instructor Tané had revealed the long-concealed truth of the nation’s founding and put the fate of the Instructional Monarchy to the judgment of public opinion. More than eighty percent of the people had voted for the survival of the Instructional Nation. Since then, the Battle of the Bridge of Conversion had become one of those stories everyone knew, endlessly reworked in plays and songs.

content.text: “Tiger! Fire! Cyber! Fiber! Diver! Viber! Jya-jya!”

The chant crashed across the hall, hilariously out of place in the gentle performance. Arsha, purple penlight in hand, roared her approval. Beside her, Koto had been forced to hold a blue one and looked bored enough to expire where she sat.
“I was lured here,” Koto said darkly, “under the pretext of ‘dropping by’ the official concept shop of the Kàlavé embassy.”

She was loaded down with bags full of Tané merchandise. Since 1994, Prime Minister Akau—praised as the greatest beauty among the Dolmalt peoples—had acquired fanatical admirers both at home and abroad. In the service of foreign-currency earnings, the Kàlavé government had opened a concept shop in Rifrēz to sell Akau goods. Tané goods were the afterthought. For Koto, whose true favorite was Tané, that arrangement was bitterly ironic.
“Sing, Count!” Arsha cried, grabbing Koto by the shoulder.
The relief of being temporarily free from work had driven her several degrees beyond her usual high spirits.
“She will absolutely regret this later,” Koto said. “She will say, ‘Count, forgive me. I am having flashbacks to things I once shouted in public, and my head hurts.’”
Koto had known Arsha a long time. Of all the executives, she was probably the one who best understood the violent swings of Arsha’s mood.

After the encore, Chisa ran off the stage. Since her debut in 1991 at the age of nineteen, she had written, composed, arranged, and programmed everything herself, single-handedly smashing the conditions for commercial success that had once been controlled by mass media and record labels.
“That fool in the VIP seats,” her manager muttered with a sigh. “Does she not understand the atmosphere of the song at all?”
The fool in question could only have been Arsha.
“It’s all right,” Chisa said. “Where I grew up in Worl, the audience would climb right onto the stage and everybody would make the music together.”
content.html: “It’s all right,” Chisa said. “Where I grew up in Worl, the audience would climb right onto the stage and everybody would make the music together.”

content.text: She was broad-minded. Though born in Armona, she had spent her formative years in Worl and returned later; that distance had spared her from being trapped by the conventions of Armona’s music industry.
“Well, never mind. That ‘fool’—Arsha Pritivitiĕ, CEO of Labaquĕda—is due in the dressing room after this.”
content.html: “Well, never mind. That ‘fool’—Arsha Pritivitiĕ, CEO of Labaquĕda—is due in the dressing room after this.”

content.text: The manager checked the schedule. Arsha’s public-side business meeting—the one about sponsorship—was coming up.
“I’m looking forward to it,” Chisa said. “It is Arsha, after all.”
She was already staring at a video-streaming site on her phone. Arsha was not only a business executive but a streamer with more than a million subscribers.
“Excuse us.”

After a knock, the door opened. Arsha entered with Koto in tow. Koto had been reduced to the role of porter and was carrying a box of Krart, Worl’s carbonated drink. The manager’s face tightened. Armona as a nation stood firmly against Krart and adored its own fizzy pride, Neskel.
“Arsha! And—is that Krart?”
Chisa leaned forward at once. To someone raised in Worl, it was the taste of a second homeland.
“It is a gift,” Arsha said. “But first, allow me to introduce myself. I am Arsha Pritivitiĕ. And the one beside me is Koto Tál Aitēl, president of the Aitēl Company.”
content.html: “It is a gift,” Arsha said. “But first, allow me to introduce myself. I am Arsha Pritivitiĕ. And the one beside me is Koto Tál Aitēl, president of the Aitēl Company.”

content.text: Arsha made the introductions. Koto bowed.
“Pleased to meet you,” Chisa said. “I’m Chisa Tál Kolon. I watch all your streams!”

Arsha and Chisa seized each other’s hands and, being each other’s hopeless otaku, failed to let go for an embarrassingly long time. When Koto at last lowered the baggage, Chisa handed her a chilled bottle of Neskel.
“It was the only cold thing I had,” Chisa said. “I hope that’s all right. You’re a Tál too, aren’t you?”
The middle name Tál denoted a noble title in Armona. In Koto’s case it reflected the historical period after the Armona–Deltai War, when Deltai had existed as an Armona protectorate.
content.html: The middle name Tál denoted a noble title in Armona. In Koto’s case it reflected the historical period after the Armona–Deltai War, when Deltai had existed as an Armona protectorate.

content.text: “Thank you,” Koto said after a sip. “It has a curious flavor. Rather like barley tea, for a count.”

Chisa laughed. In her own case, Tál indicated descent from a former noble house that had lost its title in Armona’s aristocratic reforms. Any Armonan would have known that. Koto plainly did not, which made the whole exchange much funnier.
“Well then,” Arsha said, smoothly switching masks, “shall we proceed to business?”
She opened the documents.

By bypassing both mass media and record companies and embracing internet distribution and direct marketing, Chisa had changed how Armona listened to music. She had made downloading songs part of ordinary life. The price of that freedom was that she had to earn with her own feet. Sponsorship from Labaquĕda was therefore no small matter.

“The way you express a message,” Arsha said, “has overturned the foundations not only of Armona’s popular music, but of popular music across the world. Our company wishes to invest in the power young people possess to change that world.”

There was no lie in the statement. Indeed, even as Purple Heaven, Arsha’s loyalty to Scarlet Heaven—Latia—rested partly on that same instinct: to wager on a young woman who had forced her way to prominence with startling speed.
“Thank you,” Chisa said sincerely. “I am fond of Labaquĕda’s products myself. It is an honor to have a company with such a broad view of the world help me make the world a little better.”

She meant that as well. She knew very well that she had not overturned the music industry alone. There had been people within the old system who rebelled against it, and fans who simply believed in her and pushed.
“However...”
Looking through the proposal, Chisa inserted the word like a blade. Arsha’s expression tightened.
“‘A newly written tie-in song.’ I cannot agree to that. I want to sing according to what I feel now. Writing a song for someone else’s convenience is not what I want.”
She said it without wavering. For her, that was a singer’s pride.
“Even if that is the condition of sponsorship?” Arsha asked.
Now the merchant spoke—cool, practical, and ruthless. The otaku, the executive, the Purple Heaven of Five Heavens: Arsha handled all three faces with skill. Tension rippled through the room.

“Yes,” Chisa said. “Even then. And besides, there are other candidates. Enimuĕc, for instance, is willing to make only one condition: supervision of my stage costumes. Costumes are also part of my expression, but if my songs remain mine, I can compromise there.”

It was a deliberate card to play. Enimuĕc was a front company of Bai Cong Hui. Chisa had casually dropped the name of a business rival. She knew of the rumor linking Labaquĕda and Five Heavens only as the sort of thing that turned up in tabloids. She had no idea she was saying it in front of one of Five Heavens’ top officers.
Koto’s hand slipped instantly toward her breast. If Chisa put a foot wrong, Koto’s knife would have flown. In the middle of that dangerous little silence, Arsha suddenly burst into laughter.
“Hah! Splendid. You have me there. Very well—then we will sponsor you without conditions. The costumes may be entirely your own.”
The room breathed again. Chisa had won the negotiation.
After the paperwork was finished and Arsha was on her way out, she paused and called back over her shoulder.
“Do invite me again. I am, after all, your otaku.”
Chisa waved enthusiastically. Once outside, Koto rounded on Arsha at once.
“Really. You nearly stopped my heart. That girl is unhinged.”
With the job done, Arsha rolled the stiffness out of her shoulders and answered lightly.
“A born Worl woman,” she said. “A gambler to the marrow.”
God had given people hands so that they might throw dice. The ghost of freedom was still at work.
September 1999. Alpashto City, Kulioss Urban Union.

“Now then, if you’ll follow the route, I’ll show you around. First we have the greige inspection line. Here we check the cloth for damage, stains, or color variation with the inspection machine. The standards are our own in-house specifications.”

Arsha and Koto were touring a sewing factory in the suburbs. It belonged to the multinational apparel company Enimuĕc. They had been invited by an old friend of Arsha’s who worked there. Naturally, both of them already knew that Enimuĕc served as a front company for Bai Cong Hui.
“And why, exactly, have I been dragged along?” Koto asked, stopping in her tracks.
Arsha had conscripted her as a porter for the tribute she intended to present this old friend.
“Count Aitēl, just look!” Arsha cried. “The scissors are enormous!”
She was bouncing around in a wholly undignified manner. The prospect of seeing her old friend had driven her spirits even higher than usual.
“So today I finally meet your favorite.”
Koto had heard the tale of this old friend—this favorite—ten thousand times already. Tiana Mellén, that was the name. Of course, neither of them knew that she was in fact the head of Bai Cong Hui.
content.html: Koto had heard the tale of this old friend—this favorite—ten thousand times already. Tiana Mellén, that was the name. Of course, neither of them knew that she was in fact the head of Bai Cong Hui.

content.text: September 1985. Riporc National University, Riporc Direct-Controlled City, Har-Lopinong Republic.
content.html: September 1985. Riporc National University, Riporc Direct-Controlled City, Har-Lopinong Republic.

content.text: “I cannot believe I came after all...”

Third-year university student Arsha Pritivitiĕ had attended a drinking party for the business club purely out of social obligation. She sat at the edge of the table with a half-finished beer before her. At the center of the circle of conversation sat a first-year student, Tiana Mellén. With her beauty and her natural charm, she was never short of admirers on campus.
content.html: Third-year university student Arsha Pritivitiĕ had attended a drinking party for the business club purely out of social obligation. She sat at the edge of the table with a half-finished beer before her. At the center of the circle of conversation sat a first-year student, Tiana Mellén. With her beauty and her natural charm, she was never short of admirers on campus.

content.text: Arsha lowered her eyes to her own glass and felt irritated by how stubbornly time refused to pass.
“May I sit here?”
Tiana settled into the seat beside her. Arsha moved her plate aside automatically to make room.
“What do you do when you have free time, Senpai?”
Arsha hesitated. Video-streaming sites did not yet properly exist, and talking about home-built PCs would have sounded absurdly niche.
“I spend time with cats,” she said at last. “There is one in my dorm.”
She opened her phone and showed Tiana a picture she had taken on a whim. Tiana glanced at it and smiled.
“That sounds lovely. I haven’t had much contact with animals since I came here.”
Tiana was from Kulioss. In Riporc, foreign students were by no means rare.
“In that case,” Arsha said, brightening under Tiana’s radiance, “there is a cat café in the Ritamak district. I think it would heal your soul.”

Her mood lifted further and further. Empty glasses began to gather on the table. In the end she even told Tiana about video-streaming sites and custom-built PCs—the whole embarrassing lot—and still Tiana’s impression of her did not seem to worsen.

As the party began winding down, the students started placing their own shares of the bill in the middle of the table, one by one. While Tiana was only just opening her wallet and looking at the money inside, Arsha paid for them both.
“My treat,” she said grandly. “As thanks for listening to me.”
Tiana looked apologetic. Arsha, striking the pose of a gentleman, departed the table in a flourish.
“Ohhh, exquisite!”
In the present, Arsha launched into that story of how they had met for the ten-thousand-and-first time. Koto listened with profound indifference and gazed instead at an industrial sewing machine.

Those countless retellings always shared two features. First, the pace of the story was entirely Tiana’s. Second, Arsha somehow always seemed to come out of it at a loss. None of this particularly concerned Koto, and she had long since accepted that this was simply how such things worked.
“And what sort of university life did the Count lead?” Arsha asked.
Koto’s gaze drifted to the tasteless bag plastered with photographs of Tané.
May 10, 1979. Gàtïna Grand Auditorium, direct parish of Āké, Instructional Nation of Kàlavé.
Akau was hurrying everywhere in preparation for a banquet celebrating the birthday of Koto, the second wife.
“Please arrange the lights so that they fall on His Eminence the First Instructor and the Second Wife together. And tread softly, so the ‘surprise’ is not given away!”
content.html: “Please arrange the lights so that they fall on His Eminence the First Instructor and the Second Wife together. And tread softly, so the ‘surprise’ is not given away!”

content.text: Why, Akau could not quite understand, was she working so hard for the sake of the woman who had stolen love from her?
“Akau, what should I do next?”

The architect of the whole affair—Tané—called out to her. The head of state, the religious leader of the Dírál school, and yet here he was laboring and sweating for the woman he loved. He was, Akau thought, absurdly besotted with his wife.

“Akau, I’m sorry to make you help like this. Since the previous First Instructor died, and since Mono took to her sickbed, I’ve felt it more and more strongly. You have to cherish what you still have. Koto, Karei, you as well.”
Tané looked a little lonely. Akau thumped him on the shoulder.
“If you have time for idle chatter, go fetch your princess already.”
Obedient to Akau’s instructions, Tané dashed out to collect Koto.
“Akau, can I set this box here?”
Karei placed a box on the table.
“Wait, not there—that’s the butto—”
Too late. The box hit the switch. Fanfare burst forth and the doors swung open automatically.
“My darling husband! Koto has arrived home!”
Koto marched into the hall before the preparations were complete.
“Ah.”
Akau felt the world go dark around the edges. But Koto only laughed.
“So the surprise failed magnificently! How funny, my husband!”
She flew into Tané’s arms. Akau laughed. Karei laughed. The retainers laughed. For Koto, it had been a perfect birthday.
“I truly am blessed. And yet had that woman not been there, it might have ended in even greater happiness!”
Koto made as if to draw her knife.
“I see,” Arsha said faintly.
That final needless remark had turned her slightly pale.
“That concludes the tour,” the factory manager said. “Any questions?”
The factory visit had passed in a blink.
“None,” Arsha replied. “It was delightful.”
As the two women finished up, the sharp click of heels approached. There was a pressure in it equal to that of Latia the Hollow God herself, and Koto grew wary without willing it.
“Thank you for waiting, Lady Pritivitiĕ, Lady Aitēl. I am Tiana Mellén, president of Enimuĕc.”

Koto stared. So this was Arsha’s favorite. She had the eerie impression of emptiness itself dressing up and walking about in human clothes. And from the stories Arsha had told her over the years, Koto suddenly formed a hypothesis.
Tiana was playing Arsha like a fiddle.
“Please call me ‘senpai’ like old times!” Arsha said, thrusting out a hand for a shake.
She would not let go. At length Tiana, perhaps seeing no end to it otherwise, turned and addressed Koto.
“This is our first meeting, Lady Aitēl, isn’t it?”
“Yes. Allow me to introduce myself properly. I am Koto Tál Aitēl, president of the Aitēl Company. Koto will do quite nicely.”
content.html: “Yes. Allow me to introduce myself properly. I am Koto Tál Aitēl, president of the Aitēl Company. Koto will do quite nicely.”

content.text: After a brief exchange of bows, the three of them got into a car waiting outside the factory. It took them into central Alpashto and stopped in front of the Calabar restaurant Hmï.
“Delicious, delicious!”
Arsha devoured Kïlèmēn—cheese-filled bread—with ecstatic concentration, entirely oblivious to Koto’s caution toward Tiana.
“Have you remained in touch with Lady Arsha since graduating?” Koto asked.
“Yes,” Tiana replied. “We have both become busy, but we still keep in contact now and then on Taswar.”
She gave away very little as a rule. The tension between the two of them was cut through by Arsha, who abruptly produced a luxury handbag.
“This is a Luvatén bag! You said your old one had broken, did you not? It is for you!”
She passed it across to Tiana, who accepted it with warm gratitude.
“Senpai is truly a special friend to me.”
That little declaration of affection reduced Arsha instantly to jelly. Koto, though still unable to prove her hypothesis, found Tiana more suspicious than ever.
After the three of them had finished the full course and moved on to tea, Koto proposed a game. It was only a bit of fun, she said. Tiana agreed with perfect composure.
“Please draw the floor plan of your house on this sheet of paper,” Koto said. “Do not overthink it. Just sketch it as it comes to you.”
She handed paper and pens to Arsha and Tiana.
A few minutes later, Koto collected the drawings.
“Now then,” she announced, “the results of the psychological game. They say a floor plan reveals this sort of thing about a person.”

- The largest room in the center: the place to which one most wishes to devote one’s resources in life.
- The treatment of the entrance and the living room: one’s distance from others, and with whom one wishes to be.
- The number of corridors and doors: the number of hurdles one sets before allowing others to see one’s core.
In Arsha’s case:

- The largest room in the center was a bedroom-cum-hobby room, with the specifications of a PC written across it in absurdly large letters.
- The entrance was of ordinary size, but the living room was small.
- There was only one door between the entrance and the bedroom-cum-hobby room.
“Somewhat introverted,” Koto said, “but nothing unusual.”
Then she turned to Tiana’s floor plan.
“So your largest room is a workroom.”
She spoke mildly, but pressed ahead. Where most people’s plans placed a space of rest, Tiana had drawn a room for labor. For her, repose seemed to be only a supporting character.
“Well, I have been working terribly hard lately,” Tiana said.
Koto did not let up.
“There is no living room. And the entrance, too, is small.”
The circulation was plain enough: receive guests only to the bare minimum, and send them home before they lingered.
“Ah,” Tiana said, “I must have forgotten to draw those.”
She tried to laugh it off.
“And finally,” Koto said, “the corridor is very long, and there are three doors before one reaches the workroom. It appears you dearly dislike letting anyone see what lies inside.”
Silence settled over the table.
“Perhaps I simply have no talent for drawing,” Tiana said.
She continued to blur the issue with the same even smile. Koto had seen enough. Tiana was a Machiavellian through and through.
“What a troublesome business!”

On the high-speed train home, Arsha sulked magnificently. Koto’s little game had soured the air at dinner, and Arsha was not forgiving about it. Miniatures of liquor and packets of snacks purchased at the station kiosk were already scattered over her seat.
“I am sorry,” Koto said. “I simply found that I wanted to know your favorite a little better.”
She was sincere. She really had been worried about Arsha.
“Even if I tried to stop you,” she asked, “your favorite would remain your favorite, would it not?”
Arsha answered by showing her the wallpaper on her phone. It was a two-shot photograph of Arsha and Tiana from their university days.
“Of course!”
She sounded utterly, gloriously certain.
